adam bede by george eliot book one chapter i the workshop with a single drop of ink for a mirror the egyptian sorcerer undertake to reveal to any chance comer far-reach vision of the past
this be what i undertake to do for you reader
with this drop of ink at the end of my pen i will show you the roomy workshop of mr
jonathan burge carpenter and builder in the village of hayslope as it appear on the eighteenth of june in the year of our lord 1799
the afternoon sun be warm on the five workman there busy upon door and window-frame and wainscoting
a scent of pine-wood from a tentlike pile of plank outside the open door mingle itself with the scent of the eld-bush which be spread their summer snow close to the open window opposite the slanting sunbeam shine through the transparent shaving that fly before the steady plane and light up the fine grain of the oak panelling which stand prop against the wall
on a heap of those soft shaving a rough grey shepherd dog have make himself a pleasant bed and be lie with his nose between his fore-paw occasionally wrinkle his brow to cast a glance at the tall of the five workman who be carve a shield in the centre of a wooden mantelpiece
it be to this workman that the strong barytone belong which be hear above the sound of plane and hammer singing-- awake my soul and with the sun thy daily stage of duty run shake off dull sloth
here some measurement be to be take which require more concentrated attention and the sonorous voice subside into a low whistle but it presently break out again with renew vigour-- let all thy converse be sincere thy conscience as the noonday clear
such a voice could only come from a broad chest and the broad chest belong to a large-boned muscular man nearly six foot high with a back so flat and a head so well poise that when he draw himself up to take a more distant survey of his work he have the air of a soldier stand at ease
the sleeve roll up above the elbow show an arm that be likely to win the prize for feat of strength yet the long supple hand with its broad finger-tip look ready for work of skill
in his tall stalwartness adam bede be a saxon and justify his name but the jet-black hair make the more noticeable by its contrast with the light paper cap and the keen glance of the dark eye that shine from under strongly mark prominent and mobile eyebrow indicate a mixture of celtic blood
the face be large and roughly hew and when in repose have no other beauty than such as belong to an expression of good-humour honest intelligence
it be clear at a glance that the next workman be adam s brother
he be nearly as tall he have the same type of feature the same hue of hair and complexion but the strength of the family likeness seem only to render more conspicuous the remarkable difference of expression both in form and face
seth s broad shoulder have a slight stoop his eye be grey his eyebrow have less prominence and more repose than his brother s and his glance instead of be keen be confide and benign
he have throw off his paper cap and you see that his hair be not thick and straight like adam s but thin and wavy allow you to discern the exact contour of a coronal arch that predominate very decidedly over the brow
the idle tramp always feel sure they could get a copper from seth they scarcely ever speak to adam
the concert of the tool and adam s voice be at last break by seth who lift the door at which he have be work intently place it against the wall and say there
ihave finish my door to-day anyhow the workman all look up jim salt a burly red-haired man know as sandy jim pause from his planing and adam say to seth with a sharp glance of surprise what
dost think thee st finish the door aye sure say seth with answering surprise whatbe awante to t a loud roar of laughter from the other three workman make seth look round confusedly
adam do not join in the laughter but there be a slight smile on his face as he say in a gentler tone than before why thee st forget the panel the laughter burst out afresh as seth clap his hand to his head and colour over brow and crown
hoorray shout a small lithe fellow call wiry ben run forward and seize the door
wewill hang up th door at fur end o th shop an write on t seth bede the methody his work here jim lend s hould o th red pot nonsense say adam
let it alone ben cranage
youwill mayhap be make such a slip yourself some day youwill laugh o th other side o your mouth then catch i at it adam
itwill be a good while afore my head s full o th methodie say ben
nay but itbe often full o drink and thatbe bad ben however have now get the red pot in his hand and be about to begin write his inscription make by way of preliminary an imaginary s in the air
let it alone will you adam call out lay down his tool stride up to ben and seize his right shoulder
let it alone or iwill shake the soul out o your body ben shake in adam s iron grasp but like a plucky small man as he be he donot mean to give in
with his left hand he snatch the brush from his powerless right and make a movement as if he would perform the feat of write with his left
in a moment adam turn he round seize his other shoulder and push he along pin he against the wall
but now seth speak
let be addy let be
ben will be joke
why hebe i the right to laugh at i--i canna help laugh at myself i shallnot loose he till he promise to let the door alone say adam
come ben lad say seth in a persuasive tone donot letus have a quarrel about it
you know adam will have his way
you maybe well try to turn a waggon in a narrow lane
say youwill leave the door alone and make an end on t i binna fright at adam say ben but i donna mind sayin as iwill let t alone at your askin seth come thatbe wise of you ben say adam laugh and relax his grasp
they all return to their work now but wiry ben have have the bad in the bodily contest be bent on retrieve that humiliation by a success in sarcasm
which be ye thinkin on seth he began-- the pretty parson s face or her sarmunt when ye forget the panel come and hear she ben say seth good-humouredly shebe go to preach on the green to-night happen ye d get something to think on yourself then instead o those wicked song yoube so fond on
ye might get religion and that ud be the good day s earning y ever make all i good time for that seth iwill think about that when ibe a-go to settle i life bachelor donot want such heavy earnin s
happen i shall do the coortin an the religion both together as ye do seth but ye wouldna ha i get convert an chop in atween ye an the pretty preacher an carry her aff no fear o that ben shebe neither for you nor for i to win i doubt
only you come and hear she and you willnot speak lightly on she again well ibe half a mind t ha a look at she to-night if there benot good company at th holly bush
whatwill she take for her text
happen ye can tell i seth if so be as i shouldna come up i time for t
will t be--what come ye out for to see
a prophetess
yea i say unto you and more than a prophetess--a uncommon pretty young woman come ben say adam rather sternly you let the word o the bible alone yoube go too far now what
be ye a-turnin roun adam
i think ye war dead again th woman preachin a while agoo nay ibe not turnin noway
i say nought about the woman preachin
i say you let the bible alone youhave get a jest-book han t you as yoube rare and proud on
keep your dirty finger to that why y be gettin as big a saint as seth
y be go to th preachin to-night i should think
ye ll do finely t lead the singin
but i don know what parson irwine ull say at his gran favright adam bede a-turnin methody never do you bother yourself about i ben
ibe not a-go to turn methodist any more nor you be--though itbe like enough youwill turn to something bad
mester irwine s get more sense nor to meddle wi people s do as they like in religion
thatbe between themselves and god as hebe say to i many a time aye aye but hebe none so fond o your dissenter for all that maybe ibe none so fond o josh tod s thick ale but i donot hinder you from make a fool o yourself wi t there be a laugh at this thrust of adam s but seth say very seriously
nay nay addy thee mustna say as anybody s religion s like thick ale
thee dostna believe but what the dissenter and the methodists have get the root o the matter as well as the church folk nay seth lad ibe not for laugh at no man s religion
let them follow their conscience thatbe all
only i think it ud be well if their conscience ud let them stay quiet i the church--therebe a deal to be learn there
and therebe such a thing as be oversperitial we must have something beside gospel i this world
look at the canal an th aqueduc s an th coal-pit engine and arkwright s mill there at cromford a man must learn summat beside gospel to make they thing i reckon
but t hear some o them preacher youwould think as a man must be do nothing all s life but shut s eye and look whatbe agoe on inside he
i know a man must have the love o god in his soul and the bible s god s word
but what do the bible say
why it say as god put his sperrit into the workman as build the tabernacle to make he do all the carve work and thing as want a nice hand
and this be my way o look at it therebe the sperrit o god in all thing and all time--weekday as well as sunday--and i the great work and invention and i the figuring and the mechanic
and god help we with our headpiece and our hand as well as with our soul and if a man do bit o job out o work hour--build a oven for s wife to save she from go to the bakehouse or scrat at his bit o garden and make two potato grow istead o one hebe do more good and hebe just as near to god as if he be run after some preacher and a-pray and a-groaning well do adam say sandy jim who have pause from his planing to shift his plank while adam be speak thatbe the good sarmunt ihave hear this long while
by th same token my wife s be a-plaguin on i to build she a oven this twelvemont therebe reason in what thee say st adam observe seth gravely
but thee know st thyself as itbe hear the preacher thee find st so much fault with have turn many an idle fellow into an industrious un
itbe the preacher as empty th alehouse and if a man get religion hewill do his work none the bad for that on y hewill lave the panel out o th door sometimes eh seth say wiry ben
ah ben youhave get a joke again i as will last you your life
but it isna religion as be i fault there it be seth bede as be allay a wool-gathering chap and religion hasna cure he the morebe the pity ne er hee i seth say wiry ben y be a down-right good-hearte chap panel or no panel an ye donna set up your bristle at every bit o fun like some o your kin as be mayhap cliverer seth lad say adam take no notice of the sarcasm against himself thee mustna take i unkind
i wasna drive at thee in what i say just now
some be get one way o look at thing and some be get another nay nay addy thee mean st i no unkindness say seth i know that well enough
thee t like thy dog gyp--thee bark st at i sometimes but thee allay lick st my hand after all hand work on in silence for some minute until the church clock begin to strike six
before the first stroke have die away sandy jim have loose his plane and be reach his jacket wiry ben have leave a screw half drive in and throw his screwdriver into his tool-basket mum taft who true to his name have keep silence throughout the previous conversation have fling down his hammer as he be in the act of lift it and seth too have straighten his back and be put out his hand towards his paper cap
adam alone have go on with his work as if nothing have happen
but observe the cessation of the tool he look up and say in a tone of indignation look there now
i cannot abide to see man throw away their tool i that way the minute the clock begin to strike as if they take no pleasure i their work and be afraid o do a stroke too much seth look a little conscious and begin to be slow in his preparation for go but mum taft break silence and say aye aye adam lad ye talk like a young un
when y be six-an forty like i istid o six-an twenty ye wonna be so flush o workin for nought nonsense say adam still wrathful whatbe age get to do with it i wonder
ye arena get stiff yet i reckon
i hate to see a man s arm drop down as if he be shoot before the clock s fairly strike just as if hewould never a bit o pride and delight in s work
the very grindstone ull go on turn a bit after you loose it bodderation adam exclaim wiry ben lave a chap aloon will ee
ye war afinde faut wi preacher a while agoo--y be fond enough o preachin yoursen
ye may like work well nor play but i like play well nor work thatwill commodate ye--it lave ye th more to do with this exit speech which he consider effective wiry ben shoulder his basket and leave the workshop quickly follow by mum taft and sandy jim
seth linger and look wistfully at adam as if he expect he to say something
shalt go home before thee go st to the preaching adam ask look up
nay ihave get my hat and thing at will maskery s
i shallnot be home before go for ten
iwill happen see dinah morris safe home if shebe willing
there s nobody come with she from poyser s thee know st then iwill tell mother not to look for thee say adam
thee artna go to poyser s thyself to-night say seth rather timidly as he turn to leave the workshop
nay ibe go to th school hitherto gyp have keep his comfortable bed only lift up his head and watch adam more closely as he notice the other workman depart
but no soon do adam put his ruler in his pocket and begin to twist his apron round his waist than gyp run forward and look up in his master s face with patient expectation
if gyp have have a tail he would doubtless have wag it but be destitute of that vehicle for his emotion he be like many other worthy personage destine to appear more phlegmatic than nature have make he
what
art ready for the basket eh gyp say adam with the same gentle modulation of voice as when he speak to seth
gyp jump and give a short bark as much as to say of course poor fellow he have not a great range of expression
the basket be the one which on workday hold adam s and seth s dinner and no official walk in procession could look more resolutely unconscious of all acquaintance than gyp with his basket trot at his master s heel
on leave the workshop adam lock the door take the key out and carry it to the house on the other side of the woodyard
it be a low house with smooth grey thatch and buff wall look pleasant and mellow in the evening light
the leaded window be bright and speckless and the door-stone be as clean as a white boulder at ebb tide
on the door-stone stand a clean old woman in a dark-strip linen gown a red kerchief and a linen cap talk to some speckled fowl which appear to have be draw towards she by an illusory expectation of cold potato or barley
the old woman s sight seem to be dim for she do not recognize adam till he say herebe the key dolly lay it down for i in the house will you aye sure but wunna ye come in adam
miss mary s i th house and mester burge ull be back anon hewould be glad t ha ye to supper wi m iwill be s warrand no dolly thank you ibe off home
good evening adam hasten with long stride gyp close to his heel out of the workyard and along the highroad lead away from the village and down to the valley
as he reach the foot of the slope an elderly horseman with his portmanteau strap behind he stop his horse when adam have pass he and turn round to have another long look at the stalwart workman in paper cap leather breech and dark-blue worst stocking
adam unconscious of the admiration he be exciting presently strike across the field and now break out into the tune which have all day long be run in his head let all thy converse be sincere thy conscience as the noonday clear for god s all-see eye survey thy secret thought thy work and way
chapter ii the preaching about a quarter to seven there be an unusual appearance of excitement in the village of hayslope and through the whole length of its little street from the donnithorne arms to the churchyard gate the inhabitant have evidently be draw out of their house by something more than the pleasure of lounge in the evening sunshine
the donnithorne arms stand at the entrance of the village and a small farmyard and stackyard which flank it indicate that there be a pretty take of land attach to the inn give the traveller a promise of good feed for himself and his horse which might well console he for the ignorance in which the weather-beat sign leave he as to the heraldic bearing of that ancient family the donnithorne
mr
casson the landlord have be for some time stand at the door with his hand in his pocket balance himself on his heel and toe and look towards a piece of unenclosed ground with a maple in the middle of it which he know to be the destination of certain grave-look man and woman whom he have observe pass at interval
mr
casson s person be by no mean of that common type which can be allow to pass without description
on a front view it appear to consist principally of two sphere bear about the same relation to each other as the earth and the moon that be to say the low sphere might be say at a rough guess to be thirteen time large than the upper which naturally perform the function of a mere satellite and tributary
but here the resemblance cease for mr
casson s head be not at all a melancholy-look satellite nor be it a spotty globe as milton have irreverently call the moon on the contrary no head and face could look more sleek and healthy and its expression--which be chiefly confine to a pair of round and ruddy cheek the slight knot and interruption form the nose and eye be scarcely worth mention--be one of jolly contentment only temper by that sense of personal dignity which usually make itself feel in his attitude and bearing
this sense of dignity could hardly be consider excessive in a man who have be butler to the family for fifteen year and who in his present high position be necessarily very much in contact with his inferior
how to reconcile his dignity with the satisfaction of his curiosity by walk towards the green be the problem that mr
casson have be revolve in his mind for the last five minute but when he have partly solve it by take his hand out of his pocket and thrust they into the armhole of his waistcoat by throw his head on one side and provide himself with an air of contemptuous indifference to whatever might fall under his notice his thought be divert by the approach of the horseman whom we lately see pausing to have another look at our friend adam and who now pull up at the door of the donnithorne arms
take off the bridle and give he a drink ostler say the traveller to the lad in a smock-frock who have come out of the yard at the sound of the horse s hoof
why whatbe up in your pretty village landlord he continue get down
there seem to be quite a stir itbe a methodis preaching sir itbe be gev hout as a young woman s a-go to preach on the green answer mr
casson in a treble and wheezy voice with a slightly mince accent
will you please to step in sir an tek somethink no i must be get on to rosseter
i only want a drink for my horse
and what do your parson say i wonder to a young woman preach just under his nose parson irwine sir donot live here he live at brox on over the hill there
the parsonage herebe a tumble-down place sir not fit for gentry to live in
he come here to preach of a sunday afternoon sir an put up his hoss here
itbe a grey cob sir an he set great store by t
hebe allay put up his hoss here sir iver since before i hed the donnithorne arms
ibe not this countryman you may tell by my tongue sir
theybe cur ous talker i this country sir the gentry s hard work to hunderstand them
i be bring hup among the gentry sir an get the turn o their tongue when i be a bye
why what do you think the folk here say for hevn t you the gentry you know say hevn t you well the people about here say hanna yey itbe what they call the dileck as be speak hereabout sir
thatbe what ihave hear squire donnithorne say many a time itbe the dileck say he aye aye say the strange smile
i know it very well
but youhave not get many methodists about here surely--in this agricultural spot
i should have think there would hardly be such a thing as a methodist to be find about here
yoube all farmer benot you
the methodists can seldom lay much hold on they why sir therebe a pretty lot o workman round about sir
therebe mester burge as own the timber-yard over there he undertek a good bit o build an repair
an therebe the stone-pit not far off
therebe plenty of emply i this countryside sir
an therebe a fine batch o methodisses at treddles on--thatbe the market town about three mile off--you ll maybe ha come through it sir
therebe pretty nigh a score of them on the green now as come from there
thatbe where our people get it from though therebe only two man of them in all hayslope thatbe will maskery the wheelwright and seth bede a young man as work at the carpenterin the preacher come from treddleston then do she nay sir she come out o stonyshire pretty nigh thirty mile off
but shebe a-visitin hereabout at mester poyser s at the hall farm--itbe they barn an big walnut-trees right away to the left sir
shebe own niece to poyser s wife an theywill be fine an vex at she for make a fool of herself i that way
but ihave hear as therebe no holding these methodisses when the maggit s once get i their head many of them go stark starin mad wi their religion
though this young woman s quiet enough to look at by what i can make out ihave not see she myself well i wish i have time to wait and see she but i must get on
ihave be out of my way for the last twenty minute to have a look at that place in the valley
itbe squire donnithorne s i suppose yes sir thatbe donnithorne chase that be
fine hoak there benot there sir
i should know what it be sir for ihave live butler there a-go i fifteen year
itbe captain donnithorne as be th heir sir--squire donnithorne s grandson
hewill be comin of hage this ay- arvest sir an we shall hev fine doin s
he own all the land about here sir squire donnithorne do well itbe a pretty spot whoever may own it say the traveller mount his horse and one meet some fine strap fellow about too
i meet as fine a young fellow as ever i see in my life about half an hour ago before i come up the hill--a carpenter a tall broad-shoulder fellow with black hair and black eye march along like a soldier
we want such fellow as he to lick the french aye sir thatbe adam bede that is iwill be bind--thias bede s son everybody know he hereabout
hebe an uncommon clever stiddy fellow an wonderful strong
lord bless you sir--if youwill hexcuse i for say so--he can walk forty mile a-day an lift a matter o sixty ston
hebe an uncommon favourite wi the gentry sir captain donnithorne and parson irwine mek a fine fuss wi he
but hebe a little lift up an peppery-like well good evening to you landlord i must get on your servant sir good evenin the traveller put his horse into a quick walk up the village but when he approach the green the beauty of the view that lie on his right hand the singular contrast present by the group of villager with the knot of methodists near the maple and perhaps yet more curiosity to see the young female preacher prove too much for his anxiety to get to the end of his journey and he pause
the green lie at the extremity of the village and from it the road branch off in two direction one lead far up the hill by the church and the other wind gently down towards the valley
on the side of the green that lead towards the church the broken line of thatch cottage be continue nearly to the churchyard gate but on the opposite northwestern side there be nothing to obstruct the view of gently swell meadow and woode valley and dark mass of distant hill
that rich undulating district of loamshire to which hayslope belong lie close to a grim outskirt of stonyshire overlook by its barren hill as a pretty bloom sister may sometimes be see link in the arm of a rugged tall swarthy brother and in two or three hour ride the traveller might exchange a bleak treeless region intersect by line of cold grey stone for one where his road wind under the shelter of wood or up swell hill muffle with hedgerow and long meadow-grass and thick corn and where at every turn he come upon some fine old country-seat nestle in the valley or crown the slope some homestead with its long length of barn and its cluster of golden rick some grey steeple look out from a pretty confusion of tree and thatch and dark-red tile
it be just such a picture as this last that hayslope church have make to the traveller as he begin to mount the gentle slope lead to its pleasant upland and now from his station near the green he have before he in one view nearly all the other typical feature of this pleasant land
high up against the horizon be the huge conical mass of hill like giant mound intend to fortify this region of corn and grass against the keen and hungry wind of the north not distant enough to be clothe in purple mystery but with sombre greenish side visibly speck with sheep whose motion be only reveal by memory not detect by sight woo from day to day by the change hour but respond with no change in themselves--leave for ever grim and sullen after the flush of morning the wing gleam of the april noonday the parting crimson glory of the ripen summer sun
and directly below they the eye rest on a more advanced line of hang wood divide by bright patch of pasture or furrow crop and not yet deepen into the uniform leafy curtain of high summer but still show the warm tint of the young oak and the tender green of the ash and lime
then come the valley where the wood grow thick as if they have roll down and hurry together from the patch leave smooth on the slope that they might take the well care of the tall mansion which lift its parapet and send its faint blue summer smoke among they
doubtless there be a large sweep of park and a broad glassy pool in front of that mansion but the swell slope of meadow would not let our traveller see they from the village green
he see instead a foreground which be just as lovely--the level sunlight lie like transparent gold among the gently curve stem of the feather grass and the tall red sorrel and the white ambel of the hemlock line the bushy hedgerow
it be that moment in summer when the sound of the scythe be whet make we cast more lingering look at the flower-sprinkle tress of the meadow
he might have see other beauty in the landscape if he have turn a little in his saddle and look eastward beyond jonathan burge s pasture and woodyard towards the green corn-field and walnut-tree of the hall farm but apparently there be more interest for he in the live group close at hand
every generation in the village be there from old feyther taft in his brown worst night-cap who be bent nearly double but seem tough enough to keep on his leg a long while lean on his short stick down to the baby with their little round head loll forward in quilt linen cap
now and then there be a new arrival perhaps a slouch labourer who have eat his supper come out to look at the unusual scene with a slow bovine gaze willing to hear what any one have to say in explanation of it but by no mean excited enough to ask a question
but all take care not to join the methodists on the green and identify themselves in that way with the expectant audience for there be not one of they that would not have disclaim the imputation of have come out to hear the preacher woman they have only come out to see what war a-go on like the man be chiefly gather in the neighbourhood of the blacksmith s shop
but do not imagine they gather in a knot
villager never swarm a whisper be unknown among they and they seem almost as incapable of an undertone as a cow or a stag
your true rustic turn his back on his interlocutor throw a question over his shoulder as if he mean to run away from the answer and walk a step or two far off when the interest of the dialogue culminate
so the group in the vicinity of the blacksmith s door be by no mean a close one and form no screen in front of chad cranage the blacksmith himself who stand with his black brawny arm fold lean against the door-post and occasionally send forth a bellowing laugh at his own joke give they a mark preference over the sarcasm of wiry ben who have renounce the pleasure of the holly bush for the sake of see life under a new form
but both style of wit be treat with equal contempt by mr
joshua rann
mr
rann s leathern apron and subdue griminess can leave no one in any doubt that he be the village shoemaker the thrust out of his chin and stomach and the twirling of his thumb be more subtle indication intend to prepare unwary stranger for the discovery that they be in the presence of the parish clerk
old joshway as he be irreverently call by his neighbour be in a state of simmer indignation but he have not yet open his lip except to say in a resound bass undertone like the tuning of a violoncello sehon king of the amorites for his mercy endureth for ever and og the king of basan for his mercy endureth for ever a quotation which may seem to have slight bearing on the present occasion but as with every other anomaly adequate knowledge will show it to be a natural sequence
mr
rann be inwardly maintain the dignity of the church in the face of this scandalous irruption of methodism and as that dignity be bind up with his own sonorous utterance of the response his argument naturally suggest a quotation from the psalm he have read the last sunday afternoon
the strong curiosity of the woman have draw they quite to the edge of the green where they could examine more closely the quakerlike costume and odd deportment of the female methodists
underneath the maple there be a small cart which have be bring from the wheelwright s to serve as a pulpit and round this a couple of bench and a few chair have be place
some of the methodists be rest on these with their eye close as if wrapt in prayer or meditation
other choose to continue stand and have turn their face towards the villager with a look of melancholy compassion which be highly amusing to bessy cranage the blacksmith s buxom daughter know to her neighbour as chad s bess who wonder why the folk war amakin face a that ns chad s bess be the object of peculiar compassion because her hair be turn back under a cap which be set at the top of her head expose to view an ornament of which she be much prouder than of her red cheek--namely a pair of large round ear-ring with false garnet in they ornament condemn not only by the methodists but by her own cousin and namesake timothy s bess who with much cousinly feeling often wish they ear-ring might come to good
timothy s bess though retain her maiden appellation among her familiar have long be the wife of sandy jim and possess a handsome set of matronly jewel of which it be enough to mention the heavy baby she be rock in her arm and the sturdy fellow of five in knee-breech and red leg who have a rusty milk-can round his neck by way of drum and be very carefully avoid by chad s small terrier
this young olive-branch notorious under the name of timothy s bess s ben be of an inquire disposition unchecke by any false modesty have advance beyond the group of woman and child and be walk round the methodists look up in their face with his mouth wide open and beat his stick against the milk-can by way of musical accompaniment
but one of the elderly woman bend down to take he by the shoulder with an air of grave remonstrance timothy s bess s ben first kick out vigorously then take to his heel and seek refuge behind his father s leg
ye gallow young dog say sandy jim with some paternal pride if ye donna keep that stick quiet iwill tek it from ye
what dy e mane by kickin foulk here
gie he here to i jim say chad cranage iwill tie hir up an shoe he as i do th hosse
well mester casson he continue as that personage saunter up towards the group of man how be ye t naight
be ye coom t help groon
they say folk allay groon when theybe hearkenin to th methodys as if they war bad i th inside
i mane to groon as loud as your cow do th other naight an then the praicher ull think ibe i th raight way iwould advise you not to be up to no nonsense chad say mr
casson with some dignity poyser wouldnot like to hear as his wife s niece be treat any way disrespectful for all he maynot be fond of her taking on herself to preach aye an shebe a pleasant-look un too say wiry ben
iwill stick up for the pretty woman preachin i know theywould persuade i over a deal soon nor th ugly man
i shouldna wonder if i turn methody afore the night s out an begin to coort the preacher like seth bede why seth s look rether too high i should think say mr
casson
this woman s kin wouldnot like she to demean herself to a common carpenter tchu say ben with a long treble intonation whatbe folk s kin get to do wi t
not a chip
poyser s wife may turn her nose up an forget bygone but this dinah morris they tell i be as poor as iver she be--work at a mill an s much ado to keep hersen

the stranger be strike with surprise as he see her approach and mount the cart--surprise not so much at the feminine delicacy of her appearance as at the total absence of self-consciousness in her demeanour
he have make up his mind to see her advance with a measured step and a demure solemnity of countenance he have feel sure that she face would be mantle with the smile of conscious saintship or else charge with denunciatory bitterness
he know but two type of methodist--the ecstatic and the bilious
but dinah walk as simply as if she be go to market and seem as unconscious of her outward appearance as a little boy there be no blush no tremulousness which say i know you think i a pretty woman too young to preach no cast up or down of the eyelid no compression of the lip no attitude of the arm that say but you must think of i as a saint she hold no book in her ungloved hand but let they hang down lightly cross before she as she stand and turn her grey eye on the people
there be no keenness in the eye they seem rather to be shed love than make observation they have the liquid look which tell that the mind be full of what it have to give out rather than impress by external object
she stand with her left hand towards the descending sun and leafy bough screen she from its ray but in this sober light the delicate colouring of her face seem to gather a calm vividness like flower at evening
it be a small oval face of a uniform transparent whiteness with an egg-like line of cheek and chin a full but firm mouth a delicate nostril and a low perpendicular brow surmount by a rise arch of parting between smooth lock of pale reddish hair
the hair be draw straight back behind the ear and cover except for an inch or two above the brow by a net quaker cap
the eyebrow of the same colour as the hair be perfectly horizontal and firmly pencil the eyelash though no darker be long and abundant--nothing be leave blur or unfinishe
it be one of those face that make one think of white flower with light touch of colour on their pure petal
the eye have no peculiar beauty beyond that of expression they look so simple so candid so gravely loving that no accuse scowl no light sneer could help melt away before their glance

dear friend she say in a clear but not loud voice let we pray for a blessing she close her eye and hang her head down a little continue in the same moderate tone as if speak to some one quite near she saviour of sinner

why you and i dear friend be poor

we be just the sort of people that want to hear good news
for when anybodybe well off they donot much mind about hear news from distant part but if a poor man or woman s in trouble and have hard work to make out a living they like to have a letter to tell them theyhave get a friend as will help them

but be that as much as we want to know about god
we see he be great and mighty and can do what he will we be lose as if we be struggle in great water when we try to think of he
but perhaps doubt come into your mind like this can god take much notice of we poor people
perhaps he only make the world for the great and the wise and the rich

for our life be full of trouble and if god send we good he seem to send bad too

another time he say i come to seek and to save that which be lose and another time i come not to call the righteous but sinner to repentance the lost sinners ah dear friend do that mean you and i hitherto the traveller have be chain to the spot against his will by the charm of dinah s mellow treble tone which have a variety of modulation like that of a fine instrument touch with the unconscious skill of musical instinct

he see that she have thoroughly arrest her hearer

the stranger have cease to doubt as he have do at the first glance that she could fix the attention of her rougher hearer but still he wonder whether she could have that power of rouse their more violent emotion which must surely be a necessary seal of her vocation as a methodist preacher until she come to the word lost sinner when there be a great change in her voice and manner

her pale face become paler the circle under her eye deepen as they do when tear half-gather without fall and the mild love eye take an expression of appalled pity as if she have suddenly discern a destroy angel hover over the head of the people
her voice become deep and muffle but there be still no gesture
nothing could be less like the ordinary type of the ranter than dinah
she be not preach as she hear other preach but speak directly from her own emotion and under the inspiration of her own simple faith
but now she have enter into a new current of feeling
her manner become less calm her utterance more rapid and agitated as she try to bring home to the people their guilt their wilful darkness their state of disobedience to god--as she dwelt on the hatefulness of sin the divine holiness and the suffering of the saviour by which a way have be open for their salvation
at last it seem as if in her yearning desire to reclaim the lost sheep she could not be satisfy by address her hearer as a body
she appeal first to one and then to another beseech they with tear to turn to god while there be yet time paint to they the desolation of their soul lose in sin feed on the husk of this miserable world far away from god their father and then the love of the saviour who be wait and watch for their return
there be many a responsive sigh and groan from her fellow-methodist but the village mind do not easily take fire and a little smoulder vague anxiety that might easily die out again be the utmost effect dinah s preaching have work in they at present
yet no one have retire except the child and old feyther taft who be too deaf to catch many word have some time ago go back to his inglenook

but now she begin to feel very much as if the constable have come to take she up and carry she before the justice for some undefined offence
she have a terrified sense that god whom she have always think of as very far off be very near to she and that jesus be close by look at she though she could not see he
for dinah have that belief in visible manifestation of jesus which be common among the methodists and she communicate it irresistibly to her hearer she make they feel that he be among they bodily and might at any moment show himself to they in some way that would strike anguish and penitence into their heart

dinah have be speak at least an hour and the redden light of the parting day seem to give a solemn emphasis to her closing word
the stranger who have be interested in the course of her sermon as if it have be the development of a drama--for there be this sort of fascination in all sincere unpremeditated eloquence which open to one the inward drama of the speaker s emotion--now turn his horse aside and pursue his way while dinah say let we sing a little dear friend and as he be still wind down the slope the voice of the methodists reach he rise and fall in that strange blending of exultation and sadness which belong to the cadence of a hymn
chapter iii after the preaching in less than an hour from that time seth bede be walk by dinah s side along the hedgerow-path that skirt the pasture and green corn-field which lie between the village and the hall farm
dinah have take off her little quaker bonnet again and be hold it in her hand that she might have a free enjoyment of the cool evening twilight and seth could see the expression of her face quite clearly as he walk by her side timidly revolve something he want to say to she
it be an expression of unconscious placid gravity--of absorption in thought that have no connection with the present moment or with her own personality--an expression that be most of all discourage to a lover
her very walk be discourage it have that quiet elasticity that ask for no support
seth feel this dimly he say to himself shebe too good and holy for any man let alone i and the word he have be summon rush back again before they have reach his lip

iwould make a shift and fend indoor and out to give you more liberty--more than you can have now for youhave get to get your own living now and ibe strong enough to work for we both when seth have once begin to urge his suit he go on earnestly and almost hurriedly lest dinah should speak some decisive word before he have pour forth all the argument he have prepare

they have reach one of those very narrow pass between two tall stone which perform the office of a stile in loamshire and dinah pause as she turn towards seth and say in her tender but calm treble note seth bede i thank you for your love towards i and if i could think of any man as more than a christian brother i think it would be you
but my heart be not free to marry
that be good for other woman and it be a great and a bless thing to be a wife and mother but as god have distribute to every man as the lord hath call every man so let he walk god have call i to minister to other not to have any joy or sorrow of my own but to rejoice with they that do rejoice and to weep with those that weep
he have call i to speak his word and he have greatly own my work
it could only be on a very clear show that i could leave the brother and sister at snowfield who be favour with very little of this world s good where the tree be few so that a child might count they and therebe very hard live for the poor in the winter
it have be give i to help to comfort and strengthen the little flock there and to call in many wanderer and my soul be fill with these thing from my rise up till my lie down
my life be too short and god s work be too great for i to think of make a home for myself in this world
ihave not turn a deaf ear to your word seth for when i see as your love be give to i i think it might be a leading of providence for i to change my way of life and that we should be fellow-helper and i spread the matter before the lord
but whenever i try to fix my mind on marriage and our live together other thought always come in--the time when ihave pray by the sick and dying and the happy hour ihave have preach when my heart be fill with love and the word be give to i abundantly
and when ihave open the bible for direction ihave always light on some clear word to tell i where my work lay
i believe what you say seth that you would try to be a help and not a hindrance to my work but i see that our marriage be not god s will--he draw my heart another way
i desire to live and die without husband or child
i seem to have no room in my soul for want and fear of my own it have please god to fill my heart so full with the want and suffering of his poor people seth be unable to reply and they walk on in silence
at last as they be nearly at the yard-gate he say well dinah i must seek for strength to bear it and to endure as see he who be invisible
but i feel now how weak my faith be
it seem as if when you be go i could never joy in anything any more
i think itbe something pass the love of woman as i feel for you for i could be content without your marry i if i could go and live at snowfield and be near you
i trust as the strong love god have give i towards you be a leading for we both but it seem it be only mean for my trial
perhaps i feel more for you than i ought to feel for any creature for i often cannot help say of you what the hymn says-- in darkest shade if she appear my dawning be begin she be my soul s bright morning-star and she my rise sun
that may be wrong and i be to be teach well
but you wouldnot be displeased with i if thing turn out so as i could leave this country and go to live at snowfield no seth but i counsel you to wait patiently and not lightly to leave your own country and kindre
do nothing without the lord s clear bidding
itbe a bleak and barren country there not like this land of goshen youhave be use to

youwill be continually in my prayer they have now reach the yard-gate and seth say i willnot go in dinah so farewell he pause and hesitate after she have give he her hand and then say therebe no knowing but what you may see thing different after a while
there may be a new leading let we leave that seth
itbe good to live only a moment at a time as ihave read in one of mr
wesley s book
it benot for you and i to lay plan wehave nothing to do but to obey and to trust
farewell dinah press his hand with rather a sad look in her love eye and then pass through the gate while seth turn away to walk lingeringly home
but instead of take the direct road he choose to turn back along the field through which he and dinah have already pass and i think his blue linen handkerchief be very wet with tear long before he have make up his mind that it be time for he to set his face steadily homeward
he be but three-and-twenty and have only just learn what it be to love--to love with that adoration which a young man give to a woman whom he feel to be great and well than himself
love of this sort be hardly distinguishable from religious feeling
what deep and worthy love be so whether of woman or child or art or music
our caress our tender word our still rapture under the influence of autumn sunset or pillare vista or calm majestic statue or beethoven symphony all bring with they the consciousness that they be mere wave and ripple in an unfathomable ocean of love and beauty our emotion in its keen moment pass from expression into silence our love at its high flood rush beyond its object and lose itself in the sense of divine mystery
and this blessed gift of venerate love have be give to too many humble craftsman since the world begin for we to feel any surprise that it should have exist in the soul of a methodist carpenter half a century ago while there be yet a lingering after-glow from the time when wesley and his fellow-labourer feed on the hip and haw of the cornwall hedge after exhaust limb and lung in carry a divine message to the poor
that afterglow have long fade away and the picture we be apt to make of methodism in our imagination be not an amphitheatre of green hill or the deep shade of broad-leave sycamore where a crowd of rough man and weary-hearted woman drink in a faith which be a rudimentary culture which link their thought with the past lift their imagination above the sordid detail of their own narrow life and suffuse their soul with the sense of a pitying love infinite presence sweet as summer to the houseless needy
it be too possible that to some of my reader methodism may mean nothing more than low-pitch gable up dingy street sleek grocer sponge preacher and hypocritical jargon--element which be regard as an exhaustive analysis of methodism in many fashionable quarter
that would be a pity for i cannot pretend that seth and dinah be anything else than methodist--not indeed of that modern type which read quarterly review and attend in chapel with pillare portico but of a very old-fashioned kind
they believe in present miracle in instantaneous conversion in revelation by dream and vision they draw lot and seek for divine guidance by open the bible at hazard have a literal way of interpret the scripture which be not at all sanction by approve commentator and it be impossible for i to represent their diction as correct or their instruction as liberal
still--if i have read religious history aright--faith hope and charity have not always be find in a direct ratio with a sensibility to the three concord and it be possible--thank heaven to have very erroneous theory and very sublime feeling
the raw bacon which clumsy molly spare from her own scanty store that she may carry it to her neighbour s child to stop the fit may be a piteously inefficacious remedy but the generous stirring of neighbourly kindness that prompt the deed have a beneficent radiation that be not lose
consider these thing we can hardly think dinah and seth beneath our sympathy accustom as we may be to weep over the loftier sorrow of heroine in satin boot and crinoline and of hero rid fiery horse themselves ride by still more fiery passion
poor seth
he be never on horseback in his life except once when he be a little lad and mr
jonathan burge take he up behind tell he to hold on tight and instead of burst out into wild accusing apostrophe to god and destiny he be resolve as he now walk homeward under the solemn starlight to repress his sadness to be less bent on have his own will and to live more for other as dinah do
chapter iv home and its sorrow a green valley with a brook run through it full almost to overflow with the late rain overhang by low stoop willow
across this brook a plank be throw and over this plank adam bede be pass with his undoubting step follow close by gyp with the basket evidently make his way to the thatched house with a stack of timber by the side of it about twenty yard up the opposite slope
the door of the house be open and an elderly woman be look out but she be not placidly contemplate the evening sunshine she have be watch with dim eye the gradually enlarging speck which for the last few minute she have be quite sure be her darle son adam
lisbeth bede love her son with the love of a woman to whom her first-bear have come late in life
she be an anxious spare yet vigorous old woman clean as a snowdrop
her grey hair be turn neatly back under a pure linen cap with a black band round it her broad chest be cover with a buff neckerchief and below this you see a sort of short bedgown make of blue-checker linen tie round the waist and descend to the hip from whence there be a considerable length of linsey-woolsey petticoat
for lisbeth be tall and in other point too there be a strong likeness between she and her son adam
her dark eye be somewhat dim now--perhaps from too much crying--but her broadly mark eyebrow be still black her tooth be sound and as she stand knit rapidly and unconsciously with her work-harden hand she have as firmly upright an attitude as when she be carry a pail of water on her head from the spring
there be the same type of frame and the same keen activity of temperament in mother and son but it be not from she that adam get his well-fill brow and his expression of large-hearted intelligence
family likeness have often a deep sadness in it
nature that great tragic dramatist knit we together by bone and muscle and divide we by the subtler web of our brain blend yearning and repulsion and tie we by our heart-string to the being that jar we at every movement
we hear a voice with the very cadence of our own utter the thought we despise we see eye--ah so like our mother s averted from we in cold alienation and our last darling child startle we with the air and gesture of the sister we part from in bitterness long year ago
the father to whom we owe our good heritage--the mechanical instinct the keen sensibility to harmony the unconscious skill of the modelling hand--gall we and put we to shame by his daily error the long-lose mother whose face we begin to see in the glass as our own wrinkle come once fret our young soul with her anxious humour and irrational persistence

woman who be never bitter and resentful be often the most querulous and if solomon be as wise as he be repute to be i feel sure that when he compare a contentious woman to a continual dropping on a very rainy day he have not a vixen in his eye--a fury with long nail acrid and selfish
depend upon it he mean a good creature who have no joy but in the happiness of the love one whom she contribute to make uncomfortable put by all the tid-bit for they and spend nothing on herself
such a woman as lisbeth for example--at once patient and complain self-renounce and exact brood the livelong day over what happen yesterday and what be likely to happen to-morrow and cry very readily both at the good and the evil

bodily haste and exertion usually leave our thought very much at the mercy of our feeling and imagination and it be so to-night with adam

so it will go on worsen and worsen think adam therebe no slip uphill again and no standing still when once you ve begin to slip down and then the day come back to he when he be a little fellow and use to run by his father s side proud to be take out to work and prouder still to hear his father boast to his fellow-workman how the little chap have an uncommon notion o carpentering what a fine active fellow his father be then

adam remember well the night of shame and anguish when he first see his father quite wild and foolish shout a song out fitfully among his drunken companion at the waggon overthrown he have run away once when he be only eighteen make his escape in the morning twilight with a little blue bundle over his shoulder and his mensuration book in his pocket and say to himself very decidedly that he could bear the vexation of home no long--he would go and seek his fortune set up his stick at the crossway and bend his step the way it fall
but by the time he get to stoniton the thought of his mother and seth leave behind to endure everything without he become too importunate and his resolution fail he

adam be not a man to be gratuitously superstitious but he have the blood of the peasant in he as well as of the artisan and a peasant can no more help believe in a traditional superstition than a horse can help tremble when he see a camel
besides he have that mental combination which be at once humble in the region of mystery and keen in the region of knowledge it be the depth of his reverence quite as much as his hard common sense which give he his disinclination to doctrinal religion and he often check seth s argumentative spiritualism by say eh itbe a big mystery thee know st but little about it and so it happen that adam be at once penetrate and credulous

i tell it as he tell it not attempt to reduce it to its natural element--in our eagerness to explain impression we often lose our hold of the sympathy that comprehend they
but he have the good antidote against imaginative dread in the necessity for get on with the coffin and for the next ten minute his hammer be ring so uninterruptedly that other sound if there be any might well be overpower

it be a strangely mingled picture--the fresh youth of the summer morning with its edenlike peace and loveliness the stalwart strength of the two brother in their rusty working clothe and the long coffin on their shoulder

get a blanket and make it hot as the fire in reality adam be convince that his father be dead but he know there be no other way of repress his mother s impetuous wail grief than by occupy she with some active task which have hope in it

the wide-open glaze eye be grey like seth s and have once look with mild pride on the boy before whom thias have live to hang his head in shame
seth s chief feeling be awe and distress at this sudden snatch away of his father s soul but adam s mind rush back over the past in a flood of relent and pity
when death the great reconciler have come it be never our tenderness that we repent of but our severity
chapter v the rector before twelve o clock there have be some heavy storm of rain and the water lie in deep gutter on the side of the gravel walk in the garden of broxton parsonage the great provence rose have be cruelly toss by the wind and beat by the rain and all the delicate-stem border flower have be dash down and stain with the wet soil
a melancholy morning--because it be nearly time hay-harvest should begin and instead of that the meadow be likely to be flood
but people who have pleasant home get indoor enjoyment that they would never think of but for the rain
if it have not be a wet morning mr
irwine would not have be in the dining-room playing at chess with his mother and he love both his mother and chess quite well enough to pass some cloudy hour very easily by their help
let i take you into that dining-room and show you the rev
adolphus irwine rector of broxton vicar of hayslope and vicar of blythe a pluralist at whom the severe church reformer would have find it difficult to look sour
we will enter very softly and stand still in the open doorway without awake the glossy-brown setter who be stretch across the hearth with her two puppy beside she or the pug who be doze with his black muzzle aloft like a sleepy president
the room be a large and lofty one with an ample mullione oriel window at one end the wall you see be new and not yet paint but the furniture though originally of an expensive sort be old and scanty and there be no drapery about the window
the crimson cloth over the large dining-table be very threadbare though it contrast pleasantly enough with the dead hue of the plaster on the wall but on this cloth there be a massive silver waiter with a decanter of water on it of the same pattern as two large one that be prop up on the sideboard with a coat of arm conspicuous in their centre
you suspect at once that the inhabitant of this room have inherit more blood than wealth and would not be surprised to find that mr
irwine have a finely cut nostril and upper lip but at present we can only see that he have a broad flat back and an abundance of powdered hair all throw backward and tie behind with a black ribbon--a bit of conservatism in costume which tell you that he be not a young man
he will perhaps turn round by and by and in the meantime we can look at that stately old lady his mother a beautiful aged brunette whose rich-toned complexion be well set off by the complex wrapping of pure white cambric and lace about her head and neck
she be as erect in she comely embonpoint as a statue of ceres and her dark face with its delicate aquiline nose firm proud mouth and small intense black eye be so keen and sarcastic in its expression that you instinctively substitute a pack of card for the chess-man and imagine she tell your fortune
the small brown hand with which she be lift her queen be laden with pearl diamond and turquoise and a large black veil be very carefully adjust over the crown of her cap and fall in sharp contrast on the white fold about her neck
it must take a long time to dress that old lady in the morning

and mr
irwine s sister as any person of family within ten mile of broxton could have testify be such stupid unintereste woman
it be quite a pity handsome clever mrs
irwine should have have such commonplace daughter
that fine old lady herself be worth drive ten mile to see any day her beauty her well-preserve faculty and her old-fashioned dignity make she a graceful subject for conversation in turn with the king s health the sweet new pattern in cotton dress the news from egypt and lord dacey s lawsuit which be fret poor lady dacey to death
but no one ever think of mention the miss irwines except the poor people in broxton village who regard they as deep in the science of medicine and speak of they vaguely as the gentlefolk if any one have ask old job dummilow who give he his flannel jacket he would have answer the gentlefolk last winter and widow steene dwelt much on the virtue of the stuff the gentlefolk give she for her cough
under this name too they be use with great effect as a means of tame refractory child so that at the sight of poor miss anne s sallow face several small urchin have a terrified sense that she be cognizant of all their bad misdemeanours and know the precise number of stone with which they have intend to hit farmer britton s duck
but for all who see they through a less mythical medium the miss irwines be quite superfluous existence--inartistic figure crowd the canvas of life without adequate effect
miss anne indeed if her chronic headache could have be account for by a pathetic story of disappointed love might have have some romantic interest attach to she but no such story have either be know or invent concern she and the general impression be quite in accordance with the fact that both the sister be old maid for the prosaic reason that they have never receive an eligible offer
nevertheless to speak paradoxically the existence of insignificant people have very important consequence in the world
it can be show to affect the price of bread and the rate of wage to call forth many evil temper from the selfish and many heroism from the sympathetic and in other way to play no small part in the tragedy of life
and if that handsome generous-blooded clergyman the rev
adolphus irwine have not have these two hopelessly maiden sister his lot would have be shape quite differently he would very likely have take a comely wife in his youth and now when his hair be get grey under the powder would have have tall son and bloom daughter--such possession in short as man commonly think will repay they for all the labour they take under the sun
as it be--have with all his three living no more than seven hundred a-year and see no way of keep his splendid mother and his sickly sister not to reckon a second sister who be usually speak of without any adjective in such ladylike ease as become their birth and habit and at the same time provide for a family of his own--he remain you see at the age of eight-and-forty a bachelor not make any merit of that renunciation but say laughingly if any one allude to it that he make it an excuse for many indulgence which a wife would never have allow he
and perhaps he be the only person in the world who do not think his sister unintereste and superfluous for his be one of those large-hearted sweet-blooded nature that never know a narrow or a grudging thought epicurean if you will with no enthusiasm no self-scourging sense of duty but yet as you have see of a sufficiently subtle moral fibre to have an unwearye tenderness for obscure and monotonous suffering
it be his large-hearted indulgence that make he ignore his mother s hardness towards her daughter which be the more striking from its contrast with her doting fondness towards himself he hold it no virtue to frown at irremediable fault
see the difference between the impression a man make on you when you walk by his side in familiar talk or look at he in his home and the figure he make when see from a lofty historical level or even in the eye of a critical neighbour who think of he as an embody system or opinion rather than as a man
mr
roe the travel preacher station at treddleston have include mr
irwine in a general statement concern the church clergy in the surround district whom he describe as man give up to the lust of the flesh and the pride of life hunt and shooting and adorn their own house ask what shall we eat and what shall we drink and wherewithal shall we be clothed careless of dispense the bread of life to their flock preach at good but a carnal and soul-benumb morality and trafficking in the soul of man by receive money for discharge the pastoral office in parish where they do not so much as look on the face of the people more than once a-year
the ecclesiastical historian too look into parliamentary report of that period find honourable member zealous for the church and untainted with any sympathy for the tribe of canting methodists make statement scarcely less melancholy than that of mr
roe
and it be impossible for i to say that mr
irwine be altogether belie by the generic classification assign he
he really have no very lofty aim no theological enthusiasm if i be closely question i should be oblige to confess that he feel no serious alarm about the soul of his parishioner and would have think it a mere loss of time to talk in a doctrinal and awakening manner to old feyther taft or even to chad cranage the blacksmith
if he have be in the habit of speak theoretically he would perhaps have say that the only healthy form religion could take in such mind be that of certain dim but strong emotion suffuse themselves as a hallow influence over the family affection and neighbourly duty
he think the custom of baptism more important than its doctrine and that the religious benefit the peasant draw from the church where his father worship and the sacred piece of turf where they lie bury be but slightly dependent on a clear understanding of the liturgy or the sermon
clearly the rector be not what be call in these day an earnest man he be fonder of church history than of divinity and have much more insight into man s character than interest in their opinion he be neither laborious nor obviously self-deny nor very copious in alm-giving and his theology you perceive be lax
his mental palate indeed be rather pagan and find a savouriness in a quotation from sophocles or theocritus that be quite absent from any text in isaiah or amos
but if you feed your young setter on raw flesh how can you wonder at its retain a relish for uncooked partridge in after-life
and mr
irwine s recollection of young enthusiasm and ambition be all associate with poetry and ethic that lie aloof from the bible
on the other hand i must plead for i have an affectionate partiality towards the rector s memory that he be not vindictive--and some philanthropist have be so that he be not intolerant--and there be a rumour that some zealous theologian have not be altogether free from that blemish that although he would probably have decline to give his body to be burn in any public cause and be far from bestow all his good to feed the poor he have that charity which have sometimes be lack to very illustrious virtue--he be tender to other man s failing and unwilling to impute evil
he be one of those man and they be not the commonest of whom we can know the good only by follow they away from the marketplace the platform and the pulpit enter with they into their own home hear the voice with which they speak to the young and aged about their own hearthstone and witness their thoughtful care for the everyday want of everyday companion who take all their kindness as a matter of course and not as a subject for panegyric
such man happily have live in time when great abuse flourish and have sometimes even be the live representative of the abuse
that be a thought which might comfort we a little under the opposite fact--that it be well sometimes not to follow great reformer of abuse beyond the threshold of their home
but whatever you may think of mr
irwine now if you have meet he that june afternoon rid on his grey cob with his dog run beside he--portly upright manly with a good-natured smile on his finely turn lip as he talk to his dash young companion on the bay mare you must have feel that however ill he harmonize with sound theory of the clerical office he somehow harmonize extremely well with that peaceful landscape
see they in the bright sunlight interrupt every now and then by rolling mass of cloud ascend the slope from the broxton side where the tall gable and elm of the rectory predominate over the tiny whitewash church
they will soon be in the parish of hayslope the grey church-tower and village roof lie before they to the left and far on to the right they can just see the chimney of the hall farm
chapter vi the hall farm evidently that gate be never open for the long grass and the great hemlock grow close against it and if it be open it be so rusty that the force necessary to turn it on its hinge would be likely to pull down the square stone-build pillar to the detriment of the two stone lioness which grin with a doubtful carnivorous affability above a coat of arm surmount each of the pillar
it would be easy enough by the aid of the nick in the stone pillar to climb over the brick wall with its smooth stone cop but by put our eye close to the rusty bar of the gate we can see the house well enough and all but the very corner of the grassy enclosure
it be a very fine old place of red brick soften by a pale powdery lichen which have disperse itself with happy irregularity so as to bring the red brick into term of friendly companionship with the limestone ornament surround the three gable the window and the door-place
but the window be patch with wooden pane and the door i think be like the gate--it be never open
how it would groan and grate against the stone floor if it be
for it be a solid heavy handsome door and must once have be in the habit of shut with a sonorous bang behind a liveried lackey who have just see his master and mistress off the ground in a carriage and pair
but at present one might fancy the house in the early stage of a chancery suit and that the fruit from that grand double row of walnut-tree on the right hand of the enclosure would fall and rot among the grass if it be not that we hear the booming bark of dog echo from great building at the back
and now the half-wean calf that have be shelter themselves in a gorse-build hovel against the leave-hand wall come out and set up a silly answer to that terrible bark doubtless suppose that it have reference to bucket of milk
yes the house must be inhabit and we will see by whom for imagination be a licensed trespasser it have no fear of dog but may climb over wall and peep in at window with impunity
put your face to one of the glass pane in the right-hand window what do you see
a large open fireplace with rusty dog in it and a bare board floor at the far end fleece of wool stack up in the middle of the floor some empty corn-bag
that be the furniture of the dining-room
and what through the left-hand window
several clothe-horse a pillion a spinning-wheel and an old box wide open and stuff full of coloured rag
at the edge of this box there lie a great wooden doll which so far as mutilation be concern bear a strong resemblance to the fine greek sculpture and especially in the total loss of its nose
near it there be a little chair and the butt end of a boy s leather long-lash whip
the history of the house be plain now
it be once the residence of a country squire whose family probably dwindle down to mere spinsterhood get merge in the more territorial name of donnithorne
it be once the hall it be now the hall farm
like the life in some coast town that be once a watering-place and be now a port where the genteel street be silent and grass-grow and the dock and warehouse busy and resonant the life at the hall have change its focus and no long radiate from the parlour but from the kitchen and the farmyard
plenty of life there though this be the drowsy time of the year just before hay-harvest and it be the drowsy time of the day too for it be close upon three by the sun and it be half-past three by mrs
poyser s handsome eight-day clock
but there be always a strong sense of life when the sun be brilliant after rain and now he be pour down his beam and make sparkle among the wet straw and lighting up every patch of vivid green moss on the red tile of the cow-shed and turn even the muddy water that be hurry along the channel to the drain into a mirror for the yellow-bill duck who be seize the opportunity of get a drink with as much body in it as possible
there be quite a concert of noise the great bull-dog chain against the stable be throw into furious exasperation by the unwary approach of a cock too near the mouth of his kennel and send forth a thunder bark which be answer by two fox-hound shut up in the opposite cow-house the old top-knot hen scratch with their chick among the straw set up a sympathetic croaking as the discomfited cock join they a sow with her brood all very muddy as to the leg and curl as to the tail throw in some deep staccato note our friend the calf be bleat from the home croft and under all a fine ear discern the continuous hum of human voice
for the great barn-door be throw wide open and man be busy there mend the harness under the superintendence of mr
goby the whittaw otherwise saddler who entertain they with the late treddleston gossip
it be certainly rather an unfortunate day that alick the shepherd have choose for have the whittaw since the morning turn out so wet and mrs
poyser have speak her mind pretty strongly as to the dirt which the extra number of man s shoe bring into the house at dinnertime
indeed she have not yet recover she equanimity on the subject though it be now nearly three hour since dinner and the house-floor be perfectly clean again as clean as everything else in that wonderful house-place where the only chance of collect a few grain of dust would be to climb on the salt-coffer and put your finger on the high mantel-shelf on which the glitter brass candlestick be enjoy their summer sinecure for at this time of year of course every one go to bed while it be yet light or at least light enough to discern the outline of object after you have bruise your shin against they
surely nowhere else could an oak clock-case and an oak table have get to such a polish by the hand genuine elbow polish as mrs
poyser call it for she thank god she never have any of your varnished rubbish in her house
hetty sorrel often take the opportunity when her aunt s back be turn of look at the pleasing reflection of herself in those polished surface for the oak table be usually turn up like a screen and be more for ornament than for use and she could see herself sometimes in the great round pewter dish that be range on the shelf above the long deal dinner-table or in the hob of the grate which always shine like jasper
everything be look at its bright at this moment for the sun shine right on the pewter dish and from their reflect surface pleasant jet of light be throw on mellow oak and bright brass--and on a still pleasanter object than these for some of the ray fall on dinah s finely mould cheek and light up her pale red hair to auburn as she bend over the heavy household linen which she be mend for her aunt
no scene could have be more peaceful if mrs
poyser who be iron a few thing that still remain from the monday s wash have not be make a frequent clinking with her iron and move to and fro whenever she want it to cool carry the keen glance of her blue-grey eye from the kitchen to the dairy where hetty be make up the butter and from the dairy to the back kitchen where nancy be take the pie out of the oven
do not suppose however that mrs
poyser be elderly or shrewish in her appearance she be a good-look woman not more than eight-and-thirty of fair complexion and sandy hair well-shapen light-footed
the most conspicuous article in her attire be an ample checker linen apron which almost cover she skirt and nothing could be plainer or less noticeable than her cap and gown for there be no weakness of which she be less tolerant than feminine vanity and the preference of ornament to utility
the family likeness between she and her niece dinah morris with the contrast between her keenness and dinah s seraphic gentleness of expression might have serve a painter as an excellent suggestion for a martha and mary
their eye be just of the same colour but a striking test of the difference in their operation be see in the demeanour of trip the black-and-tan terrier whenever that much-suspect dog unwarily expose himself to the freezing arctic ray of mrs
poyser s glance
her tongue be not less keen than her eye and whenever a damsel come within earshot seem to take up an unfinished lecture as a barrel-organ take up a tune precisely at the point where it have leave off
the fact that it be churn day be another reason why it be inconvenient to have the whittaw and why consequently mrs
poyser should scold molly the housemaid with unusual severity
to all appearance molly have get through her after-dinner work in an exemplary manner have clean herself with great dispatch and now come to ask submissively if she should sit down to her spinning till milk time
but this blameless conduct accord to mrs
poyser shroud a secret indulgence of unbecoming wish which she now drag forth and hold up to molly s view with cut eloquence
spinning indeed
it benot spin as youwould be at iwill be bind and let you have your own way
i never know your equal for gallowsness
to think of a gell o your age want to go and sit with half-a-dozen man
iwould ha be ashamed to let the word pass over my lip if ihave be you
and you as have be here ever since last michaelmas and i hire you at treddles on stattit without a bit o character--as i say you might be grateful to be hire in that way to a respectable place and you know no more o what belong to work when you come here than the mawkin i the field
as poor a two-fiste thing as ever i see you know you be
who teach you to scrub a floor i should like to know
why youwould leave the dirt in heap i the corner--anybody ud think youwould never be bring up among christians
and as for spinning why youhave waste as much as your wage i the flax youhave spoil learn to spin
and youhave a right to feel that and not to go about as gap and as thoughtless as if you be behold to nobody
comb the wool for the whittaw indeed
thatbe what youwould like to be do be it
thatbe the way with you--thatbe the road youwould all like to go headlong to ruin
yoube never easy till youhave get some sweetheart as be as big a fool as yourself you think youwill be finely off when yoube married i daresay and have get a three-legged stool to sit on and never a blanket to cover you and a bit o oat-cake for your dinner as three child be a-snatching at ibe sure i donna want t go wi the whittaw say molly whimpering and quite overcome by this dantean picture of her future on y we allay use to comb the wool for n at mester ottley s an so i just ax ye

and to think o your know no well molly and be here a-go i nine month and not for want o talk to neither--and what be you stanne there for like a jack as be run down instead o get your wheel out
yoube a rare un for sit down to your work a little while after itbe time to put by munny my iron s twite tell pease put it down to warm the small chirrup voice that utter this request come from a little sunny-haired girl between three and four who seat on a high chair at the end of the iron table be arduously clutch the handle of a miniature iron with her tiny fat fist and iron rag with an assiduity that require she to put her little red tongue out as far as anatomy would allow
cold be it my darling

you look th image o your aunt judith dinah when you sit a-sewing

judith and i allay hang together though she have such queer way but your mother and she never could agree
ah your mother little thought as shewould have a daughter just cut out after the very pattern o judith and leave she an orphan too for judith to take care on and bring up with a spoon when she be in the graveyard at stoniton
i allay say that o judith as shewould bear a pound weight any day to save anybody else carry a ounce

and she be very fond of you too aunt rachel
i often hear her talk of you in the same sort of way
