iwould ha be glad to behave to you like a mother s sister if youwould come and live i this country where therebe some shelter and victual for man and beast and folk donot live on the naked hill like poultry a-scratching on a gravel bank
and then you might get married to some decent man and therewould be plenty ready to have you if youwould only leave off that preaching as be ten time bad than anything your aunt judith ever do
and even if youwould marry seth bede as be a poor wool-gathering methodist andbe never like to have a penny beforehand i know your uncle ud help you with a pig and very like a cow for hebe allay be good-natur d to my kin for all theybe poor and make them welcome to the house and ud do for you iwill be bind as much as ever hewould do for hetty though shebe his own niece
and therebe linen in the house as i could well spare you for ihave get lot o sheet and table-clothing and towel as benot make up
therebe a piece o sheet i could give you as that squint kitty spin--she be a rare girl to spin for all she squint and the child couldnot abide she and you know the spinningbe go on constant and therebe new linen weave twice as fast as the old wear out
but wherebe the use o talking if ye wonna be persuade and settle down like any other woman in her sens i stead o wear yourself out with walk and preaching and give away every penny you get so as youhave nothing save against sickness and all the thing youhave get i the world i verily believe ud go into a bundle no big nor a double cheese
and all because youhave get notion i your head about religion more nor whatbe i the catechism and the prayer-book but not more than whatbe in the bible aunt say dinah
yes and the bible too for that matter mrs
poyser rejoin rather sharply else why shouldnot they as know good whatbe in the bible--the parson and people as have get nothing to do but learn it--do the same as you do
but for the matter o that if everybody be to do like you the world must come to a standstill for if everybody try to do without house and home and with poor eating and drinking and be allay talk as we must despise the thing o the world as you say i should like to know where the pick o the stock and the corn and the good new-milk cheese ud have to go
everybody ud be want bread make o tail end and everybody ud be run after everybody else to preach to them istead o bring up their family and lay by against a bad harvest
it stand to sense as that cannot be the right religion nay dear aunt you never hear i say that all people be call to forsake their work and their family
itbe quite right the land should be plough and sow and the precious corn store and the thing of this life care for and right that people should rejoice in their family and provide for they so that this be do in the fear of the lord and that they be not unmindful of the soul s want while they be care for the body
we can all be servant of god wherever our lot be cast but he give we different sort of work accord as he fit we for it and call we to it
i can no more help spend my life in try to do what i can for the soul of other than you could help run if you hear little totty cry at the other end of the house the voice would go to your heart you would think the dear child be in trouble or in danger and you couldnot rest without run to help she and comfort she ah say mrs

chapter vii the dairy the dairy be certainly worth look at it be a scene to sicken for with a sort of calenture in hot and dusty street--such coolness such purity such fresh fragrance of new-press cheese of firm butter of wooden vessel perpetually bath in pure water such soft colouring of red earthenware and creamy surface brown wood and polished tin grey limestone and rich orange-red rust on the iron weight and hook and hinge
but one get only a confused notion of these detail when they surround a distractingly pretty girl of seventeen stand on little patten and round her dimpled arm to lift a pound of butter out of the scale
hetty blush a deep rose-colour when captain donnithorne enter the dairy and speak to she but it be not at all a distressed blush for it be inwreathe with smile and dimple and with sparkle from under long curl dark eyelash and while her aunt be discourse to he about the limited amount of milk that be to be spar for butter and cheese so long as the calf be not all wean and a large quantity but inferior quality of milk yield by the shorthorn which have be buy on experiment together with other matter which must be interesting to a young gentleman who would one day be a landlord hetty toss and pat her pound of butter with quite a self-possess coquettish air slyly conscious that no turn of her head be lose
there be various order of beauty cause man to make fool of themselves in various style from the desperate to the sheepish but there be one order of beauty which seem make to turn the head not only of man but of all intelligent mammal even of woman
it be a beauty like that of kitten or very small downy duck make gentle ripple noise with their soft bill or baby just begin to toddle and to engage in conscious mischief--a beauty with which you can never be angry but that you feel ready to crush for inability to comprehend the state of mind into which it throw you
hetty sorrel s be that sort of beauty
her aunt mrs
poyser who profess to despise all personal attraction and intend to be the severest of mentor continually gaze at hetty s charm by the sly fascinate in spite of herself and after administer such a scolding as naturally flow from her anxiety to do well by her husband s niece--who have no mother of her own to scold she poor thing she would often confess to her husband when they be safe out of hearing that she firmly believe the naughty the little huzzy behave the pretty she look it be of little use for i to tell you that hetty s cheek be like a rose-petal that dimple play about her pouting lip that her large dark eye hide a soft roguishness under their long lash and that her curly hair though all push back under her round cap while she be at work steal back in dark delicate ring on her forehead and about her white shell-like ear it be of little use for i to say how lovely be the contour of her pink-and-white neckerchief tuck into her low plum-colour stuff bodice or how the linen butter-make apron with its bib seem a thing to be imitate in silk by duchess since it fall in such charming line or how her brown stocking and thick-sole buckle shoe lose all that clumsiness which they must certainly have have when empty of her foot and ankle--of little use unless you have see a woman who affect you as hetty affect her beholder for otherwise though you might conjure up the image of a lovely woman she would not in the least resemble that distract kittenlike maiden
i might mention all the divine charm of a bright spring day but if you have never in your life utterly forget yourself in strain your eye after the mount lark or in wander through the still lane when the fresh-open blossom fill they with a sacred silent beauty like that of fretted aisle where would be the use of my descriptive catalogue
i could never make you know what i mean by a bright spring day
hetty s be a spring-tide beauty it be the beauty of young frisking thing round-limbe gambolling circumvent you by a false air of innocence--the innocence of a young star-browe calf for example that be incline for a promenade out of bound lead you a severe steeplechase over hedge and ditch and only come to a stand in the middle of a bog
and they be the pretty attitude and movement into which a pretty girl be throw in make up butter--toss movement that give a charming curve to the arm and a sideward inclination of the round white neck little pat and roll movement with the palm of the hand and nice adaptation and finishing which cannot at all be effect without a great play of the pouting mouth and the dark eye
and then the butter itself seem to communicate a fresh charm--it be so pure so sweet-scented it be turn off the mould with such a beautiful firm surface like marble in a pale yellow light
moreover hetty be particularly clever at make up the butter it be the one performance of hers that her aunt allow to pass without severe criticism so she handle it with all the grace that belong to mastery
i hope you will be ready for a great holiday on the thirtieth of july mrs
poyser say captain donnithorne when he have sufficiently admire the dairy and give several improvise opinion on swede turnip and shorthorn
you know what be to happen then and i shall expect you to be one of the guest who come early and leave late
will you promise i your hand for two dance miss hetty
if i donot get your promise now i know i shall hardly have a chance for all the smart young farmer will take care to secure you hetty smile and blush but before she could answer mrs
poyser interpose scandalize at the mere suggestion that the young squire could be exclude by any mean partner
indeed sir you be very kind to take that notice of she

chapter viii a vocation dinah who have rise when the gentleman come in but still keep hold of the sheet she be mend curtsy respectfully when she see mr

itbe a dreary bleak place
they be build a cotton-mill there but thatbe many year ago now
i suppose the place be a good deal change by the employment that mill must have bring it be change so far as the mill have bring people there who get a livelihood for themselves by work in it and make it well for the tradesfolk
i work in it myself and have reason to be grateful for thereby i have enough and to spare
but itbe still a bleak place as you say sir--very different from this country you have relation live there probably so that you be attach to the place as your home i have an aunt there once she bring i up for i be an orphan
but she be take away seven year ago and i have no other kindre that i know of besides my aunt poyser who be very good to i and would have i come and live in this country which to be sure be a good land wherein they eat bread without scarceness
but ibe not free to leave snowfield where i be first plant and have grow deep into it like the small grass on the hill-top ah i daresay you have many religious friend and companion there you be a methodist--a wesleyan i think yes my aunt at snowfield belong to the society and i have cause to be thankful for the privilege i have have thereby from my early childhood and have you be long in the habit of preach
for i understand you preach at hayslope last night i first take to the work four year since when i be twenty-one your society sanction woman s preaching then it donot forbid they sir when theyhave a clear call to the work and when their ministry be own by the conversion of sinner and the strengthening of god s people
mrs
fletcher as you may have hear about be the first woman to preach in the society i believe before she be marry when she be miss bosanquet and mr
wesley approve of she undertake the work
she have a great gift and there be many other now live who be precious fellow-helper in the work of the ministry
i understand therebe be voice raise against it in the society of late but i cannot but think their counsel will come to nought
it benot for man to make channel for god s spirit as they make channel for the watercourse and say flow here but flow not there but donot you find some danger among your people--i donot mean to say that it be so with you far from it--but donot you find sometimes that both man and woman fancy themselves channel for god s spirit and be quite mistaken so that they set about a work for which they be unfit and bring holy thing into contempt doubtless it be so sometimes for there have be evil-doer among we who have seek to deceive the brother and some there be who deceive their own self
but we be not without discipline and correction to put a check upon these thing
therebe a very strict order keep among we and the brother and sister watch for each other s soul as they that must give account
they donot go every one his own way and say be i my brother s keeper but tell i--if i may ask and i be really interested in know it--how you first come to think of preach indeed sir i donot think of it at all--i d be use from the time i be sixteen to talk to the little child and teach they and sometimes i have have my heart enlarge to speak in class and be much draw out in prayer with the sick
but i have feel no call to preach for when ibe not greatly work upon ibe too much give to sit still and keep by myself
it seem as if i could sit silent all day long with the thought of god overflow my soul--as the pebble lie bath in the willow brook
for thought be so great--aren t they sir
they seem to lie upon we like a deep flood and itbe my besetment to forget where i be and everything about i and lose myself in thought that i could give no account of for i could neither make a beginning nor end of they in word
that be my way as long as i can remember but sometimes it seem as if speech come to i without any will of my own and word be give to i that come out as the tear come because our heart be full and we cannot help it
and those be always time of great blessing though i have never think it could be so with i before a congregation of people
but sir we be lead on like the little child by a way that we know not
i be call to preach quite suddenly and since then i have never be leave in doubt about the work that be lay upon i but tell i the circumstance--just how it be the very day you begin to preach it be one sunday i walk with brother marlowe who be an aged man one of the local preacher all the way to hetton-deeps--thatbe a village where the people get their living by work in the lead-mine and where therebe no church nor preacher but they live like sheep without a shepherd
itbe well than twelve mile from snowfield so we set out early in the morning for it be summertime and i have a wonderful sense of the divine love as we walk over the hill where therebe no tree you know sir as there be here to make the sky look small but you see the heaven stretch out like a tent and you feel the everlaste arm around you
but before we get to hetton brother marlowe be seize with a dizziness that make he afraid of fall for he overwork himself sadly at his year in watch and pray and walk so many mile to speak the word as well as carry on his trade of linen-weaving
and when we get to the village the people be expect he for hehave appoint the time and the place when he be there before and such of they as care to hear the word of life be assemble on a spot where the cottage be thick so as other might be draw to come
but he feel as he couldnot stand up to preach and he be force to lie down in the first of the cottage we come to
so i go to tell the people think wewould go into one of the house and i would read and pray with they
but as i pass along by the cottage and see the aged and tremble woman at the door and the hard look of the man who seem to have their eye no more fill with the sight of the sabbath morning than if they have be dumb oxen that never look up to the sky i feel a great movement in my soul and i tremble as if i be shake by a strong spirit enter into my weak body
and i go to where the little flock of people be gather together and step on the low wall that be build against the green hillside and i speak the word that be give to i abundantly
and they all come round i out of all the cottage and many weep over their sin and have since be join to the lord
that be the beginning of my preaching sir and ihave preach ever since dinah have let her work fall during this narrative which she utter in her usual simple way but with that sincere articulate thrill treble by which she always master her audience
she stoop now to gather up her sewing and then go on with it as before

irwine be deeply interested

itbe wonderful how rich be the harvest of soul up those high-walled street where you seem to walk as in a prison-yard and the ear be deafen with the sound of worldly toil

chapter ix hetty s world while she adjust the broad leaf that set off the pale fragrant butter as the primrose be set off by its nest of green i be afraid hetty be think a great deal more of the look captain donnithorne have cast at she than of adam and his trouble
bright admire glance from a handsome young gentleman with white hand a gold chain occasional regimental and wealth and grandeur immeasurable--those be the warm ray that set poor hetty s heart vibrate and play its little foolish tune over and over again
we do not hear that memnon s statue give forth its melody at all under the rushing of the mighty wind or in response to any other influence divine or human than certain short-live sunbeam of morning and we must learn to accommodate ourselves to the discovery that some of those cunningly fashion instrument call human soul have only a very limited range of music and will not vibrate in the least under a touch that fill other with tremulous rapture or quivering agony
hetty be quite use to the thought that people like to look at she
she be not blind to the fact that young luke britton of broxton come to hayslope church on a sunday afternoon on purpose that he might see she and that he would have make much more decided advance if her uncle poyser thinking but lightly of a young man whose father s land be so foul as old luke britton s have not forbid her aunt to encourage he by any civility
she be aware too that mr
craig the gardener at the chase be over head and ear in love with she and have lately make unmistakable avowal in luscious strawberry and hyperbolical pea
she know still well that adam bede--tall upright clever brave adam bede--who carry such authority with all the people round about and whom her uncle be always delighted to see of an evening say that adam know a fine sight more o the natur o thing than those as think themselves his betters she know that this adam who be often rather stern to other people and not much give to run after the lass could be make to turn pale or red any day by a word or a look from she
hetty s sphere of comparison be not large but she couldnot help perceive that adam be something like a man always know what to say about thing could tell her uncle how to prop the hovel and have mend the churn in no time know with only look at it the value of the chestnut-tree that be blow down and why the damp come in the wall and what they must do to stop the rat and write a beautiful hand that you could read off and could do figure in his head--a degree of accomplishment totally unknown among the rich farmer of that countryside
not at all like that slouch luke britton who when she once walk with he all the way from broxton to hayslope have only broken silence to remark that the grey goose have begin to lay
and as for mr
craig the gardener he be a sensible man enough to be sure but he be knock-kneed and have a queer sort of sing-song in his talk moreover on the most charitable supposition he must be far on the way to forty
hetty be quite certain her uncle want she to encourage adam and would be pleased for she to marry he
for those be time when there be no rigid demarcation of rank between the farmer and the respectable artisan and on the home hearth as well as in the public house they might be see take their jug of ale together the farmer have a latent sense of capital and of weight in parish affair which sustain he under his conspicuous inferiority in conversation
martin poyser be not a frequenter of public house but he like a friendly chat over his own home-brew and though it be pleasant to lay down the law to a stupid neighbour who have no notion how to make the good of his farm it be also an agreeable variety to learn something from a clever fellow like adam bede
accordingly for the last three year--ever since he have superintend the building of the new barn--adam have always be make welcome at the hall farm especially of a winter evening when the whole family in patriarchal fashion master and mistress child and servant be assemble in that glorious kitchen at well-graduate distance from the blaze fire
and for the last two year at least hetty have be in the habit of hear she uncle say adam bede may be work for wage now but hewill be a master-man some day as sure as i sit in this chair
mester burge be in the right on t to want he to go partner and marry his daughter if itbe true what they say the woman as marry he ull have a good take be t lady day or michaelmas a remark which mrs
poyser always follow up with her cordial assent
ah she would say itbe all very fine have a ready-make rich man but mayhappen hewill be a ready-make fool and itbe no use fill your pocket full o money if youhave get a hole in the corner
itwill do you no good to sit in a spring-cart o your own if youhave get a soft to drive you hewill soon turn you over into the ditch
i allay say iwould never marry a man as have get no brain for wherebe the use of a woman have brain of her own if shebe tackle to a geck as everybodybe a-laugh at
she might as well dress herself fine to sit back ard on a donkey these expression though figurative sufficiently indicate the bent of mrs
poyser s mind with regard to adam and though she and her husband might have view the subject differently if hetty have be a daughter of their own it be clear that they would have welcome the match with adam for a penniless niece
for what could hetty have be but a servant elsewhere if her uncle have not take she in and bring she up as a domestic help to her aunt whose health since the birth of totty have not be equal to more positive labour than the superintendence of servant and child
but hetty have never give adam any steady encouragement
even in the moment when she be most thoroughly conscious of his superiority to her other admirer she have never bring herself to think of accept he
she like to feel that this strong skilful keen-eyed man be in her power and would have be indignant if he have show the least sign of slip from under the yoke of her coquettish tyranny and attach himself to the gentle mary burge who would have be grateful enough for the most trifling notice from he
mary burge indeed
such a sallow-face girl if she put on a bit of pink ribbon she look as yellow as a crow-flower and her hair be as straight as a hank of cotton and always when adam stay away for several week from the hall farm and otherwise make some show of resistance to his passion as a foolish one hetty take care to entice he back into the net by little air of meekness and timidity as if she be in trouble at his neglect
but as to marry adam that be a very different affair

her cheek never grow a shade deeply when his name be mention she feel no thrill when she see he pass along the causeway by the window or advance towards she unexpectedly in the footpath across the meadow she feel nothing when his eye rest on she but the cold triumph of know that he love she and would not care to look at mary burge
he could no more stir in she the emotion that make the sweet intoxication of young love than the mere picture of a sun can stir the spring sap in the subtle fibre of the plant
she see he as he be--a poor man with old parent to keep who would not be able for a long while to come to give she even such luxury as she share in her uncle s house
and hetty s dream be all of luxury to sit in a carpet parlour and always wear white stocking to have some large beautiful ear-ring such as be all the fashion to have nottingham lace round the top of her gown and something to make her handkerchief smell nice like miss lydia donnithorne s when she draw it out at church and not to be oblige to get up early or be scold by anybody
she think if adam have be rich and could have give she these thing she love he well enough to marry he
but for the last few week a new influence have come over hetty--vague atmospheric shape itself into no self-confess hope or prospect but produce a pleasant narcotic effect make she tread the ground and go about her work in a sort of dream unconscious of weight or effort and show she all thing through a soft liquid veil as if she be live not in this solid world of brick and stone but in a beatified world such as the sun light up for we in the water
hetty have become aware that mr
arthur donnithorne would take a good deal of trouble for the chance of see she that he always place himself at church so as to have the full view of she both sit and stand that he be constantly find reason for call at the hall farm and always would contrive to say something for the sake of make she speak to he and look at he
the poor child no more conceive at present the idea that the young squire could ever be her lover than a baker s pretty daughter in the crowd whom a young emperor distinguish by an imperial but admire smile conceive that she shall be make empress
but the baker s daughter go home and dream of the handsome young emperor and perhaps weigh the flour amiss while she be think what a heavenly lot it must be to have he for a husband
and so poor hetty have get a face and a presence haunt she wake and sleeping dream bright soft glance have penetrate she and suffuse her life with a strange happy languor
the eye that shed those glance be really not half so fine as adam s which sometimes look at she with a sad beseech tenderness but they have find a ready medium in hetty s little silly imagination whereas adam s could get no entrance through that atmosphere
for three week at least her inward life have consist of little else than live through in memory the look and word arthur have direct towards she--of little else than recall the sensation with which she hear his voice outside the house and see he enter and become conscious that his eye be fix on she and then become conscious that a tall figure look down on she with eye that seem to touch she be come near in clothe of beautiful texture with an odour like that of a flower-garden borne on the evening breeze
foolish thought
but all this happen you must remember nearly sixty year ago and hetty be quite uneducated--a simple farmer s girl to whom a gentleman with a white hand be dazzle as an olympian god

that have never happen yet and now her imagination instead of retrace the past be busy fashion what would happen to-morrow--whereabout in the chase she should see he come towards she how she should put her new rose-colour ribbon on which he have never see and what he would say to she to make she return his glance--a glance which she would be live through in her memory over and over again all the rest of the day
in this state of mind how could hetty give any feeling to adam s trouble or think much about poor old thias be drown
young soul in such pleasant delirium as hers be as unsympathetic as butterfly sipping nectar they be isolate from all appeal by a barrier of dream--by invisible look and impalpable arm

shebe a perfect hebe and if i be an artist i would paint she
itbe amazing what pretty girl one see among the farmer daughter when the man be such clown
that common round red face one see sometimes in the man--all cheek and no feature like martin poyser s--come out in the woman of the famuly as the most charming phiz imaginable well i have no objection to your contemplating hetty in an artistic light but i must not have you feed her vanity and fill her little noddle with the notion that shebe a great beauty attractive to fine gentleman or you will spoil she for a poor man s wife--honest craig s for example whom i have see bestow soft glance on she
the little puss seem already to have air enough to make a husband as miserable as itbe a law of nature for a quiet man to be when he marry a beauty
apropos of marry i hope our friend adam will get settle now the poor old man s go
he will only have his mother to keep in future and ihave a notion that therebe a kindness between he and that nice modest girl mary burge from something that fall from old jonathan one day when i be talk to he
but when i mention the subject to adam he look uneasy and turn the conversation
i suppose the love-making donot run smooth or perhaps adam hang back till hebe in a well position
he have independence of spirit enough for two man--rather an excess of pride if anything that would be a capital match for adam
he would slip into old burge s shoe and make a fine thing of that building business iwill answer for he
i should like to see he well settle in this parish he would be ready then to act as my grand-vizier when i want one

i pique myself on my wisdom there arthur and as an old fellow to whom wisdom have become cheap i bestow it upon you thank you

chapter x dinah visits lisbeth at five o clock lisbeth come downstairs with a large key in her hand it be the key of the chamber where her husband lie dead
throughout the day except in her occasional outburst of wail grief she have be in incessant movement perform the initial duty to she dead with the awe and exactitude that belong to religious rite
she have bring out her little store of bleached linen which she have for long year keep in reserve for this supreme use
it seem but yesterday--that time so many midsummer ago when she have tell thias where this linen lay that he might be sure and reach it out for she when she die for she be the elder of the two
then there have be the work of cleanse to the strict purity every object in the sacred chamber and of remove from it every trace of common daily occupation
the small window which have hitherto freely let in the frosty moonlight or the warm summer sunrise on the work man s slumber must now be darken with a fair white sheet for this be the sleep which be as sacred under the bare rafter as in ceile house
lisbeth have even mend a long-neglect and unnoticeable rent in the checkered bit of bed-curtain for the moment be few and precious now in which she would be able to do the small office of respect or love for the still corpse to which in all her thought she attribute some consciousness
our dead be never dead to we until we have forget they they can be injure by we they can be wound they know all our penitence all our aching sense that their place be empty all the kiss we bestow on the small relic of their presence
and the aged peasant woman most of all believe that her dead be conscious
decent burial be what lisbeth have be think of for herself through year of thrift with an indistinct expectation that she should know when she be be carry to the churchyard follow by her husband and her son and now she feel as if the great work of her life be to be do in see that thias be bury decently before she--under the white thorn where once in a dream she have think she lie in the coffin yet all the while see the sunshine above and smell the white blossom that be so thick upon the thorn the sunday she go to be church after adam be bear
but now she have do everything that could be do to-day in the chamber of death--have do it all herself with some aid from her son in lifting for she would let no one be fetched to help she from the village not be fond of female neighbour generally and her favourite dolly the old housekeeper at mr
burge s who have come to condole with she in the morning as soon as she hear of thias s death be too dim-sighted to be of much use
she have lock the door and now hold the key in her hand as she throw herself wearily into a chair that stand out of its place in the middle of the house floor where in ordinary time she would never have consent to sit

but what at another time would have be intolerable to lisbeth s habit of order and cleanliness seem to she now just what should be it be right that thing should look strange and disorder and wretche now the old man have come to his end in that sad way the kitchen ought not to look as if nothing have happen
adam overcome with the agitation and exertion of the day after his night of hard work have fall asleep on a bench in the workshop and seth be in the back kitchen make a fire of stick that he might get the kettle to boil and persuade his mother to have a cup of tea an indulgence which she rarely allow herself
there be no one in the kitchen when lisbeth enter and throw herself into the chair
she look round with blank eye at the dirt and confusion on which the bright afternoon s sun shine dismally it be all of a piece with the sad confusion of her mind--that confusion which belong to the first hour of a sudden sorrow when the poor human soul be like one who have be deposit sleep among the ruin of a vast city and wake up in dreary amazement not know whether it be the growing or the die day--not know why and whence come this illimitable scene of desolation or why he too find himself desolate in the midst of it
at another time lisbeth s first thought would have be where be adam but the sudden death of her husband have restore he in these hour to that first place in her affection which he have hold six-and-twenty year ago
she have forget his fault as we forget the sorrow of our depart childhood and think of nothing but the young husband s kindness and the old man s patience

chapter xi in the cottage it be but half-past four the next morning when dinah tired of lie awake listening to the bird and watch the grow light through the little window in the garret roof rise and begin to dress herself very quietly lest she should disturb lisbeth

therebe nothing but whatbe bearable as long as a man can work he say to himself the natur o thing donot change though it seem as if one s own life be nothing but change

he have just go into the workshop when his quick ear detect a light rapid foot on the stair--certainly not his mother s

his hearty affection for the rector date from the age of frock and trouser

it would be ridiculous to be pry and analytic in such case as if one be inquire into the character of a confidential clerk

if he should not come

but this little thing would be speak ill of directly if she happen to be see walk with he and then those excellent people the poysers to whom a good name be as precious as if they have the good blood in the land in their vein--he should hate himself if he make a scandal of that sort on the estate that be to be his own some day and among tenant by whom he like above all to be respect
he could no more believe that he should so fall in his own esteem than that he should break both his leg and go on crutch all the rest of his life
he couldnot imagine himself in that position it be too odious too unlike he
and even if no one know anything about it they might get too fond of each other and then there could be nothing but the misery of parting after all
no gentleman out of a ballad could marry a farmer s niece
there must be an end to the whole thing at once
it be too foolish
and yet he have be so determined this morning before he go to gawaine s and while he be there something have take hold of he and make he gallop back

there be but one resource
he would go and tell irwine--tell he everything
the mere act of tell it would make it seem trivial the temptation would vanish as the charm of fond word vanish when one repeat they to the indifferent
in every way it would help he to tell irwine
he would ride to broxton rectory the first thing after breakfast to-morrow

he feel sure he should sleep now he have have enough to tire he and there be no more need for he to think

it be well know that great scholar who have show the most pitiless acerbity in their criticism of other man s scholarship have yet be of a relenting and indulgent temper in private life and i have hear of a learn man meekly rock the twin in the cradle with his left hand while with his right he inflict the most lacerate sarcasm on an opponent who have betray a brutal ignorance of hebrew
weakness and error must be forgive--alas
they be not alien to we--but the man who take the wrong side on the momentous subject of the hebrew point must be treat as the enemy of his race
there be the same sort of antithetic mixture in martin poyser he be of so excellent a disposition that he have be kind and more respectful than ever to his old father since he have make a deed of gift of all his property and no man judge his neighbour more charitably on all personal matter but for a farmer like luke britton for example whose fallow be not well clean who donot know the rudiment of hedging and ditching and show but a small share of judgment in the purchase of winter stock martin poyser be as hard and implacable as the north-east wind

she could see quite well the peg in the old paint linen-press on which she hang her hat and gown she could see the head of every pin on her red cloth pin-cushion she could see a reflection of herself in the old-fashioned look-glass quite as distinct as be needful consider that she have only to brush her hair and put on her night-cap
a queer old look-glass
hetty get into an ill temper with it almost every time she dress
it have be consider a handsome glass in its day and have probably be buy into the poyser family a quarter of a century before at a sale of genteel household furniture
even now an auctioneer could say something for it it have a great deal of tarnished gilding about it it have a firm mahogany base well supply with drawer which open with a decided jerk and send the content leap out from the farth corner without give you the trouble of reach they above all it have a brass candle-socket on each side which would give it an aristocratic air to the very last
but hetty object to it because it have numerous dim blotch sprinkle over the mirror which no rubbing would remove and because instead of swing backwards and forwards it be fix in an upright position so that she could only get one good view of her head and neck and that be to be have only by sit down on a low chair before her dressing-table
and the dressing-table be no dressing-table at all but a small old chest of drawer the most awkward thing in the world to sit down before for the big brass handle quite hurt her knee and she couldnot get near the glass at all comfortably
but devout worshipper never allow inconvenience to prevent they from perform their religious rite and hetty this evening be more bent on her peculiar form of worship than usual
have take off her gown and white kerchief she draw a key from the large pocket that hang outside her petticoat and unlock one of the low drawer in the chest reach from it two short bit of wax candle--secretly buy at treddleston--and stick they in the two brass socket

it be into this small glass that she choose to look first after seat herself

chapter xvi link arthur donnithorne you remember be under an engagement with himself to go and see mr
irwine this friday morning and he be awake and dress so early that he determine to go before breakfast instead of after
the rector he know breakfast alone at half-past nine the lady of the family have a different breakfast-hour arthur will have an early ride over the hill and breakfast with he
one can say everything good over a meal
the progress of civilization have make a breakfast or a dinner an easy and cheerful substitute for more troublesome and disagreeable ceremony
we take a less gloomy view of our error now our father confessor listen to we over his egg and coffee
we be more distinctly conscious that rude penance be out of the question for gentleman in an enlightened age and that mortal sin be not incompatible with an appetite for muffin
an assault on our pocket which in more barbarous time would have be make in the brusque form of a pistol-shot be quite a well-breed and smile procedure now it have become a request for a loan throw in as an easy parenthesis between the second and third glass of claret
still there be this advantage in the old rigid form that they commit you to the fulfilment of a resolution by some outward deed when you have put your mouth to one end of a hole in a stone wall and be aware that there be an expectant ear at the other end you be more likely to say what you come out with the intention of say than if you be seat with your leg in an easy attitude under the mahogany with a companion who will have no reason to be surprised if you have nothing particular to say
however arthur donnithorne as he wind among the pleasant lane on horseback in the morning sunshine have a sincere determination to open his heart to the rector and the swirl sound of the scythe as he pass by the meadow be all the pleasanter to he because of this honest purpose

a man about town might perhaps consider that these influence be not to be feel out of a child s story-book but when you be among the field and hedgerow it be impossible to maintain a consistent superiority to simple natural pleasure

book two chapter xvii in which the story pause a little this rector of broxton be little well than a pagan i hear one of my reader exclaim

you might have put into his mouth the most beautiful thing--quite as good as read a sermon certainly i could if i hold it the high vocation of the novelist to represent thing as they never have be and never will be
then of course i might refashion life and character entirely after my own liking i might select the most unexceptionable type of clergyman and put my own admirable opinion into his mouth on all occasion
but it happen on the contrary that my strong effort be to avoid any such arbitrary picture and to give a faithful account of man and thing as they have mirror themselves in my mind

sixty year ago--it be a long time so no wonder thing have change--all clergyman be not zealous indeed there be reason to believe that the number of zealous clergyman be small and it be probable that if one among the small minority have own the living of broxton and hayslope in the year 1799 you would have like he no well than you like mr

the world be not just what we like do touch it up with a tasteful pencil and make believe it be not quite such a mixed entangle affair
let all people who hold unexceptionable opinion act unexceptionably
let your most faulty character always be on the wrong side and your virtuous one on the right
then we shall see at a glance whom we be to condemn and whom we be to approve
then we shall be able to admire without the slight disturbance of our prepossession we shall hate and despise with that true ruminant relish which belong to undoubting confidence but my good friend what will you do then with your fellow-parishioner who oppose your husband in the vestry
with your newly appoint vicar whose style of preach you find painfully below that of his regretted predecessor

with your neighbour mrs

these fellow-mortal every one must be accept as they be you can neither straighten their nose nor brighten their wit nor rectify their disposition and it be these people--amongst whom your life be pass--that it be needful you should tolerate pity and love it be these more or less ugly stupid inconsistent people whose movement of goodness you should be able to admire--for whom you should cherish all possible hope all possible patience
and i would not even if i have the choice be the clever novelist who could create a world so much well than this in which we get up in the morning to do our daily work that you would be likely to turn a hard cold eye on the dusty street and the common green field--on the real breathing man and woman who can be chill by your indifference or injure by your prejudice who can be cheer and help onward by your fellow-feeling your forbearance your outspoken brave justice
so i be content to tell my simple story without try to make thing seem well than they be dread nothing indeed but falsity which in spite of one s good effort there be reason to dread
falsehood be so easy truth so difficult
the pencil be conscious of a delightful facility in draw a griffin--the long the claw and the large the wing the well but that marvellous facility which we mistook for genius be apt to forsake we when we want to draw a real unexaggerated lion
examine your word well and you will find that even when you have no motive to be false it be a very hard thing to say the exact truth even about your own immediate feeling--much hard than to say something fine about they which be not the exact truth
it be for this rare precious quality of truthfulness that i delight in many dutch painting which lofty-minded people despise
i find a source of delicious sympathy in these faithful picture of a monotonous homely existence which have be the fate of so many more among my fellow-mortal than a life of pomp or of absolute indigence of tragic suffering or of world-stir action
i turn without shrink from cloud-bear angel from prophet sibyls and heroic warrior to an old woman bend over her flower-pot or eat her solitary dinner while the noonday light soften perhaps by a screen of leaf fall on her mob-cap and just touch the rim of her spinning-wheel and her stone jug and all those cheap common thing which be the precious necessary of life to she--or i turn to that village wedding keep between four brown wall where an awkward bridegroom open the dance with a high-shoulder broad-faced bride while elderly and middle-aged friend look on with very irregular nose and lip and probably with quart-pot in their hand but with an expression of unmistakable contentment and goodwill

yes
thank god human feeling be like the mighty river that bless the earth it do not wait for beauty--it flow with resistless force and bring beauty with it
all honour and reverence to the divine beauty of form
let we cultivate it to the utmost in man woman and child--in our garden and in our house
but let we love that other beauty too which lie in no secret of proportion but in the secret of deep human sympathy
paint we an angel if you can with a float violet robe and a face pal by the celestial light paint we yet oftener a madonna turn her mild face upward and open her arm to welcome the divine glory but do not impose on we any aesthetic rule which shall banish from the region of art those old woman scrap carrot with their work-wear hand those heavy clown take holiday in a dingy pot-house those rounded back and stupid weather-beat face that have bend over the spade and do the rough work of the world--those home with their tin pan their brown pitcher their rough cur and their cluster of onion
in this world there be so many of these common coarse people who have no picturesque sentimental wretchedness
it be so needful we should remember their existence else we may happen to leave they quite out of our religion and philosophy and frame lofty theory which only fit a world of extreme
therefore let art always remind we of they therefore let we always have man ready to give the love pain of a life to the faithful representing of commonplace thing--man who see beauty in these commonplace thing and delight in show how kindly the light of heaven fall on they
there be few prophet in the world few sublimely beautiful woman few hero
i cannot afford to give all my love and reverence to such rarity i want a great deal of those feeling for my every-day fellow-man especially for the few in the foreground of the great multitude whose face i know whose hand i touch for whom i have to make way with kindly courtesy
neither be picturesque lazzaroni or romantic criminal half so frequent as your common labourer who get his own bread and eat it vulgarly but creditably with his own pocket-knife
it be more needful that i should have a fibre of sympathy connect i with that vulgar citizen who weigh out my sugar in a vilely assorted cravat and waistcoat than with the handsome rascal in red scarf and green feather--more needful that my heart should swell with love admiration at some trait of gentle goodness in the faulty people who sit at the same hearth with i or in the clergyman of my own parish who be perhaps rather too corpulent and in other respect be not an oberlin or a tillotson than at the deed of hero whom i shall never know except by hearsay or at the sublime abstract of all clerical grace that be ever conceive by an able novelist

human converse i think some wise man have remark be not rigidly sincere
but i herewith discharge my conscience and declare that i have have quite enthusiastic movement of admiration towards old gentleman who speak the bad english who be occasionally fretful in their temper and who have never move in a high sphere of influence than that of parish overseer and that the way in which i have come to the conclusion that human nature be lovable--the way i have learn something of its deep pathos its sublime mystery--have be by live a great deal among people more or less commonplace and vulgar of whom you would perhaps hear nothing very surprising if you be to inquire about they in the neighbourhood where they dwelt
ten to one most of the small shopkeeper in their vicinity see nothing at all in they
for i have observe this remarkable coincidence that the select nature who pant after the ideal and find nothing in pantaloon or petticoat great enough to command their reverence and love be curiously in unison with the narrow and pettiest
for example i have often hear mr
gedge the landlord of the royal oak who use to turn a bloodshot eye on his neighbour in the village of shepperton sum up his opinion of the people in his own parish--and they be all the people he know--in these emphatic word aye sir ihave say it often and iwill say it again theybe a poor lot i this parish--a poor lot sir big and little i think he have a dim idea that if he could migrate to a distant parish he might find neighbour worthy of he and indeed he do subsequently transfer himself to the saracen s head which be do a thriving business in the back street of a neighbouring market-town
but oddly enough he have find the people up that back street of precisely the same stamp as the inhabitant of shepperton-- a poor lot sir big and little and they as come for a go o gin be no well than they as come for a pint o twopenny--a poor lot chapter xviii church hetty hetty donot you know church begin at two and itbe go half after one a ready
have you get nothing well to think on this good sunday as poor old thias bede s to be put into the ground and he drownde i th dead o the night as itbe enough to make one s back run cold but you must be dizen yourself as if there be a wedding i stid of a funeral well aunt say hetty i cannot be ready so soon as everybody else when ihave get totty s thing to put on
and iwould ever such work to make her stand still hetty be come downstairs and mrs
poyser in her plain bonnet and shawl be stand below
if ever a girl look as if she have be make of rose that girl be hetty in her sunday hat and frock
for her hat be trim with pink and her frock have pink spot sprinkle on a white ground
there be nothing but pink and white about she except in her dark hair and eye and her little buckled shoe

poyser be provoke at herself for she could hardly keep from smile as any mortal be inclined to do at the sight of pretty round thing
so she turn without speak and join the group outside the house door follow by hetty whose heart be flutter so at the thought of some one she expect to see at church that she hardly feel the ground she trod on
and now the little procession set off
mr
poyser be in his sunday suit of drab with a red-and-green waistcoat and a green watch-ribbon have a large cornelian seal attach pendant like a plumb-line from that promontory where his watch-pocket be situate a silk handkerchief of a yellow tone round his neck and excellent grey rib stocking knit by mrs
poyser s own hand set off the proportion of his leg
mr
poyser have no reason to be ashamed of his leg and suspect that the grow abuse of top-boot and other fashion tend to disguise the nether limb have their origin in a pitiable degeneracy of the human calf
still less have he reason to be ashamed of his round jolly face which be good humour itself as he say come hetty--come little un and give his arm to his wife lead the way through the causeway gate into the yard
the little uns address be marty and tommy boy of nine and seven in little fustian tail coat and knee-breech relieve by rosy cheek and black eye look as much like their father as a very small elephant be like a very large one
hetty walk between they and behind come patient molly whose task it be to carry totty through the yard and over all the wet place on the road for totty have speedily recover from her threaten fever have insist on go to church to-day and especially on wear her red-and-black necklace outside her tippet
and there be many wet place for she to be carry over this afternoon for there have be heavy shower in the morning though now the cloud have roll off and lie in tower silvery mass on the horizon
you might have know it be sunday if you have only wake up in the farmyard
the cock and hen seem to know it and make only croon subdue noise the very bull-dog look less savage as if he would have be satisfied with a small bite than usual
the sunshine seem to call all thing to rest and not to labour
it be asleep itself on the moss-grow cow-shed on the group of white duck nestle together with their bill tuck under their wing on the old black sow stretch languidly on the straw while her large young one find an excellent spring-bed on his mother s fat rib on alick the shepherd in his new smock-frock take an uneasy siesta half-sit half-stand on the granary step
alick be of opinion that church like other luxury be not to be indulge in often by a foreman who have the weather and the ewe on his mind
church
nay--i n get summat else to think on be an answer which he often utter in a tone of bitter significance that silence further question
i feel sure alick mean no irreverence indeed i know that his mind be not of a speculative negative cast and he would on no account have miss go to church on christmas day easter sunday and whissuntide but he have a general impression that public worship and religious ceremony like other non-productive employment be intend for people who have leisure
therebe father a-standing at the yard-gate say martin poyser
i reckon he want to watch we down the field
itbe wonderful what sight he have and he turn seventy-five ah i often think itbe wi th old folk as it be wi the babbie say mrs

itbe god a mighty s way o quieten them i reckon afore they go to sleep old martin open the gate as he see the family procession approach and hold it wide open lean on his stick--please to do this bit of work for like all old man whose life have be spend in labour he like to feel that he be still useful--that there be a well crop of onion in the garden because he be by at the sowing--and that the cow would be milk the well if he stay at home on a sunday afternoon to look on
he always go to church on sacrament sundays but not very regularly at other time on wet sundays or whenever he have a touch of rheumatism he use to read the three first chapter of genesis instead
theywill ha putten thias bede i the ground afore ye get to the churchyard he say as his son come up

for the hedgerow in those day shut out one s view even on the well-manage farm and this afternoon the dog-rose be toss out their pink wreath the nightshade be in its yellow and purple glory the pale honeysuckle grow out of reach peep high up out of a holly bush and over all an ash or a sycamore every now and then throw its shadow across the path

the woman who manage a dairy have a large share in make the rent so she may well be allow to have her opinion on stock and their keep an exercise which strengthen her understanding so much that she find herself able to give her husband advice on most other subject
therebe that shorthorne sally she say as they enter the home close and she catch sight of the meek beast that lie chew the cud and look at she with a sleepy eye

poyser they like the shorthorn as give such a lot o milk
therebe chowne s wife want he to buy no other sort whatbe it sinnify what chowne s wife like
a poor soft thing wi no more head-piece nor a sparrow
shewould take a big cullender to strain her lard wi and then wonder as the scratchin s run through
ihave see enough of she to know as iwill niver take a servant from her house again--all hugger-mugger--and youwould niver know when you go in whether it be monday or friday the wash draggin on to th end o the week and as for her cheese i know well enough it rise like a loaf in a tin last year
and then she talk o the weather bein i fault as therebe folk ud stand on their head and then say the fault be i their boot well chowne s be want to buy sally so we can get rid of she if thee lik st say mr

therebe chowne s wife ugly enough to turn the milk an save the rennet but shewill niver save nothing any other way

she provoke i past bearing sometimes and as i tell she she go clean again the scriptur for that say love your neighbour as yourself but i say if you love your neighbour no well nor you do yourself dinah itbe little enough youwould do for he

the damp hay that must be scatter and turn afresh to-morrow be not a cheer sight to mr
poyser who during hay and corn harvest have often some mental struggle as to the benefit of a day of rest but no temptation would have induce he to carry on any field-work however early in the morning on a sunday for have not michael holdsworth have a pair of oxen swelter while he be plough on good friday
that be a demonstration that work on sacred day be a wicked thing and with wickedness of any sort martin poyser be quite clear that he would have nothing to do since money get by such mean would never prosper
it a most make your finger itch to be at the hay now the sun shin so he observe as they pass through the big meadow but itbe poor foolishness to think o saving by go against your conscience
therebe that jim wakefield as they use to call gentleman wakefield use to do the same of a sunday as o weekday and take no heed to right or wrong as if there be nayth god nor devil

why i see he myself last market-day a-carry a basket wi orange in t ah to be sure say mrs

the money as be get sobe like to burn hole i your pocket
iwould niver wish we to leave our lad a sixpence but what be get i the rightful way
and as for the weather therebe one above make it and we must put up wi t itbe nothing of a plague to what the wench be notwithstanding the interruption in their walk the excellent habit which mrs
poyser s clock have of take time by the forelock have secure their arrival at the village while it be still a quarter to two though almost every one who mean to go to church be already within the churchyard gate
those who stay at home be chiefly mother like timothy s bess who stand at her own door nurse her baby and feeling as woman feel in that position--that nothing else can be expect of they
it be not entirely to see thias bede s funeral that the people be stand about the churchyard so long before service begin that be their common practice
the woman indeed usually enter the church at once and the farmer wife talk in an undertone to each other over the tall pew about their illness and the total failure of doctor s stuff recommend dandelion-tea and other home-make specific as far preferable--about the servant and their grow exorbitance as to wage whereas the quality of their service decline from year to year and there be no girl nowadays to be trust any far than you could see she--about the bad price mr
dingall the treddleston grocer be give for butter and the reasonable doubt that might be hold as to his solvency notwithstanding that mrs
dingall be a sensible woman and they be all sorry for she for she have very good kin
meantime the man linger outside and hardly any of they except the singer who have a humming and fragmentary rehearsal to go through enter the church until mr
irwine be in the desk
they see no reason for that premature entrance--what could they do in church if they be there before service began and they do not conceive that any power in the universe could take it ill of they if they stay out and talk a little about bus ness chad cranage look like quite a new acquaintance to-day for he have get his clean sunday face which always make his little granddaughter cry at he as a stranger
but an experienced eye would have fix on he at once as the village blacksmith after see the humble deference with which the big saucy fellow take off his hat and stroke his hair to the farmer for chad be accustom to say that a work-man must hold a candle to a personage understand to be as black as he be himself on weekday by which evil-sound rule of conduct he mean what be after all rather virtuous than otherwise namely that man who have horse to be shod must be treat with respect
chad and the rougher sort of workman keep aloof from the grave under the white thorn where the burial be go forward but sandy jim and several of the farm-labourer make a group round it and stand with their hat off as fellow-mourner with the mother and son
other hold a midway position sometimes watch the group at the grave sometimes listen to the conversation of the farmer who stand in a knot near the church door and be now join by martin poyser while his family pass into the church
on the outside of this knot stand mr
casson the landlord of the donnithorne arms in his most striking attitude--that be to say with the forefinger of his right hand thrust between the button of his waistcoat his left hand in his breech pocket and his head very much on one side look on the whole like an actor who have only a mono-syllabic part entrust to he but feel sure that the audience discern his fitness for the lead business curiously in contrast with old jonathan burge who hold his hand behind he and lean forward cough asthmatically with an inward scorn of all knowingness that could not be turn into cash
the talk be in rather a low tone than usual to-day hush a little by the sound of mr
irwine s voice read the final prayer of the burial-service
they have all have their word of pity for poor thias but now they have get upon the nearer subject of their own grievance against satchell the squire s bailiff who play the part of steward so far as it be not perform by old mr
donnithorne himself for that gentleman have the meanness to receive his own rent and make bargain about his own timber
this subject of conversation be an additional reason for not be loud since satchell himself might presently be walk up the paved road to the church door
and soon they become suddenly silent for mr
irwine s voice have cease and the group round the white thorn be disperse itself towards the church
they all move aside and stand with their hat off while mr
irwine pass
adam and seth be come next with their mother between they for joshua rann officiate as head sexton as well as clerk and be not yet ready to follow the rector into the vestry
but there be a pause before the three mourner come on lisbeth have turn round to look again towards the grave
ah
there be nothing now but the brown earth under the white thorn
yet she cry less to-day than she have do any day since her husband s death
along with all her grief there be mixed an unusual sense of her own importance in have a burial and in mr
irwine s read a special service for her husband and besides she know the funeral psalm be go to be sing for he
she feel this counter-excitement to her sorrow still more strongly as she walk with her son towards the church door and see the friendly sympathetic nod of their fellow-parishioner
the mother and son pass into the church and one by one the loiterer follow though some still linger without the sight of mr
donnithorne s carriage which be wind slowly up the hill perhaps help to make they feel that there be no need for haste
but presently the sound of the bassoon and the key-bugle burst forth the evening hymn which always open the service have begin and every one must now enter and take his place
i cannot say that the interior of hayslope church be remarkable for anything except for the grey age of its oaken pew--great square pew mostly range on each side of a narrow aisle
it be free indeed from the modern blemish of gallery
the choir have two narrow pew to themselves in the middle of the right-hand row so that it be a short process for joshua rann to take his place among they as principal bass and return to his desk after the singing be over
the pulpit and desk grey and old as the pew stand on one side of the arch lead into the chancel which also have its grey square pew for mr
donnithorne s family and servant
yet i assure you these grey pew with the buff-wash wall give a very pleasing tone to this shabby interior and agree extremely well with the ruddy face and bright waistcoat
and there be liberal touch of crimson toward the chancel for the pulpit and mr
donnithorne s own pew have handsome crimson cloth cushion and to close the vista there be a crimson altar-cloth embroider with golden ray by miss lydia s own hand
but even without the crimson cloth the effect must have be warm and cheer when mr
irwine be in the desk look benignly round on that simple congregation--on the hardy old man with bent knee and shoulder perhaps but with vigour leave for much hedge-clip and thatch on the tall stalwart frame and roughly cut bronzed face of the stone-cutter and carpenter on the half-dozen well-to-do farmer with their apple-cheek family and on the clean old woman mostly farm-labourer wife with their bit of snow-white cap-border under their black bonnet and with their wither arm bare from the elbow fold passively over their chest
for none of the old people hold book--why should they
not one of they could read
but they know a few good word by heart and their withered lip now and then move silently follow the service without any very clear comprehension indeed but with a simple faith in its efficacy to ward off harm and bring blessing
and now all face be visible for all be stand up--the little child on the seat peep over the edge of the grey pew while good bishop ken s evening hymn be be sing to one of those lively psalm-tune which die out with the last generation of rector and choral parish clerk
melody die out like the pipe of pan with the ear that love they and listen for they
adam be not in his usual place among the singer to-day for he sit with his mother and seth and he notice with surprise that bartle massey be absent too--all the more agreeable for mr
joshua rann who give out his bass note with unusual complacency and throw an extra ray of severity into the glance he send over his spectacle at the recusant will maskery
i beseech you to imagine mr
irwine look round on this scene in his ample white surplice that become he so well with his powdered hair throw back his rich brown complexion and his finely cut nostril and upper lip for there be a certain virtue in that benignant yet keen countenance as there be in all human face from which a generous soul beam out
and over all stream the delicious june sunshine through the old window with their desultory patch of yellow red and blue that throw pleasant touch of colour on the opposite wall
i think as mr
irwine look round to-day his eye rest an instant long than usual on the square pew occupy by martin poyser and his family
and there be another pair of dark eye that find it impossible not to wander thither and rest on that round pink-and-white figure
but hetty be at that moment quite careless of any glance--she be absorb in the thought that arthur donnithorne would soon be come into church for the carriage must surely be at the church-gate by this time
she have never see he since she part with he in the wood on thursday evening and oh how long the time have seem
thing have go on just the same as ever since that evening the wonder that have happen then have bring no change after they they be already like a dream
when she hear the church door swing her heart beat so she dare not look up
she feel that her aunt be curtsy she curtsy herself
that must be old mr
donnithorne--he always come first the wrinkle small old man peer round with short-sighted glance at the bowing and curtsying congregation then she know miss lydia be pass and though hetty like so much to look at her fashionable little coal-scuttle bonnet with the wreath of small rose round it she donot mind it to-day
but there be no more curtsy--no he be not come she feel sure there be nothing else pass the pew door but the house-keeper s black bonnet and the lady s maid s beautiful straw hat that have once be miss lydia s and then the powdered head of the butler and footman
no he be not there yet she would look now--she might be mistake--for after all she have not look
so she lift up her eyelid and glance timidly at the cushion pew in the chancel--there be no one but old mr
donnithorne rub his spectacle with his white handkerchief and miss lydia open the large gilt-edge prayer-book
the chill disappointment be too hard to bear
she feel herself turn pale her lip tremble she be ready to cry
oh what should she do
everybody would know the reason they would know she be cry because arthur be not there
and mr
craig with the wonderful hothouse plant in his button-hole be star at she she know
it be dreadfully long before the general confession begin so that she could kneel down
two great drop would fall then but no one see they except good-natured molly for her aunt and uncle kneel with their back towards she
molly unable to imagine any cause for tear in church except faintness of which she have a vague traditional knowledge draw out of her pocket a queer little flat blue smell-bottle and after much labour in pull the cork out thrust the narrow neck against hetty s nostril
it donna smell she whisper think this be a great advantage which old salt have over fresh one they do you good without bite your nose
hetty push it away peevishly but this little flash of temper do what the salt could not have do--it rouse she to wipe away the trace of her tear and try with all her might not to shed any more
hetty have a certain strength in her vain little nature she would have bear anything rather than be laugh at or point at with any other feeling than admiration she would have press her own nail into her tender flesh rather than people should know a secret she do not want they to know
what fluctuation there be in her busy thought and feeling while mr
irwine be pronounce the solemn absolution in her deaf ear and through all the tone of petition that follow
anger lie very close to disappointment and soon win the victory over the conjecture her small ingenuity could devise to account for arthur s absence on the supposition that he really want to come really want to see she again
and by the time she rise from her knee mechanically because all the rest be rise the colour have return to her cheek even with a heighten glow for she be frame little indignant speech to herself say she hat arthur for give she this pain--she would like he to suffer too
yet while this selfish tumult be go on in her soul her eye be bent down on her prayer-book and the eyelid with their dark fringe look as lovely as ever
adam bede think so as he glance at she for a moment on rise from his knee
but adam s thought of hetty do not deafen he to the service they rather blend with all the other deep feeling for which the church service be a channel to he this afternoon as a certain consciousness of our entire past and our imagined future blend itself with all our moment of keen sensibility
and to adam the church service be the good channel he could have find for his mingled regret yearning and resignation its interchange of beseech cry for help with outburst of faith and praise its recurrent response and the familiar rhythm of its collect seem to speak for he as no other form of worship could have do as to those early christians who have worship from their childhood upwards in catacomb the torch-light and shadow must have seem near the divine presence than the heathenish daylight of the street
the secret of our emotion never lie in the bare object but in its subtle relation to our own past no wonder the secret escape the unsympathize observer who might as well put on his spectacle to discern odour
but there be one reason why even a chance comer would have find the service in hayslope church more impressive than in most other village nook in the kingdom--a reason of which i be sure you have not the slight suspicion
it be the reading of our friend joshua rann
where that good shoemaker get his notion of reading from remain a mystery even to his most intimate acquaintance
i believe after all he get it chiefly from nature who have pour some of her music into this honest conceited soul as she have be know to do into other narrow soul before his
she have give he at least a fine bass voice and a musical ear but i cannot positively say whether these alone have suffice to inspire he with the rich chant in which he deliver the response
the way he roll from a rich deep forte into a melancholy cadence subsiding at the end of the last word into a sort of faint resonance like the linger vibration of a fine violoncello i can compare to nothing for its strong calm melancholy but the rush and cadence of the wind among the autumn bough
this may seem a strange mode of speak about the reading of a parish clerk--a man in rusty spectacle with stubbly hair a large occiput and a prominent crown
but that be nature s way she will allow a gentleman of splendid physiognomy and poetic aspiration to sing woefully out of tune and not give he the slight hint of it and take care that some narrow-browed fellow troll a ballad in the corner of a pot-house shall be as true to his interval as a bird
joshua himself be less proud of his reading than of his singing and it be always with a sense of heighten importance that he pass from the desk to the choir
still more to-day it be a special occasion for an old man familiar to all the parish have die a sad death--not in his bed a circumstance the most painful to the mind of the peasant--and now the funeral psalm be to be sing in memory of his sudden departure
moreover bartle massey be not at church and joshua s importance in the choir suffer no eclipse
it be a solemn minor strain they sing
the old psalm-tune have many a wail among they and the words-- thou sweep st we off as with a flood we vanish hence like dreams-- seem to have a close application than usual in the death of poor thias
the mother and son listen each with peculiar feeling
lisbeth have a vague belief that the psalm be do her husband good it be part of that decent burial which she would have think it a great wrong to withhold from he than to have cause he many unhappy day while he be live
the more there be say about her husband the more there be do for he surely the safe he would be
it be poor lisbeth s blind way of feel that human love and pity be a ground of faith in some other love
seth who be easily touch shed tear and try to recall as he have do continually since his father s death all that he have hear of the possibility that a single moment of consciousness at the last might be a moment of pardon and reconcilement for be it not write in the very psalm they be sing that the divine dealing be not measure and circumscribe by time
adam have never be unable to join in a psalm before
he have know plenty of trouble and vexation since he have be a lad but this be the first sorrow that have hem in his voice and strangely enough it be sorrow because the chief source of his past trouble and vexation be for ever go out of his reach
he have not be able to press his father s hand before their parting and say father you know it be all right between we i never forget what i owe you when i be a lad you forgive i if i have be too hot and hasty now and then adam think but little to-day of the hard work and the earning he have spend on his father his thought run constantly on what the old man s feeling have be in moment of humiliation when he have hold down his head before the rebuke of his son
when our indignation be bear in submissive silence we be apt to feel twinge of doubt afterwards as to our own generosity if not justice how much more when the object of our anger have go into everlasting silence and we have see his face for the last time in the meekness of death
ah
i be always too hard adam say to himself
itbe a sore fault in i as ibe so hot and out o patience with people when they do wrong and my heart get shut up against them so as i cannot bring myself to forgive them
i see clear enough therebe more pride nor love in my soul for i could soon make a thousand stroke with th hammer for my father than bring myself to say a kind word to he
and there go plenty o pride and temper to the stroke as the devil will be have his finger in what we call our duty as well as our sin
mayhap the good thing i ever do in my life be only do what be easy for myself
itbe allay be easy for i to work nor to sit still but the real tough job for i ud be to master my own will and temper and go right against my own pride
it seem to i now if i be to find father at home to-night i should behave different but therebe no knowing--perhaps nothing ud be a lesson to we if it donot come too late
itbe well we should feel as lifebe a reckoning we cannot make twice over therebe no real making amend in this world any more nor you can mend a wrong subtraction by do your addition right this be the key-note to which adam s thought have perpetually return since his father s death and the solemn wail of the funeral psalm be only an influence that bring back the old thought with strong emphasis
so be the sermon which mr
irwine have choose with reference to thias s funeral
it speak briefly and simply of the word in the midst of life we be in death how the present moment be all we can call our own for work of mercy of righteous dealing and of family tenderness
all very old truth--but what we think the old truth become the most startling to we in the week when we have look on the dead face of one who have make a part of our own life
for when man want to impress we with the effect of a new and wonderfully vivid light do they not let it fall on the most familiar object that we may measure its intensity by remember the former dimness
then come the moment of the final blessing when the forever sublime word the peace of god which passeth all understanding seem to blend with the calm afternoon sunshine that fall on the bow head of the congregation and then the quiet rising the mother tie on the bonnet of the little maiden who have sleep through the sermon the father collect the prayer-book until all stream out through the old archway into the green churchyard and begin their neighbourly talk their simple civility and their invitation to tea for on a sunday every one be ready to receive a guest--it be the day when all must be in their good clothe and their good humour
