chapter xix adam on a working day notwithstanding mr
craig s prophecy the dark-blue cloud disperse itself without have produce the threaten consequence
the weather as he observe the next morning-- the weather you see be a ticklish thing an a fool ull hit on t sometimes when a wise man miss thatbe why the almaneck get so much credit
itbe one o they chancy thing as fool thrive on this unreasonable behaviour of the weather however could displease no one else in hayslope besides mr
craig
all hand be to be out in the meadow this morning as soon as the dew have rise the wife and daughter do double work in every farmhouse that the maid might give their help in toss the hay and when adam be march along the lane with his basket of tool over his shoulder he catch the sound of jocose talk and ringing laughter from behind the hedge
the jocose talk of hay-maker be good at a distance like those clumsy bell round the cow neck it have rather a coarse sound when it come close and may even grate on your ear painfully but hear from far off it mingle very prettily with the other joyous sound of nature
man s muscle move well when their soul be make merry music though their merriment be of a poor blundering sort not at all like the merriment of bird
and perhaps there be no time in a summer s day more cheering than when the warmth of the sun be just begin to triumph over the freshness of the morning--when there be just a lingering hint of early coolness to keep off languor under the delicious influence of warmth
the reason adam be walk along the lane at this time be because his work for the rest of the day lie at a country-house about three mile off which be be put in repair for the son of a neighbouring squire and he have be busy since early morning with the packing of panel door and chimney-piece in a waggon which be now go on before he while jonathan burge himself have ride to the spot on horseback to await its arrival and direct the workman
this little walk be a rest to adam and he be unconsciously under the charm of the moment
it be summer morning in his heart and he see hetty in the sunshine--a sunshine without glare with slant ray that tremble between the delicate shadow of the leaf
he think yesterday when he put out his hand to she as they come out of church that there be a touch of melancholy kindness in her face such as he have not see before and he take it as a sign that she have some sympathy with his family trouble
poor fellow
that touch of melancholy come from quite another source but how be he to know
we look at the one little woman s face we love as we look at the face of our mother earth and see all sort of answer to our own yearning
it be impossible for adam not to feel that what have happen in the last week have bring the prospect of marriage near to he
hitherto he have feel keenly the danger that some other man might step in and get possession of hetty s heart and hand while he himself be still in a position that make he shrink from ask she to accept he
even if he have have a strong hope that she be fond of he--and his hope be far from be strong--he have be too heavily burden with other claim to provide a home for himself and hetty--a home such as he could expect she to be content with after the comfort and plenty of the farm
like all strong nature adam have confidence in his ability to achieve something in the future he feel sure he should some day if he live be able to maintain a family and make a good broad path for himself but he have too cool a head not to estimate to the full the obstacle that be to be overcome
and the time would be so long
and there be hetty like a bright-cheek apple hang over the orchard wall within sight of everybody and everybody must long for she
to be sure if she love he very much she would be content to wait for he but do she love he
his hope have never rise so high that he have dare to ask she
he be clear-sight enough to be aware that her uncle and aunt would have look kindly on his suit and indeed without this encouragement he would never have persevere in go to the farm but it be impossible to come to any but fluctuate conclusion about hetty s feeling
she be like a kitten and have the same distractingly pretty look that mean nothing for everybody that come near she
but now he could not help say to himself that the heavy part of his burden be remove and that even before the end of another year his circumstance might be bring into a shape that would allow he to think of marry
it would always be a hard struggle with his mother he know she would be jealous of any wife he might choose and she have set her mind especially against hetty--perhaps for no other reason than that she suspect hetty to be the woman he have choose
it would never do he fear for his mother to live in the same house with he when he be marry and yet how hard she would think it if he ask she to leave he
yes there be a great deal of pain to be go through with his mother but it be a case in which he must make she feel that his will be strong--it would be well for she in the end
for himself he would have like that they should all live together till seth be married and they might have build a bit themselves to the old house and make more room
he do not like to part wi th lad they have hardly every be separate for more than a day since they be bear
but adam have no soon catch his imagination leap forward in this way--make arrangement for an uncertain future--than he check himself
a pretty building ibe make without either brick or timber
ibe up i the garret a ready and havenot so much as dig the foundation whenever adam be strongly convinced of any proposition it take the form of a principle in his mind it be knowledge to be act on as much as the knowledge that damp will cause rust
perhaps here lay the secret of the hardness he have accuse himself of he have too little fellow-feeling with the weakness that err in spite of foreseen consequence
without this fellow-feeling how be we to get enough patience and charity towards our stumbling fall companion in the long and changeful journey
and there be but one way in which a strong determined soul can learn it--by get his heart-string bind round the weak and err so that he must share not only the outward consequence of their error but their inward suffering
that be a long and hard lesson and adam have at present only learn the alphabet of it in his father s sudden death which by annihilate in an instant all that have stimulate his indignation have send a sudden rush of thought and memory over what have claim his pity and tenderness
but it be adam s strength not its correlative hardness that influence his meditation this morning
he have long make up his mind that it would be wrong as well as foolish for he to marry a blooming young girl so long as he have no other prospect than that of grow poverty with a grow family
and his saving have be so constantly draw upon besides the terrible sweep of pay for seth s substitute in the militia that he have not enough money beforehand to furnish even a small cottage and keep something in reserve against a rainy day
he have good hope that he should be firm on his leg by and by but he could not be satisfied with a vague confidence in his arm and brain he must have definite plan and set about they at once
the partnership with jonathan burge be not to be think of at present--there be thing implicitly tack to it that he could not accept but adam think that he and seth might carry on a little business for themselves in addition to their journeyman s work by buy a small stock of superior wood and make article of household furniture for which adam have no end of contrivance
seth might gain more by work at separate job under adam s direction than by his journeyman s work and adam in his overhour could do all the nice work that require peculiar skill
the money gain in this way with the good wage he receive as foreman would soon enable they to get beforehand with the world so sparingly as they would all live now
no soon have this little plan shape itself in his mind than he begin to be busy with exact calculation about the wood to be buy and the particular article of furniture that should be undertake first--a kitchen cupboard of his own contrivance with such an ingenious arrangement of slide-door and bolt such convenient nook for stow household provender and such a symmetrical result to the eye that every good housewife would be in rapture with it and fall through all the gradation of melancholy longing till her husband promise to buy it for she
adam picture to himself mrs
poyser examine it with her keen eye and try in vain to find out a deficiency and of course close to mrs
poyser stand hetty and adam be again beguile from calculation and contrivance into dream and hope
yes he would go and see she this evening--it be so long since he have be at the hall farm
he would have like to go to the night-school to see why bartle massey have not be at church yesterday for he fear his old friend be ill but unless he could manage both visit this last must be put off till to-morrow--the desire to be near hetty and to speak to she again be too strong
as he make up his mind to this he be come very near to the end of his walk within the sound of the hammer at work on the refitting of the old house
the sound of tool to a clever workman who love his work be like the tentative sound of the orchestra to the violinist who have to bear his part in the overture the strong fibre begin their accustomed thrill and what be a moment before joy vexation or ambition begin its change into energy
all passion become strength when it have an outlet from the narrow limit of our personal lot in the labour of our right arm the cunning of our right hand or the still creative activity of our thought
look at adam through the rest of the day as he stand on the scaffolding with the two-foot ruler in his hand whistle low while he consider how a difficulty about a floor-joist or a window-frame be to be overcome or as he push one of the young workman aside and take his place in upheave a weight of timber say let alone lad
thee st get too much gristle i thy bone yet or as he fix his keen black eye on the motion of a workman on the other side of the room and warn he that his distance be not right
look at this broad-shoulder man with the bare muscular arm and the thick firm black hair toss about like trodden meadow-grass whenever he take off his paper cap and with the strong barytone voice burst every now and then into loud and solemn psalm-tune as if seek an outlet for superfluous strength yet presently check himself apparently cross by some thought which jar with the singing
perhaps if you have not be already in the secret you might not have guess what sad memory what warm affection what tender flutter hope have their home in this athletic body with the break finger-nail--in this rough man who know no well lyric than he could find in the old and new version and an occasional hymn who know the small possible amount of profane history and for whom the motion and shape of the earth the course of the sun and the change of the season lie in the region of mystery just make visible by fragmentary knowledge
it have cost adam a great deal of trouble and work in overhour to know what he know over and above the secret of his handicraft and that acquaintance with mechanic and figure and the nature of the material he work with which be make easy to he by inborn inherit faculty--to get the mastery of his pen and write a plain hand to spell without any other mistake than must in fairness be attribute to the unreasonable character of orthography rather than to any deficiency in the speller and moreover to learn his musical note and part-singing
besides all this he have read his bible include the apocryphal book poor richard s almanac taylor s holy living and dying the pilgrim s progress with bunyan s life and holy war a great deal of bailey s dictionary valentine and orson and part of a history of babylon which bartle massey have lend he
he might have have many more book from bartle massey but he have no time for read the commin print as lisbeth call it so busy as he be with figure in all the leisure moment which he do not fill up with extra carpentry
adam you perceive be by no mean a marvellous man nor properly speaking a genius yet i will not pretend that his be an ordinary character among workman and it would not be at all a safe conclusion that the next good man you may happen to see with a basket of tool over his shoulder and a paper cap on his head have the strong conscience and the strong sense the blend susceptibility and self-command of our friend adam
he be not an average man
yet such man as he be rear here and there in every generation of our peasant artisan--with an inheritance of affection nurture by a simple family life of common need and common industry and an inheritance of faculty train in skilful courageous labour they make their way upwards rarely as genius most commonly as painstaking honest man with the skill and conscience to do well the task that lie before they
their life have no discernible echo beyond the neighbourhood where they dwelt but you be almost sure to find there some good piece of road some building some application of mineral produce some improvement in farming practice some reform of parish abuse with which their name be associate by one or two generation after they
their employer be the rich for they the work of their hand have wear well and the work of their brain have guide well the hand of other man
they go about in their youth in flannel or paper cap in coat black with coal-dust or streak with lime and red paint in old age their white hair be see in a place of honour at church and at market and they tell their well-dress son and daughter seat round the bright hearth on winter evening how pleased they be when they first earn their twopence a-day
other there be who die poor and never put off the workman s coal on weekday
they have not have the art of get rich but they be man of trust and when they die before the work be all out of they it be as if some main screw have get loose in a machine the master who employ they say where shall i find their like chapter xx adam visit the hall farm adam come back from his work in the empty waggon--that be why he have change his clothe--and be ready to set out to the hall farm when it still want a quarter to seven

i war go thirty an th feyther too afore we war marry an young enough too

but he guess where mrs

in that leafy flowery bushy time to look for any one in this garden be like play at hide-and-seek there be the tall hollyhock begin to flower and dazzle the eye with their pink white and yellow there be the syringas and guelder rose all large and disorderly for want of trimming there be leafy wall of scarlet bean and late pea there be a row of bushy filbert in one direction and in another a huge apple-tree make a barren circle under its low-spread bough
but what signify a barren patch or two
the garden be so large
there be always a superfluity of broad bean--it take nine or ten of adam s stride to get to the end of the uncut grass walk that run by the side of they and as for other vegetable there be so much more room than be necessary for they that in the rotation of crop a large flourish bed of groundsel be of yearly occurrence on one spot or other
the very rose-tree at which adam stop to pluck one look as if they grow wild they be all huddle together in bushy mass now flaunt with wide-open petal almost all of they of the streaked pink-and-white kind which doubtless date from the union of the house of york and lancaster
adam be wise enough to choose a compact provence rise that peep out half-smother by its flaunting scentless neighbour and hold it in his hand--he think he should be more at ease hold something in his hand--as he walk on to the far end of the garden where he remember there be the large row of currant-tree not far off from the great yew-tree arbour
but he have not go many step beyond the rose when he hear the shaking of a bough and a boy s voice say now then totty hold out your pinny--therebe a duck the voice come from the bough of a tall cherry-tree where adam have no difficulty in discern a small blue-pinafore figure perch in a commodious position where the fruit be thick
doubtless totty be below behind the screen of pea
yes--with her bonnet hang down her back and her fat face dreadfully smear with red juice turn up towards the cherry-tree while she hold her little round hole of a mouth and her red-stain pinafore to receive the promise downfall
i be sorry to say more than half the cherry that fall be hard and yellow instead of juicy and red but totty spend no time in useless regret and she be already suck the third juiciest when adam say there now totty youhave get your cherry
run into the house with them to mother--she want you--shebe in the dairy

tommy my lad take care yoube not shoot for a little thieving bird say adam as he walk on towards the currant-tree
he could see there be a large basket at the end of the row hetty would not be far off and adam already feel as if she be look at he

strange that she have not hear he come
perhaps it be because she be make the leave rustle

that blush make his heart beat with a new happiness

i frighten you he say with a delicious sense that it donot signify what he say since hetty seem to feel as much as he do let i pick the currant up that be soon do for they have only fall in a tangled mass on the grass-plot and adam as he rise and give she the basin again look straight into her eye with the subdue tenderness that belong to the first moment of hopeful love
hetty do not turn away her eye her blush have subside and she meet his glance with a quiet sadness which content adam because it be so unlike anything he have see in she before
therebe not many more currant to get she say i shall soon ha do now iwill help you say adam and he fetch the large basket which be nearly full of currant and set it close to they
not a word more be speak as they gather the currant

she be not indifferent to his presence after all she have blush when she see he and then there be that touch of sadness about she which must surely mean love since it be the opposite of her usual manner which have often impress he as indifference
and he could glance at she continually as she bend over the fruit while the level evening sunbeam steal through the thick apple-tree bough and rest on her round cheek and neck as if they too be in love with she
it be to adam the time that a man can least forget in after-life the time when he believe that the first woman he have ever love betray by a slight something--a word a tone a glance the quivering of a lip or an eyelid--that she be at least begin to love he in return
the sign be so slight it be scarcely perceptible to the ear or eye--he could describe it to no one--it be a mere feather-touch yet it seem to have change his whole being to have merge an uneasy yearning into a delicious unconsciousness of everything but the present moment
so much of our early gladness vanishe utterly from our memory we can never recall the joy with which we lay our head on our mother s bosom or ride on our father s back in childhood
doubtless that joy be work up into our nature as the sunlight of long-past morning be work up in the soft mellowness of the apricot but it be go for ever from our imagination and we can only believe in the joy of childhood
but the first glad moment in our first love be a vision which return to we to the last and bring with it a thrill of feel intense and special as the recurrent sensation of a sweet odour breathe in a far-off hour of happiness
it be a memory that give a more exquisite touch to tenderness that feed the madness of jealousy and add the last keenness to the agony of despair
hetty bend over the red bunch the level ray pierce the screen of apple-tree bough the length of bushy garden beyond his own emotion as he look at she and believe that she be think of he and that there be no need for they to talk--adam remember it all to the last moment of his life
and hetty
you know quite well that adam be mistake about she
like many other man he think the sign of love for another be sign of love towards himself
when adam be approach unseen by she she be absorb as usual in think and wonder about arthur s possible return
the sound of any man s footstep would have affect she just in the same way--she would have felt it might be arthur before she have time to see and the blood that forsook her cheek in the agitation of that momentary feeling would have rush back again at the sight of any one else just as much as at the sight of adam
he be not wrong in think that a change have come over hetty the anxiety and fear of a first passion with which she be tremble have become strong than vanity have give she for the first time that sense of helpless dependence on another s feeling which awaken the clinging deprecate womanhood even in the shallow girl that can ever experience it and create in her a sensibility to kindness which find she quite hard before
for the first time hetty feel that there be something soothing to she in adam s timid yet manly tenderness
she want to be treat lovingly--oh it be very hard to bear this blank of absence silence apparent indifference after those moment of glow love
she be not afraid that adam would tease she with love-making and flattering speech like her other admirer he have always be so reserved to she she could enjoy without any fear the sense that this strong brave man love she and be near she
it never enter into her mind that adam be pitiable too--that adam too must suffer one day
hetty we know be not the first woman that have behave more gently to the man who love she in vain because she have herself begin to love another
it be a very old story but adam know nothing about it so he drink in the sweet delusion

chapter xxi the night-school and the schoolmaster bartle massey s be one of a few scatter house on the edge of a common which be divide by the road to treddleston
adam reach it in a quarter of an hour after leave the hall farm and when he have his hand on the door-latch he could see through the curtainless window that there be eight or nine head bend over the desk light by thin dip
when he enter a reading lesson be go forward and bartle massey merely nod leave he to take his place where he please
he have not come for the sake of a lesson to-night and his mind be too full of personal matter too full of the last two hour he have pass in hetty s presence for he to amuse himself with a book till school be over so he sit down in a corner and look on with an absent mind
it be a sort of scene which adam have behold almost weekly for year he know by heart every arabesque flourish in the framed speciman of bartle massey s handwriting which hang over the schoolmaster s head by way of keep a lofty ideal before the mind of his pupil he know the back of all the book on the shelf run along the whitewashed wall above the peg for the slate he know exactly how many grain be go out of the ear of indian corn that hang from one of the rafter he have long ago exhaust the resource of his imagination in try to think how the bunch of leathery seaweed have look and grow in its native element and from the place where he sit he could make nothing of the old map of england that hang against the opposite wall for age have turn it of a fine yellow brown something like that of a well-season meerschaum
the drama that be go on be almost as familiar as the scene nevertheless habit have not make he indifferent to it and even in his present self-absorb mood adam feel a momentary stirring of the old fellow-feeling as he look at the rough man painfully hold pen or pencil with their cramp hand or humbly labour through their reading lesson
the reading class now seat on the form in front of the schoolmaster s desk consist of the three most backward pupil
adam would have know it only by see bartle massey s face as he look over his spectacle which he have shift to the ridge of his nose not require they for present purpose
the face wear its mild expression the grizzle bushy eyebrow have take their more acute angle of compassionate kindness and the mouth habitually compress with a pout of the low lip be relax so as to be ready to speak a helpful word or syllable in a moment
this gentle expression be the more interesting because the schoolmaster s nose an irregular aquiline twist a little on one side have rather a formidable character and his brow moreover have that peculiar tension which always impress one as a sign of a keen impatient temperament the blue vein stand out like cord under the transparent yellow skin and this intimidating brow be soften by no tendency to baldness for the grey bristly hair cut down to about an inch in length stand round it in as close rank as ever
nay bill nay bartle be say in a kind tone as he nod to adam begin that again and then perhaps itwill come to you what d-r-y spell
itbe the same lesson you read last week you know bill be a sturdy fellow aged four-and-twenty an excellent stone-sawyer who could get as good wage as any man in the trade of his year but he find a reading lesson in word of one syllable a hard matter to deal with than the hard stone he have ever have to saw
the letter he complain be so uncommon alike there be no tellin them one from another the sawyer s business not be concern with minute difference such as exist between a letter with its tail turn up and a letter with its tail turn down
but bill have a firm determination that he would learn to read found chiefly on two reason first that tom hazelow his cousin could read anything right off whether it be print or writing and tom have send he a letter from twenty mile off say how he be prosper in the world and have get an overlooker s place secondly that sam phillips who saw with he have learn to read when he be turn twenty and what could be do by a little fellow like sam phillips bill consider could be do by himself see that he could pound sam into wet clay if circumstance require it
so here he be point his big finger towards three word at once and turn his head on one side that he might keep well hold with his eye of the one word which be to be discriminate out of the group
the amount of knowledge bartle massey must possess be something so dim and vast that bill s imagination recoil before it he would hardly have venture to deny that the schoolmaster might have something to do in bring about the regular return of daylight and the change in the weather
the man seat next to bill be of a very different type he be a methodist brickmaker who after spend thirty year of his life in perfect satisfaction with his ignorance have lately get religion and along with it the desire to read the bible
but with he too learn be a heavy business and on his way out to-night he have offer as usual a special prayer for help see that he have undertake this hard task with a single eye to the nourishment of his soul--that he might have a great abundance of text and hymn wherewith to banish evil memory and the temptation of old habit--or in brief language the devil
for the brickmaker have be a notorious poacher and be suspect though there be no good evidence against he of be the man who have shoot a neighbouring gamekeeper in the leg
however that might be it be certain that shortly after the accident refer to which be coincident with the arrival of an awakening methodist preacher at treddleston a great change have be observe in the brickmaker and though he be still know in the neighbourhood by his old sobriquet of brimstone there be nothing he hold in so much horror as any further transaction with that evil-smell element
he be a broad-cheste fellow with a fervid temperament which help he well in imbibe religious idea than in the dry process of acquire the mere human knowledge of the alphabet
indeed he have be already a little shaken in his resolution by a brother methodist who assure he that the letter be a mere obstruction to the spirit and express a fear that brimstone be too eager for the knowledge that puffeth up
the third beginner be a much more promising pupil
he be a tall but thin and wiry man nearly as old as brimstone with a very pale face and hand stain a deep blue
he be a dyer who in the course of dipping homespun wool and old woman s petticoat have get fire with the ambition to learn a great deal more about the strange secret of colour
he have already a high reputation in the district for his dye and he be bent on discover some method by which he could reduce the expense of crimson and scarlet
the druggist at treddleston have give he a notion that he might save himself a great deal of labour and expense if he could learn to read and so he have begin to give his spare hour to the night-school resolve that his little chap should lose no time in come to mr
massey s day-school as soon as he be old enough
it be touch to see these three big man with the mark of their hard labour about they anxiously bend over the worn book and painfully make out the grass be green the stick be dry the corn be ripe a very hard lesson to pass to after column of single word all alike except in the first letter
it be almost as if three rough animal be make humble effort to learn how they might become human
and it touch the tender fibre in bartle massey s nature for such full-grow child as these be the only pupil for whom he have no severe epithet and no impatient tone

now you see you donot do this thing a bit well than you do a fortnight ago and iwill tell you whatbe the reason
you want to learn account--thatbe well and good

you go whistle about and take no more care what yoube think of than if your head be gutter for any rubbish to swill through that happen to be in the way and if you get a good notion in them itbe pretty soon wash out again

you may say to yourself ibe one fool and jackbe another if my fool s head weigh four pound and jack s three pound three ounce and three quarter how many pennyweight heavy would my head be than jack s a man that have get his heart in learn figure would make sum for himself and work them in his head
when he sit at his shoemaking hewould count his stitch by five and then put a price on his stitch say half a farthing and then see how much money he could get in an hour and then ask himself how much money hewould get in a day at that rate and then how much ten workman would get work three or twenty or a hundred year at that rate--and all the while his needle would be go just as fast as if he leave his head empty for the devil to dance in
but the long and the short of it be--i ll have nobody in my night-school that donot strive to learn what he come to learn as hard as if he be strive to get out of a dark hole into broad daylight
iwill send no man away because hebe stupid if billy taft the idiot want to learn anything iwould not refuse to teach he

no rain have fall for the last three or four day and the weather be perfect for that time of the year there be less dust than usual on the dark-green hedge-row and on the wild camomile that star the roadside yet the grass be dry enough for the little child to roll on it and there be no cloud but a long dash of light downy ripple high high up in the far-off blue sky
perfect weather for an outdoor july merry-making yet surely not the good time of year to be bear in
nature seem to make a hot pause just then all the lovely flower be go the sweet time of early growth and vague hope be past and yet the time of harvest and ingathere be not come and we tremble at the possible storm that may ruin the precious fruit in the moment of its ripeness
the wood be all one dark monotonous green the waggon-load of hay no long creep along the lane scatter their sweet-smell fragment on the blackberry branch the pasture be often a little tan yet the corn have not get its last splendour of red and gold the lamb and calf have lose all trace of their innocent frisky prettiness and have become stupid young sheep and cow
but it be a time of leisure on the farm--that pause between hay-and corn-harvest and so the farmer and labourer in hayslope and broxton think the captain do well to come of age just then when they could give their undivided mind to the flavour of the great cask of ale which have be brew the autumn after the heir be bear and be to be tap on his twenty-first birthday
the air have be merry with the ringing of church-bell very early this morning and every one have make haste to get through the needful work before twelve when it would be time to think of get ready to go to the chase
the midday sun be stream into hetty s bedchamber and there be no blind to temper the heat with which it fall on her head as she look at herself in the old speck glass
still that be the only glass she have in which she could see her neck and arm for the small hanging glass she have fetch out of the next room--the room that have be dinah s--would show she nothing below her little chin and that beautiful bit of neck where the roundness of her cheek melt into another roundness shadow by dark delicate curl
and to-day she think more than usual about her neck and arm for at the dance this evening she be not to wear any neckerchief and she have be busy yesterday with she spot pink-and-white frock that she might make the sleeve either long or short at will
she be dress now just as she be to be in the evening with a tucker make of real lace which she aunt have lend she for this unparalleled occasion but with no ornament besides she have even take out her small round ear-ring which she wear every day
but there be something more to be do apparently before she put on her neckerchief and long sleeve which she be to wear in the day-time for now she unlock the drawer that hold her private treasure
it be more than a month since we see her unlock that drawer before and now it hold new treasure so much more precious than the old one that these be thrust into the corner
hetty would not care to put the large coloured glass ear-ring into her ear now for see
she have get a beautiful pair of gold and pearl and garnet lie snugly in a pretty little box line with white satin
oh the delight of take out that little box and look at the ear-ring
do not reason about it my philosphical reader and say that hetty be very pretty must have know that it do not signify whether she have on any ornament or not and that moreover to look at ear-ring which she could not possibly wear out of her bedroom could hardly be a satisfaction the essence of vanity be a reference to the impression produce on other you will never understand woman s nature if you be so excessively rational
try rather to divest yourself of all your rational prejudice as much as if you be study the psychology of a canary bird and only watch the movement of this pretty round creature as she turn her head on one side with an unconscious smile at the ear-ring nestle in the little box
ah you think it be for the sake of the person who have give they to she and her thought be go back now to the moment when they be put into her hand
no else why should she have care to have ear-ring rather than anything else
and i know that she have long for ear-ring from among all the ornament she could imagine
little little ear arthur have say pretend to pinch they one evening as hetty sit beside he on the grass without her hat
i wish i have some pretty ear-ring she say in a moment almost before she know what she be say--the wish lie so close to her lip it would flutter past they at the slight breath
and the next day--it be only last week--arthur have ride over to rosseter on purpose to buy they
that little wish so naively utter seem to he the prettiest bit of childishness he have never hear anything like it before and he have wrap the box up in a great many cover that he might see hetty unwrap it with grow curiosity till at last her eye flash back their new delight into his
no she be not think most of the giver when she smile at the ear-ring for now she be take they out of the box not to press they to her lip but to fasten they in her ear--only for one moment to see how pretty they look as she peep at they in the glass against the wall with first one position of the head and then another like a listening bird
it be impossible to be wise on the subject of ear-ring as one look at she what should those delicate pearl and crystal be make for if not for such ear
one cannot even find fault with the tiny round hole which they leave when they be take out perhaps water-nixie and such lovely thing without soul have these little round hole in their ear by nature ready to hang jewel in
and hetty must be one of they it be too painful to think that she be a woman with a woman s destiny before she--a woman spin in young ignorance a light web of folly and vain hope which may one day close round her and press upon she a rancorous poison garment change all at once her fluttering trivial butterfly sensation into a life of deep human anguish
but she cannot keep in the ear-ring long else she may make her uncle and aunt wait
she put they quickly into the box again and shut they up
some day she will be able to wear any ear-ring she like and already she live in an invisible world of brilliant costume shimmer gauze soft satin and velvet such as the lady s maid at the chase have show she in miss lydia s wardrobe
she feel the bracelet on her arm and tread on a soft carpet in front of a tall mirror
but she have one thing in the drawer which she can venture to wear to-day because she can hang it on the chain of dark-brown berry which she have be use to wear on grand day with a tiny flat scent-bottle at the end of it tuck inside her frock and she must put on her brown berry--her neck would look so unfinished without it
hetty be not quite as fond of the locket as of the ear-ring though it be a handsome large locket with enamel flower at the back and a beautiful gold border round the glass which show a light-brown slightly wave lock form a background for two little dark ring
she must keep it under her clothe and no one would see it
but hetty have another passion only a little less strong than her love of finery and that other passion make she like to wear the locket even hide in her bosom
she would always have wear it if she have dare to encounter her aunt s question about a ribbon round her neck
so now she slip it on along her chain of dark-brown berry and snap the chain round her neck
it be not a very long chain only allow the locket to hang a little way below the edge of her frock
and now she have nothing to do but to put on her long sleeve her new white gauze neckerchief and her straw hat trim with white to-day instead of the pink which have become rather fade under the july sun
that hat make the drop of bitterness in hetty s cup to-day for it be not quite new--everybody would see that it be a little tan against the white ribbon--and mary burge she feel sure would have a new hat or bonnet on
she look for consolation at her fine white cotton stocking they really be very nice indeed and she have give almost all her spare money for they
hetty s dream of the future could not make she insensible to triumph in the present
to be sure captain donnithorne love she so that he would never care about look at other people but then those other people donot know how he love she and she be not satisfied to appear shabby and insignificant in their eye even for a short space
the whole party be assemble in the house-place when hetty go down all of course in their sunday clothe and the bell have be ring so this morning in honour of the captain s twenty-first birthday and the work have all be get do so early that marty and tommy be not quite easy in their mind until their mother have assure they that go to church be not part of the day s festivity
mr
poyser have once suggest that the house should be shut up and leave to take care of itself for say he therebe no danger of anybody s breaking in--everybody ll be at the chase thieve an all
if we lock th house up all the man can go itbe a day they wonna see twice i their life but mrs
poyser answer with great decision i never leave the house to take care of itself since i be a missis and i never will
therebe be ill-look tramp enoo about the place this last week to carry off every ham an every spoon we n get and they all collogue together they tramp as itbe a mercy they hanna come and poison the dog and murder we all in our bed afore we know some friday night when we n get the money in th house to pay the man
and itbe like enough the tramp know where webe go as well as we do oursen for if old harry want any work do you may be sure hewill find the mean nonsense about murder we in our bed say mr

the covered cart without spring be stand ready to carry the whole family except the man-servant

all broxton and all hayslope be to be at the chase and make merry there in honour of th heir and the old man and woman who have never be so far down this side of the hill for the last twenty year be be bring from broxton and hayslope in one of the farmer s waggon at mr

it be the band of the benefit club which have muster in all its glory--that be to say in bright-blue scarf and blue favour and carry its banner with the motto let brotherly love continue encircle a picture of a stone-pit
the cart of course be not to enter the chase
every one must get down at the lodge and the vehicle must be send back

the house would have be nothing but a plain square mansion of queen anne s time but for the remnant of an old abbey to which it be unite at one end in much the same way as one may sometimes see a new farmhouse rise high and prim at the end of old and low farm-office
the fine old remnant stand a little backward and under the shadow of tall beech but the sun be now on the tall and more advanced front the blind be all down and the house seem asleep in the hot midday
it make hetty quite sad to look at it arthur must be somewhere in the back room with the grand company where he could not possibly know that she be come and she should not see he for a long long while--not till after dinner when they say he be to come up and make a speech

a very handsome young briton he look to-day in high spirit and a bright-blue frock-coat the high mode--his arm no long in a sling
so open-look and candid too but candid people have their secret and secret leave no line in young face

that be capital advice of your irwine about the dinner--to let they be as orderly and comfortable as possible and only for the tenant especially as i have only a limited sum after all for though my grandfather talk of a carte blanche he couldnot make up his mind to trust i when it come to the point never mind youwill give more pleasure in this quiet way say mr

in this sort of thing people be constantly confound liberality with riot and disorder
it sound very grand to say that so many sheep and oxen be roasted whole and everybody eat who like to come but in the end it generally happen that no one have have an enjoyable meal
if the people get a good dinner and a moderate quantity of ale in the middle of the day theywill be able to enjoy the game as the day cool
you cannot hinder some of they from get too much towards evening but drunkenness and darkness go well together than drunkenness and daylight well i hope there willnot be much of it
ihave keep the treddleston people away by have a feast for they in the town and ihave get casson and adam bede and some other good fellow to look to the giving out of ale in the booth and to take care thing donot go too far

what a capital thing it be that they save this piece of the old abbey say arthur

i believe the secret of it be he see from the first it would be a profitable plan but he have some particular dislike of adam to get over--and besides the fact that i propose a thing be generally a reason with he for reject it
therebe the most curious contradiction in my grandfather i know he mean to leave i all the money he have save and he be likely enough to have cut off poor aunt lydia who have be a slave to he all her life with only five hundred a-year for the sake of give i all the more and yet i sometimes think he positively hat i because ibe his heir
i believe if i be to break my neck he would feel it the great misfortune that could befall he and yet it seem a pleasure to he to make my life a series of petty annoyance ah my boy it be not only woman s love that be two greek word omit as old aeschylus call it

chapter xxiv the health-drinking when the dinner be over and the first draught from the great cask of birthday ale be bring up room be make for the broad mr

chapter xxv the games the great dance be not to begin until eight o clock but for any lad and lass who like to dance on the shady grass before then there be music always at hand--for be not the band of the benefit club capable of play excellent jig reel and hornpipe

pity it be not a board floor
then the rhythmic stamping of the thick shoe would have be well than any drum
that merry stamp that gracious nodding of the head that wave bestowal of the hand--where can we see they now
that simple dancing of well-cover matron lay aside for an hour the care of house and dairy remember but not affect youth not jealous but proud of the young maiden by their side--that holiday sprightliness of portly husband pay little compliment to their wife as if their courting day be come again--those lad and lass a little confused and awkward with their partner have nothing to say--it would be a pleasant variety to see all that sometimes instead of low dress and large skirt and scan glance explore costume and languid man in lacquer boot smile with double meaning
there be but one thing to mar martin poyser s pleasure in this dance it be that he be always in close contact with luke britton that slovenly farmer
he think of throw a little glaze coldness into his eye in the crossing of hand but then as miss irwine be opposite to he instead of the offensive luke he might freeze the wrong person

but hetty s face have a language that transcend she feeling
there be face which nature charge with a meaning and pathos not belong to the single human soul that flutter beneath they but speak the joy and sorrow of foregone generation--eye that tell of deep love which doubtless have be and be somewhere but not pair with these eye--perhaps pair with pale eye that can say nothing just as a national language may be instinct with poetry unfelt by the lip that use it
that look of hetty s oppress arthur with a dread which yet have something of a terrible unconfessed delight in it that she love he too well

for this last view of the matter must be the true one
how could hetty have an accepted lover quite unknown to he

and so adam go to bed comfort have weave for himself an ingenious web of probability--the sure screen a wise man can place between himself and the truth

the reaping of the wheat have begin in our north midland county of loamshire but the harvest be likely still to be retard by the heavy rain which be cause inundation and much damage throughout the country
from this last trouble the broxton and hayslope farmer on their pleasant upland and in their brook-water valley have not suffer and as i cannot pretend that they be such exceptional farmer as to love the general good well than their own you will infer that they be not in very low spirit about the rapid rise in the price of bread so long as there be hope of gather in their own corn undamaged and occasional day of sunshine and dry wind flatter this hope
the eighteenth of august be one of these day when the sunshine look brighter in all eye for the gloom that go before
grand mass of cloud be hurry across the blue and the great round hill behind the chase seem alive with their fly shadow the sun be hide for a moment and then shine out warm again like a recover joy the leaf still green be toss off the hedgerow tree by the wind around the farmhouse there be a sound of clapping door the apple fall in the orchard and the stray horse on the green side of the lane and on the common have their mane blow about their face
and yet the wind seem only part of the general gladness because the sun be shin
a merry day for the child who run and shout to see if they could top the wind with their voice and the grow-up people too be in good spirit incline to believe in yet fine day when the wind have fall
if only the corn be not ripe enough to be blow out of the husk and scatter as untimely seed
and yet a day on which a blighting sorrow may fall upon a man
for if it be true that nature at certain moment seem charge with a presentiment of one individual lot must it not also be true that she seem unmindful unconscious of another
for there be no hour that have not its birth of gladness and despair no morning brightness that do not bring new sickness to desolation as well as new force to genius and love
there be so many of we and our lot be so different what wonder that nature s mood be often in harsh contrast with the great crisis of our life
we be child of a large family and must learn as such child do not to expect that our hurt will be make much of--to be content with little nurture and caressing and help each other the more
it be a busy day with adam who of late have do almost double work for he be continue to act as foreman for jonathan burge until some satisfactory person could be find to supply his place and jonathan be slow to find that person
but he have do the extra work cheerfully for his hope be buoyant again about hetty
every time she have see he since the birthday she have seem to make an effort to behave all the more kindly to he that she might make he understand she have forgive his silence and coldness during the dance
he have never mention the locket to she again too happy that she smile at he--still happy because he observe in she a more subdue air something that he interpret as the growth of womanly tenderness and seriousness
ah he think again and again shebe only seventeen shewill be thoughtful enough after a while
and her aunt allay say how clever she be at the work
shewill make a wife as mother ll have no occasion to grumble at after all to be sure he have only see she at home twice since the birthday for one sunday when he be intend to go from church to the hall farm hetty have join the party of upper servant from the chase and have go home with they--almost as if she be inclined to encourage mr
craig
shebe takin too much likin to they folk i the house keeper s room mrs

but when he hasten to she she be very kind and ask he to go in again when he have take she to the yard gate

that be the last time he have see she but he mean to make leisure for go to the farm to-morrow
to-day he know be her day for go to the chase to sew with the lady s maid so he would get as much work do as possible this evening that the next might be clear
one piece of work that adam be superintend be some slight repair at the chase farm which have be hitherto occupy by satchell as bailiff but which it be now rumour that the old squire be go to let to a smart man in top-boot who have be see to ride over it one day
nothing but the desire to get a tenant could account for the squire s undertaking repair though the saturday-evening party at mr
casson s agree over their pipe that no man in his sens would take the chase farm unless there be a bit more ploughland lay to it
however that might be the repair be order to be execute with all dispatch and adam act for mr
burge be carry out the order with his usual energy
but to-day have be occupy elsewhere he have not be able to arrive at the chase farm till late in the afternoon and he then discover that some old roofing which he have calculate on preserve have give way
there be clearly no good to be do with this part of the building without pull it all down and adam immediately see in his mind a plan for build it up again so as to make the most convenient of cow-shed and calf-pen with a hovel for implement and all without any great expense for material
so when the workman be go he sit down take out his pocket-book and busy himself with sketch a plan and make a specification of the expense that he might show it to burge the next morning and set he on persuade the squire to consent
to make a good job of anything however small be always a pleasure to adam and he sit on a block with his book rest on a planing-table whistle low every now and then and turn his head on one side with a just perceptible smile of gratification--of pride too for if adam love a bit of good work he love also to think i do it and i believe the only people who be free from that weakness be those who have no work to call their own
it be nearly seven before he have finish and put on his jacket again and on give a last look round he observe that seth who have be work here to-day have leave his basket of tool behind he
why th lad s forget his tool think adam and hebe get to work up at the shop to-morrow
there never be such a chap for wool-gathering hewould leave his head behind he if it be loose
however itbe lucky ihave see them iwill carry them home the building of the chase farm lie at one extremity of the chase at about ten minute walking distance from the abbey
adam have come thither on his pony intend to ride to the stable and put up his nag on his way home
at the stable he encounter mr
craig who have come to look at the captain s new horse on which he be to ride away the day after to-morrow and mr
craig detain he to tell how all the servant be to collect at the gate of the courtyard to wish the young squire luck as he ride out so that by the time adam have get into the chase and be stride along with the basket of tool over his shoulder the sun be on the point of setting and be send level crimson ray among the great trunk of the old oak and touch every bare patch of ground with a transient glory that make it look like a jewel dropt upon the grass
the wind have fall now and there be only enough breeze to stir the delicate-stem leaf
any one who have be sit in the house all day would have be glad to walk now but adam have be quite enough in the open air to wish to shorten his way home and he bethink himself that he might do so by strike across the chase and go through the grove where he have never be for year
he hurry on across the chase stalk along the narrow path between the fern with gyp at his heel not linger to watch the magnificent change of the light--hardly once think of it--yet feel its presence in a certain calm happy awe which mingle itself with his busy work-day thought
how could he help feel it
the very deer feel it and be more timid
presently adam s thought recur to what mr
craig have say about arthur donnithorne and picture his go away and the change that might take place before he come back then they travel back affectionately over the old scene of boyish companionship and dwelt on arthur s good quality which adam have a pride in as we all have in the virtue of the superior who honour we
a nature like adam s with a great need of love and reverence in it depend for so much of its happiness on what it can believe and feel about other
and he have no ideal world of dead hero he know little of the life of man in the past he must find the being to whom he could cling with love admiration among those who come within speech of he
these pleasant thought about arthur bring a milder expression than usual into his keen rough face perhaps they be the reason why when he open the old green gate lead into the grove he pause to pat gyp and say a kind word to he
after that pause he stride on again along the broad winding path through the grove
what grand beech
adam delight in a fine tree of all thing as the fisherman s sight be keen on the sea so adam s perception be more at home with tree than with other object
he keep they in his memory as a painter do with all the fleck and knot in their bark all the curve and angle of their bough and have often calculate the height and content of a trunk to a nicety as he stand look at it
no wonder that not-withstand his desire to get on he could not help pause to look at a curious large beech which he have see stand before he at a turning in the road and convince himself that it be not two tree wed together but only one
for the rest of his life he remember that moment when he be calmly examine the beech as a man remember his last glimpse of the home where his youth be pass before the road turn and he see it no more
the beech stand at the last turning before the grove end in an archway of bough that let in the eastern light and as adam step away from the tree to continue his walk his eye fall on two figure about twenty yard before he
he remain as motionless as a statue and turn almost as pale
the two figure be stand opposite to each other with clasped hand about to part and while they be bend to kiss gyp who have be run among the brushwood come out catch sight of they and give a sharp bark
they separate with a start--one hurry through the gate out of the grove and the other turn round walk slowly with a sort of saunter towards adam who still stand transfix and pale clutch tight the stick with which he hold the basket of tool over his shoulder and look at the approach figure with eye in which amazement be fast turn to fierceness
arthur donnithorne look flushed and excited he have try to make unpleasant feeling more bearable by drink a little more wine than usual at dinner to-day and be still enough under its flattering influence to think more lightly of this unwished-for rencontre with adam than he would otherwise have do
after all adam be the good person who could have happen to see he and hetty together--he be a sensible fellow and would not babble about it to other people
arthur feel confident that he could laugh the thing off and explain it away
and so he saunter forward with elaborate carelessness--his flushed face his evening dress of fine cloth and fine linen his hand half-thrust into his waistcoat pocket all shine upon by the strange evening light which the light cloud have catch up even to the zenith and be now shed down between the topmost branch above he
adam be still motionless look at he as he come up
he understand it all now--the locket and everything else that have be doubtful to he a terrible scorch light show he the hide letter that change the meaning of the past
if he have move a muscle he must inevitably have spring upon arthur like a tiger and in the conflicting emotion that fill those long moment he have tell himself that he would not give loose to passion he would only speak the right thing
he stand as if petrify by an unseen force but the force be his own strong will
well adam say arthur youhave be look at the fine old beech eh
theybe not to be come near by the hatchet though this be a sacred grove
i overtake pretty little hetty sorrel as i be come to my den--the hermitage there
she ought not to come home this way so late
so i take care of she to the gate and ask for a kiss for my pain
but i must get back now for this road be confoundedly damp
good-night adam
i shall see you to-morrow--to say good-bye you know arthur be too much preoccupied with the part he be play himself to be thoroughly aware of the expression in adam s face
he do not look directly at adam but glance carelessly round at the tree and then lift up one foot to look at the sole of his boot
he care to say no more--he have throw quite dust enough into honest adam s eye--and as he speak the last word he walk on
stop a bit sir say adam in a hard peremptory voice without turn round
ihave get a word to say to you arthur pause in surprise
susceptible person be more affected by a change of tone than by unexpected word and arthur have the susceptibility of a nature at once affectionate and vain
he be still more surprised when he see that adam have not move but stand with his back to he as if summon he to return
what do he mean
he be go to make a serious business of this affair
arthur feel his temper rise
a patronising disposition always have its mean side and in the confusion of his irritation and alarm there enter the feeling that a man to whom he have show so much favour as to adam be not in a position to criticize his conduct
and yet he be dominate as one who feel himself in the wrong always be by the man whose good opinion he care for
in spite of pride and temper there be as much deprecation as anger in his voice when he say what do you mean adam i mean sir answere adam in the same harsh voice still without turn round-- i mean sir that you donot deceive i by your light word
this be not the first time youhave meet hetty sorrel in this grove and this be not the first time youhave kiss she arthur feel a startled uncertainty how far adam be speak from knowledge and how far from mere inference

that be a reflection that mar the console prospect

but what could a man do in such a dilemma
he be bind in honour to say no word that could injure hetty his first duty be to guard she
he would never have tell or act a lie on his own account
good god

pity that consequence be determine not by excuse but by action well the letter must be write it be the only mean that promise a solution of the difficulty

happily irwine and gawaine be come to dinner and by twelve o clock the next day he should have leave the chase mile behind he

for i feel it i feel it--infinite love be suffer too--yea in the fulness of knowledge it suffer it yearn it mourn and that be a blind self-seeking which want to be free from the sorrow wherewith the whole creation groaneth and travaileth
surely it be not true blessedness to be free from sorrow while there be sorrow and sin in the world sorrow be then a part of love and love do not seek to throw it off
it be not the spirit only that tell i this--i see it in the whole work and word of the gospel
be there not plead in heaven
be not the man of sorrows there in that crucify body wherewith he ascend

these thought have be much bear in on i of late and i have see with new clearness the meaning of those word if any man love i let he take up my cross i have hear this enlarge on as if it mean the trouble and persecution we bring on ourselves by confess jesus
but surely that be a narrow thought
the true cross of the redeemer be the sin and sorrow of this world--that be what lie heavy on his heart--and that be the cross we shall share with he that be the cup we must drink of with he if we would have any part in that divine love which be one with his sorrow
in my outward lot which you ask about i have all thing and abound

chapter xxxi in hetty s bed-chamber it be no long light enough to go to bed without a candle even in mrs

dearest hetty--i have speak truly when i have say that i love you and i shall never forget our love
i shall be your true friend as long as life last and i hope to prove this to you in many way
if i say anything to pain you in this letter do not believe it be for want of love and tenderness towards you for there be nothing i would not do for you if i know it to be really for your happiness
i cannot bear to think of my little hetty shed tear when i be not there to kiss they away and if i follow only my own inclination i should be with she at this moment instead of write
it be very hard for i to part from she--hard still for i to write word which may seem unkind though they spring from the true kindness
dear dear hetty sweet as our love have be to i sweet as it would be to i for you to love i always i feel that it would have be well for we both if we have never have that happiness and that it be my duty to ask you to love i and care for i as little as you can
the fault have all be mine for though i have be unable to resist the longing to be near you i have feel all the while that your affection for i might cause you grief
i ought to have resist my feeling
i should have do so if i have be a well fellow than i be but now since the past cannot be alter i be bind to save you from any evil that i have power to prevent
and i feel it would be a great evil for you if your affection continue so fixed on i that you could think of no other man who might be able to make you happy by his love than i ever can and if you continue to look towards something in the future which cannot possibly happen
for dear hetty if i be to do what you one day speak of and make you my wife i should do what you yourself would come to feel be for your misery instead of your welfare
i know you can never be happy except by marry a man in your own station and if i be to marry you now i should only be add to any wrong i have do besides offend against my duty in the other relation of life
you know nothing dear hetty of the world in which i must always live and you would soon begin to dislike i because there would be so little in which we should be alike
and since i cannot marry you we must part--we must try not to feel like lover any more
i be miserable while i say this but nothing else can be
be angry with i my sweet one i deserve it but do not believe that i shall not always care for you--always be grateful to you--always remember my hetty and if any trouble should come that we do not now foresee trust in i to do everything that lie in my power
i have tell you where you be to direct a letter to if you want to write but i put it down below lest you should have forget
do not write unless there be something i can really do for you for dear hetty we must try to think of each other as little as we can
forgive i and try to forget everything about i except that i shall be as long as i live your affectionate friend arthur donnithorne slowly hetty have read this letter and when she look up from it there be the reflection of a blanched face in the old dim glass--a white marble face with rounded childish form but with something sad than a child s pain in it
hetty do not see the face--she see nothing--she only feel that she be cold and sick and trembling
the letter shake and rustle in her hand
she lay it down
it be a horrible sensation--this cold and trembling
it sweep away the very idea that produce it and hetty get up to reach a warm cloak from her clothe-press wrap it round she and sit as if she be think of nothing but get warm
presently she take up the letter with a firm hand and begin to read it through again
the tear come this time--great rush tear that blind she and blotch the paper
she feel nothing but that arthur be cruel--cruel to write so cruel not to marry she
reason why he could not marry she have no existence for her mind how could she believe in any misery that could come to she from the fulfilment of all she have be long for and dream of
she have not the idea that could make up the notion of that misery
as she throw down the letter again she catch sight of her face in the glass it be redden now and wet with tear it be almost like a companion that she might complain to--that would pity she
she lean forward on her elbow and look into those dark overfloode eye and at the quivering mouth and see how the tear come thick and thick and how the mouth become convulse with sob
the shattering of all her little dream-world the crush blow on her new-bear passion afflict her pleasure-crave nature with an overpowering pain that annihilate all impulse to resistance and suspend her anger
she sit sob till the candle go out and then weary aching stupefy with crying throw herself on the bed without undressing and go to sleep
there be a feeble dawn in the room when hetty awoke a little after four o clock with a sense of dull misery the cause of which break upon she gradually as she begin to discern the object round she in the dim light
and then come the frightening thought that she have to conceal her misery as well as to bear it in this dreary daylight that be come
she could lie no long
she get up and go towards the table there lie the letter
she open her treasure-drawer there lie the ear-ring and the locket--the sign of all her short happiness--the sign of the lifelong dreariness that be to follow it
look at the little trinket which she have once eye and finger so fondly as the earnest of her future paradise of finery she live back in the moment when they have be give to she with such tender caress such strangely pretty word such glow look which fill she with a bewilder delicious surprise--they be so much sweet than she have think anything could be
and the arthur who have speak to she and look at she in this way who be present with she now--whose arm she feel round she his cheek against her his very breath upon she--be the cruel cruel arthur who have write that letter that letter which she snatch and crush and then open again that she might read it once more
the half-benumb mental condition which be the effect of the last night s violent crying make it necessary to she to look again and see if she wretched thought be actually true--if the letter be really so cruel
she have to hold it close to the window else she could not have read it by the faint light
yes
it be bad--it be more cruel
she crush it up again in anger
she hat the writer of that letter--hat he for the very reason that she hang upon he with all her love--all the girlish passion and vanity that make up her love
she have no tear this morning
she have weep they all away last night and now she feel that dry-eyed morning misery which be bad than the first shock because it have the future in it as well as the present
every morning to come as far as her imagination could stretch she would have to get up and feel that the day would have no joy for she
for there be no despair so absolute as that which come with the first moment of our first great sorrow when we have not yet know what it be to have suffer and be heal to have despair and to have recover hope
as hetty begin languidly to take off the clothe she have wear all the night that she might wash herself and brush her hair she have a sickening sense that her life would go on in this way
she should always be do thing she have no pleasure in get up to the old task of work see people she care nothing about go to church and to treddleston and to tea with mrs
best and carry no happy thought with she
for her short poisonous delight have spoil for ever all the little joy that have once make the sweetness of her life--the new frock ready for treddleston fair the party at mr
britton s at broxton wake the beaux that she would say no to for a long while and the prospect of the wedding that be to come at last when she would have a silk gown and a great many clothe all at once
these thing be all flat and dreary to she now everything would be a weariness and she would carry about for ever a hopeless thirst and longing
she pause in the midst of her languid undressing and lean against the dark old clothe-press
her neck and arm be bare her hair hang down in delicate ring--and they be just as beautiful as they be that night two month ago when she walk up and down this bed-chamber glowing with vanity and hope
she be not think of her neck and arm now even her own beauty be indifferent to she
her eye wander sadly over the dull old chamber and then look out vacantly towards the grow dawn
do a remembrance of dinah come across her mind
of her foreboding word which have make she angry
of dinah s affectionate entreaty to think of she as a friend in trouble
no the impression have be too slight to recur
any affection or comfort dinah could have give she would have be as indifferent to hetty this morning as everything else be except her bruised passion
she be only think she could never stay here and go on with the old life--she could well bear something quite new than sink back into the old everyday round
she would like to run away that very morning and never see any of the old face again
but hetty s be not a nature to face difficulty--to dare to loose her hold on the familiar and rush blindly on some unknown condition
hers be a luxurious and vain nature--not a passionate one--and if she be ever to take any violent measure she must be urge to it by the desperation of terror
there be not much room for her thought to travel in the narrow circle of her imagination and she soon fix on the one thing she would do to get away from her old life she would ask her uncle to let she go to be a lady s maid
miss lydia s maid would help she to get a situation if she krew hetty have her uncle s leave
when she have think of this she fasten up her hair and begin to wash it seem more possible to she to go downstairs and try to behave as usual
she would ask her uncle this very day
on hetty s blooming health it would take a great deal of such mental suffering as her to leave any deep impress and when she be dress as neatly as usual in her working-dress with her hair tuck up under her little cap an indifferent observer would have be more strike with the young roundness of her cheek and neck and the darkness of her eye and eyelash than with any sign of sadness about she
but when she take up the crushed letter and put it in her drawer that she might lock it out of sight hard smart tear have no relief in they as the great drop have that fall last night force their way into her eye
she wipe they away quickly she must not cry in the day-time
nobody should find out how miserable she be nobody should know she be disappoint about anything and the thought that the eye of her aunt and uncle would be upon she give she the self-command which often accompany a great dread
for hetty look out from her secret misery towards the possibility of their ever know what have happen as the sick and weary prisoner might think of the possible pillory
they would think she conduct shameful and shame be torture
that be poor little hetty s conscience
so she lock up her drawer and go away to her early work
in the evening when mr
poyser be smoke his pipe and his good-nature be therefore at its superlative moment hetty seize the opportunity of her aunt s absence to say uncle i wish youwould let i go for a lady s maid mr
poyser take the pipe from his mouth and look at hetty in mild surprise for some moment
she be sewing and go on with her work industriously
why whatbe put that into your head my wench he say at last after he have give one conservative puff
i should like it--i should like it well than farm-work nay nay you fancy so because you donna know it my wench
it wouldnot be half so good for your health nor for your luck i life
iwould like you to stay wi we till youhave get a good husband yoube my own niece and i wouldnot have you go to service though it be a gentleman s house as long as ihave get a home for you mr
poyser pause and puff away at his pipe
i like the needlework say hetty and i should get good wage have your aunt be a bit sharp wi you say mr
poyser not notice hetty s further argument
you mustna mind that my wench--she do it for your good
she wish you well an there benot many aunt as be no kin to you ud ha do by you as she have no it benot my aunt say hetty but i should like the work well it be all very well for you to learn the work a bit--an i gev my consent to that fast enough sin mrs
pomfret be willing to teach you
for if anything be t happen itbe well to know how to turn your hand to different sort o thing

you wouldna like your grand-child to take wage na-a-y say old martin with an elongation of the word mean to make it bitter as well as negative while he lean forward and look down on the floor
but the wench take arter her mother
iwould hard work t hould she in an she marry i spite o i--a feller wi on y two head o stock when there should ha be ten on s farm--she might well die o th inflammation afore she war thirty it be seldom the old man make so long a speech but his son s question have fall like a bit of dry fuel on the ember of a long unextinguished resentment which have always make the grandfather more indifferent to hetty than to his son s child
her mother s fortune have be spend by that good-for-nought sorrel and hetty have sorrel s blood in her vein
poor thing poor thing say martin the young who be sorry to have provoke this retrospective harshness
shewould but bad luck
but hetty s get as good a chance o get a solid sober husband as any gell i this country after throw out this pregnant hint mr

crying s for they as ha get no home not for they as want to get rid o one

think
why i think we shall have the fowl steal before we be much old wi that gell forget to lock the pen up o night
whatbe the matter now hetty
what be you cry at why shebe be want to go for a lady s maid say mr

i tell she we can do well for she nor that i think shewould get some maggot in her head shebe go about wi her mouth button up so all day

poyser soothingly

they young filly ull run away often wi ou know why her uncle s answer however have have another effect on hetty besides that of disappoint she and make her cry
she know quite well whom he have in his mind in his allusion to marriage and to a sober solid husband and when she be in her bedroom again the possibility of she marry adam present itself to she in a new light
in a mind where no strong sympathy be at work where there be no supreme sense of right to which the agitated nature can cling and steady itself to quiet endurance one of the first result of sorrow be a desperate vague clutch after any deed that will change the actual condition
poor hetty s vision of consequence at no time more than a narrow fantastic calculation of her own probable pleasure and pain be now quite shut out by reckless irritation under present suffering and she be ready for one of those convulsive motiveless action by which wretche man and woman leap from a temporary sorrow into a lifelong misery
why should she not marry adam
she do not care what she do so that it make some change in her life
she feel confident that he would still want to marry she and any further thought about adam s happiness in the matter have never yet visit she
strange perhaps you will say this rush of impulse to-ward a course that might have seem the most repugnant to her present state of mind and in only the second night of her sadness yes the action of a little trivial soul like hetty s struggle amidst the serious sad destiny of a human being be strange
so be the motion of a little vessel without ballast toss about on a stormy sea
how pretty it look with its parti-colour sail in the sunlight moor in the quiet bay
let that man bear the loss who loose it from its mooring but that will not save the vessel--the pretty thing that might have be a lasting joy
chapter xxxii mrs
poyser have she say out the next saturday evening there be much excited discussion at the donnithorne arm concern an incident which have occur that very day--no less than a second appearance of the smart man in top-boot say by some to be a mere farmer in treaty for the chase farm by other to be the future steward but by mr
