but in so complex a thing as human nature we must consider it be hard to find rule without exception
of course i know that as a rule sensible man fall in love with the most sensible woman of their acquaintance see through all the pretty deceit of coquettish beauty never imagine themselves love when they be not love cease love on all proper occasion and marry the woman most fit for they in every respect--indeed so as to compel the approbation of all the maiden lady in their neighbourhood
but even to this rule an exception will occur now and then in the lapse of century and my friend adam be one
for my own part however i respect he none the less--nay i think the deep love he have for that sweet rounded blossom-like dark-eye hetty of whose inward self he be really very ignorant come out of the very strength of his nature and not out of any inconsistent weakness
be it any weakness pray to be work on by exquisite music
to feel its wondrous harmony search the subtle winding of your soul the delicate fibre of life where no memory can penetrate and bind together your whole being past and present in one unspeakable vibration melt you in one moment with all the tenderness all the love that have be scatter through the toilsome year concentrate in one emotion of heroic courage or resignation all the hard-learn lesson of self-renounce sympathy blend your present joy with past sorrow and your present sorrow with all your past joy
if not then neither be it a weakness to be so wrought upon by the exquisite curve of a woman s cheek and neck and arm by the liquid depth of her beseech eye or the sweet childish pout of her lip
for the beauty of a lovely woman be like music what can one say more
beauty have an expression beyond and far above the one woman s soul that it clothe as the word of genius have a wide meaning than the thought that prompt they
it be more than a woman s love that move we in a woman s eye--it seem to be a far-off mighty love that have come near to we and make speech for itself there the rounded neck the dimpled arm move we by something more than their prettiness--by their close kinship with all we have know of tenderness and peace

whence i fear the tragedy of human life be likely to continue for a long time to come in spite of mental philosopher who be ready with the good receipt for avoid all mistake of the kind

so he give his hand to burge on that bargain and go home with his mind full of happy vision in which my refined reader will perhaps be shock when i say it the image of hetty hover and smile over plan for season timber at a trifling expense calculation as to the cheapening of brick per thousand by water-carriage and a favourite scheme for the strengthening of roof and wall with a peculiar form of iron girder

chapter xxxiv the betrothal it be a dry sunday and really a pleasant day for the 2 d of november

bright february day have a strong charm of hope about they than any other day in the year
one like to pause in the mild ray of the sun and look over the gate at the patient plough-horse turn at the end of the furrow and think that the beautiful year be all before one
the bird seem to feel just the same their note be as clear as the clear air
there be no leaf on the tree and hedgerow but how green all the grassy field be
and the dark purplish brown of the plough earth and of the bare branch be beautiful too
what a glad world this look like as one drive or ride along the valley and over the hill
i have often think so when in foreign country where the field and wood have look to i like our english loamshire--the rich land till with just as much care the wood roll down the gentle slope to the green meadow--i have come on something by the roadside which have remind i that i be not in loamshire an image of a great agony--the agony of the cross
it have stand perhaps by the cluster apple-blossom or in the broad sunshine by the cornfield or at a turning by the wood where a clear brook be gurgle below and surely if there come a traveller to this world who know nothing of the story of man s life upon it this image of agony would seem to he strangely out of place in the midst of this joyous nature
he would not know that hide behind the apple-blossom or among the golden corn or under the shroud bough of the wood there might be a human heart beat heavily with anguish--perhaps a young bloom girl not know where to turn for refuge from swift-advance shame understand no more of this life of our than a foolish lost lamb wander far and far in the nightfall on the lonely heath yet taste the bitter of life s bitterness
such thing be sometimes hide among the sunny field and behind the blossoming orchard and the sound of the gurgle brook if you come close to one spot behind a small bush would be mingle for your ear with a despairing human sob
no wonder man s religion have much sorrow in it no wonder he need a suffer god
hetty in her red cloak and warm bonnet with her basket in her hand be turn towards a gate by the side of the treddleston road but not that she may have a more lingering enjoyment of the sunshine and think with hope of the long unfolding year
she hardly know that the sun be shin and for week now when she have hop at all it have be for something at which she herself tremble and shudder
she only want to be out of the high-road that she may walk slowly and not care how she face look as she dwell on wretched thought and through this gate she can get into a field-path behind the wide thick hedgerow
her great dark eye wander blankly over the field like the eye of one who be desolate homeless unloved not the promise bride of a brave tender man
but there be no tear in they her tear be all weep away in the weary night before she go to sleep
at the next stile the pathway branch off there be two road before she--one along by the hedgerow which will by and by lead she into the road again the other across the field which will take she much far out of the way into the scantlands low shroud pasture where she will see nobody
she choose this and begin to walk a little fast as if she have suddenly think of an object towards which it be worth while to hasten
soon she be in the scantlands where the grassy land slope gradually downwards and she leave the level ground to follow the slope
far on there be a clump of tree on the low ground and she be make her way towards it
no it be not a clump of tree but a dark shroud pool so full with the wintry rain that the under bough of the eld-bush lie low beneath the water
she sit down on the grassy bank against the stoop stem of the great oak that hang over the dark pool
she have thought of this pool often in the night of the month that have just go by and now at last she be come to see it
she clasp her hand round her knee and lean forward and look earnestly at it as if try to guess what sort of bed it would make for her young round limb
no she have not courage to jump into that cold watery bed and if she have they might find she--they might find out why she have drown herself
there be but one thing leave to she she must go away go where they cannot find she
after the first on-come of her great dread some week after she betrothal to adam she have wait and wait in the blind vague hope that something would happen to set she free from her terror but she could wait no long
all the force of her nature have be concentrate on the one effort of concealment and she have shrink with irresistible dread from every course that could tend towards a betrayal of her miserable secret
whenever the thought of writing to arthur have occur to she she have reject it
he could do nothing for she that would shelter she from discovery and scorn among the relative and neighbour who once more make all her world now her airy dream have vanish
her imagination no long see happiness with arthur for he could do nothing that would satisfy or soothe her pride
no something else would happen--something must happen--to set she free from this dread
in young childish ignorant soul there be constantly this blind trust in some unshapen chance it be as hard to a boy or girl to believe that a great wretchedness will actually befall they as to believe that they will die
but now necessity be press hard upon she--now the time of her marriage be close at hand--she could no long rest in this blind trust
she must run away she must hide herself where no familiar eye could detect she and then the terror of wander out into the world of which she know nothing make the possibility of go to arthur a thought which bring some comfort with it
she feel so helpless now so unable to fashion the future for herself that the prospect of throw herself on he have a relief in it which be strong than her pride
as she sit by the pool and shudder at the dark cold water the hope that he would receive she tenderly--that he would care for she and think for she--be like a sense of lulling warmth that make she for the moment indifferent to everything else and she begin now to think of nothing but the scheme by which she should get away

book five chapter xxxvi the journey of hope a long lonely journey with sadness in the heart away from the familiar to the strange that be a hard and dreary thing even to the rich the strong the instructed a hard thing even when we be call by duty not urge by dread
what be it then to hetty
with her poor narrow thought no long melt into vague hope but press upon by the chill of definite fear repeat again and again the same small round of memory--shape again and again the same childish doubtful image of what be to come--see nothing in this wide world but the little history of her own pleasure and pain with so little money in her pocket and the way so long and difficult
unless she could afford always to go in the coach--and she feel sure she could not for the journey to stoniton be more expensive than she have expect--it be plain that she must trust to carrier cart or slow waggon and what a time it would be before she could get to the end of her journey

the beginning of hardship be like the first taste of bitter food--it seem for a moment unbearable yet if there be nothing else to satisfy our hunger we take another bite and find it possible to go on
when hetty recover from her burst of weeping she rally her fainting courage it be rain and she must try to get on to a village where she might find rest and shelter

the parish you can perhaps hardly understand the effect of that word on a mind like hetty s bring up among people who be somewhat hard in their feeling even towards poverty who live among the field and have little pity for want and rag as a cruel inevitable fate such as they sometimes seem in city but hold they a mark of idleness and vice--and it be idleness and vice that bring burden on the parish

despair no more lean on other than perfect contentment and in despair pride cease to be counteract by the sense of dependence
hetty feel that no one could deliver she from the evil that would make life hateful to she and no one she say to herself should ever know her misery and humiliation
no she would not confess even to dinah
she would wander out of sight and drown herself where her body would never be find and no one should know what have become of she
when she get off this coach she begin to walk again and take cheap ride in cart and get cheap meal go on and on without distinct purpose yet strangely by some fascination take the way she have come though she be determined not to go back to her own country
perhaps it be because she have fix her mind on the grassy warwickshire field with the bushy tree-stud hedgerow that make a hiding-place even in this leafless season
she go more slowly than she come often get over the stile and sit for hour under the hedgerow look before she with blank beautiful eye fancy herself at the edge of a hide pool low down like that in the scantlands wonder if it be very painful to be drown and if there would be anything bad after death than what she dread in life
religious doctrine have take no hold on hetty s mind
she be one of those numerous people who have have godfather and godmother learn their catechism be confirm and go to church every sunday and yet for any practical result of strength in life or trust in death have never appropriate a single christian idea or christian feeling
you would misunderstand her thought during these wretched day if you imagine that they be influence either by religious fear or religious hope
she choose to go to stratford-on-avon again where she have go before by mistake for she remember some grassy field on her former way towards it--field among which she think she might find just the sort of pool she have in her mind
yet she take care of her money still she carry her basket death seem still a long way off and life be so strong in she
she crave food and rest--she hasten towards they at the very moment she be picture to herself the bank from which she would leap towards death
it be already five day since she have leave windsor for she have wander about always avoid speech or questioning look and recover her air of proud self-dependence whenever she be under observation choose her decent lodging at night and dress herself neatly in the morning and set off on her way steadily or remain under shelter if it rain as if she have a happy life to cherish
and yet even in her most self-conscious moment the face be sadly different from that which have smile at itself in the old speck glass or smile at other when they glance at it admiringly
a hard and even fierce look have come in the eye though their lash be as long as ever and they have all their dark brightness
and the cheek be never dimple with smile now
it be the same rounded pouting childish prettiness but with all love and belief in love depart from it--the sadder for its beauty like that wondrous medusa-face with the passionate passionless lip
at last she be among the field she have be dream of on a long narrow pathway lead towards a wood
if there should be a pool in that wood
it would be well hide than one in the field
no it be not a wood only a wild brake where there have once be gravel-pit leave mound and hollow stud with brushwood and small tree
she roam up and down think there be perhaps a pool in every hollow before she come to it till her limb be weary and she sit down to rest
the afternoon be far advanced and the leaden sky be darken as if the sun be set behind it
after a little while hetty start up again feel that darkness would soon come on and she must put off find the pool till to-morrow and make her way to some shelter for the night
she have quite lose her way in the field and might as well go in one direction as another for aught she know
she walk through field after field and no village no house be in sight but there at the corner of this pasture there be a break in the hedge the land seem to dip down a little and two tree lean towards each other across the opening
hetty s heart give a great heat as she think there must be a pool there
she walk towards it heavily over the tufte grass with pale lip and a sense of trembling
it be as if the thing be come in spite of herself instead of be the object of her search
there it be black under the darken sky no motion no sound near
she set down her basket and then sink down herself on the grass tremble
the pool have its wintry depth now by the time it get shallow as she remember the pool do at hayslope in the summer no one could find out that it be her body
but then there be her basket--she must hide that too
she must throw it into the water--make it heavy with stone first and then throw it in
she get up to look about for stone and soon bring five or six which she lay down beside her basket and then sit down again
there be no need to hurry--there be all the night to drown herself in
she sit lean she elbow on the basket
she be weary hungry
there be some bun in her basket--three which she have supply herself with at the place where she eat her dinner
she take they out now and eat they eagerly and then sit still again look at the pool
the soothe sensation that come over she from the satisfaction of her hunger and this fix dreamy attitude bring on drowsiness and presently her head sink down on her knee
she be fast asleep
when she awake it be deep night and she feel chill
she be frightened at this darkness--frighten at the long night before she
if she could but throw herself into the water
no not yet
she begin to walk about that she might get warm again as if she would have more resolution then
oh how long the time be in that darkness
the bright hearth and the warmth and the voice of home the secure uprising and lie down the familiar field the familiar people the sunday and holiday with their simple joy of dress and feasting--all the sweet of her young life rush before she now and she seem to be stretch her arm towards they across a great gulf
she set her tooth when she think of arthur
she curse he without know what her cursing would do
she wish he too might know desolation and cold and a life of shame that he dare not end by death
the horror of this cold and darkness and solitude--out of all human reach--become great every long minute
it be almost as if she be dead already and know that she be dead and long to get back to life again
but no she be alive still she have not take the dreadful leap
she feel a strange contradictory wretchedness and exultation wretchedness that she do not dare to face death exultation that she be still in life--that she might yet know light and warmth again
she walk backwards and forwards to warm herself begin to discern something of the object around she as her eye become accustomed to the night--the dark line of the hedge the rapid motion of some living creature--perhaps a field-mouse--rush across the grass
she no long feel as if the darkness hedge she in
she think she could walk back across the field and get over the stile and then in the very next field she think she remember there be a hovel of furze near a sheepfold
if she could get into that hovel she would be warm
she could pass the night there for that be what alick do at hayslope in lambing-time
the thought of this hovel bring the energy of a new hope
she take up her basket and walk across the field but it be some time before she get in the right direction for the stile
the exercise and the occupation of find the stile be a stimulus to she however and lighten the horror of the darkness and solitude
there be sheep in the next field and she startle a group as she set down her basket and get over the stile and the sound of their movement comfort she for it assure she that her impression be right--this be the field where she have see the hovel for it be the field where the sheep be
right on along the path and she would get to it
she reach the opposite gate and feel her way along its rail and the rail of the sheep-fold till her hand encounter the pricking of the gorsy wall
delicious sensation
she have find the shelter
she grope her way touch the prickly gorse to the door and push it open
it be an ill-smell close place but warm and there be straw on the ground
hetty sink down on the straw with a sense of escape
tear come--she have never shed tear before since she leave windsor--tear and sob of hysterical joy that she have still hold of life that she be still on the familiar earth with the sheep near she
the very consciousness of her own limb be a delight to she she turn up her sleeve and kiss her arm with the passionate love of life
soon warmth and weariness lull she in the midst of her sob and she fall continually into dozing fancy herself at the brink of the pool again--fancy that she have jump into the water and then awake with a start and wonder where she be
but at last deep dreamless sleep come her head guard by her bonnet find a pillow against the gorsy wall and the poor soul drive to and fro between two equal terror find the one relief that be possible to it--the relief of unconsciousness
alas
that relief seem to end the moment it have begin
it seem to hetty as if those dozen dream have only pass into another dream--that she be in the hovel and her aunt be stand over she with a candle in her hand
she tremble under her aunt s glance and open her eye
there be no candle but there be light in the hovel--the light of early morning through the open door
and there be a face look down on she but it be an unknown face belong to an elderly man in a smock-frock
why what do you do here young woman the man say roughly
hetty tremble still bad under this real fear and shame than she have do in her momentary dream under her aunt s glance
she feel that she be like a beggar already--find sleep in that place
but in spite of her trembling she be so eager to account to the man for her presence here that she find word at once
i lose my way she say
ibe travel--north ard and i get away from the road into the field and be overtake by the dark
will you tell i the way to the near village she get up as she be speak and put her hand to her bonnet to adjust it and then lay hold of her basket
the man look at she with a slow bovine gaze without give she any answer for some second
then he turn away and walk towards the door of the hovel but it be not till he get there that he stand still and turn his shoulder half-round towards she say aw i can show you the way to norton if you like
but what do you do gettin out o the highroad he add with a tone of gruff reproof
y ull be gettin into mischief if you dooant mind yes say hetty i willnot do it again
iwill keep in the road if youwill be so good as show i how to get to it why dooant you keep where therebe a finger-poasse an folk to ax the way on the man say still more gruffly
anybody ud think you be a wild woman an look at yer hetty be frighten at this gruff old man and still more at this last suggestion that she look like a wild woman
as she follow he out of the hovel she think she would give he a sixpence for tell she the way and then he would not suppose she be wild
as he stop to point out the road to she she put her hand in her pocket to get the six-pence ready and when he be turn away without say good-morning she hold it out to he and say thank you will you please to take something for your trouble he look slowly at the sixpence and then say i want none o your money
youwould well take care on t else youwill get it stool from yer if you go trapesin about the field like a mad woman a-thatway the man leave she without further speech and hetty hold on her way
another day have rise and she must wander on
it be no use to think of drown herself--she could not do it at least while she have money leave to buy food and strength to journey on
but the incident on her wake this morning heighten her dread of that time when her money would be all go she would have to sell her basket and clothe then and she would really look like a beggar or a wild woman as the man have say
the passionate joy in life she have feel in the night after escape from the brink of the black cold death in the pool be go now
life now by the morning light with the impression of that man s hard wonder look at she be as full of dread as death--it be bad it be a dread to which she feel chain from which she shrink and shrink as she do from the black pool and yet could find no refuge from it
she take out her money from her purse and look at it
she have still two-and-twenty shilling it would serve she for many day more or it would help she to get on fast to stonyshire within reach of dinah
the thought of dinah urge itself more strongly now since the experience of the night have drive her shudder imagination away from the pool
if it have be only go to dinah--if nobody besides dinah would ever know--hetty could have make up her mind to go to she
the soft voice the pity eye would have draw she
but afterwards the other people must know and she could no more rush on that shame than she could rush on death
she must wander on and on and wait for a low depth of despair to give her courage
perhaps death would come to she for she be get less and less able to bear the day s weariness
and yet--such be the strange action of our soul draw we by a lurking desire towards the very end we dread--hetty when she set out again from norton ask the straight road northward towards stonyshire and keep it all that day
poor wander hetty with the rounded childish face and the hard unloving despairing soul look out of it--with the narrow heart and narrow thought no room in they for any sorrow but her own and taste that sorrow with the more intense bitterness
my heart bleed for she as i see she toil along on her weary foot or seat in a cart with her eye fix vacantly on the road before she never think or care whither it tend till hunger come and make she desire that a village may be near
what will be the end the end of her objectless wandering apart from all love care for human being only through her pride cling to life only as the hunt wound brute cling to it
god preserve you and i from be the beginner of such misery
chapter xxxviii the quest the first ten day after hetty s departure pass as quietly as any other day with the family at the hall farm and with adam at his daily work
they have expect hetty to stay away a week or ten day at least perhaps a little long if dinah come back with she because there might then be something to detain they at snowfield
but when a fortnight have pass they begin to feel a little surprise that hetty do not return she must surely have find it pleasanter to be with dinah than any one could have suppose
adam for his part be get very impatient to see she and he resolve that if she do not appear the next day saturday he would set out on sunday morning to fetch she
there be no coach on a sunday but by set out before it be light and perhaps get a lift in a cart by the way he would arrive pretty early at snowfield and bring back hetty the next day--dinah too if she be come
it be quite time hetty come home and he would afford to lose his monday for the sake of bring she
his project be quite approve at the farm when he go there on saturday evening
mrs
poyser desire he emphatically not to come back without hetty for she have be quite too long away consider the thing she have to get ready by the middle of march and a week be surely enough for any one to go out for their health
as for dinah mrs
poyser have small hope of their bring she unless they could make she believe the folk at hayslope be twice as miserable as the folk at snowfield
though say mrs
poyser by way of conclusion you might tell she shebe get but one aunt leave and shebe waste pretty nigh to a shadder and we shall p rhaps all be go twenty mile far off her next michaelmas and shall die o broken heart among strange folk and leave the child fatherless and motherless nay nay say mr
poyser who certainly have the air of a man perfectly heart-whole it isna so bad as that
thee t look rarely now and get flesh every day
but iwould be glad for dinah t come for shewould help thee wi the little un they take t her wonderful so at daybreak on sunday adam set off
seth go with he the first mile or two for the thought of snowfield and the possibility that dinah might come again make he restless and the walk with adam in the cold morning air both in their good clothe help to give he a sense of sunday calm
it be the last morning in february with a low grey sky and a slight hoar-frost on the green border of the road and on the black hedge
they hear the gurgling of the full brooklet hurry down the hill and the faint twittering of the early bird
for they walk in silence though with a pleased sense of companionship

iwill be an old bachelor belike and make a fuss wi thy child the y turn away from each other and seth walk leisurely homeward mentally repeat one of his favourite hymn--he be very fond of hymn dark and cheerless be the morn unaccompanie by thee joyless be the day s return till thy mercy s beam i see till thou inward light impart glad my eye and warm my heart
visit then this soul of mine pierce the gloom of sin and grief-- fill i radiancy divine scatter all my unbelief
more and more thyself display shin to the perfect day
adam walk much fast and any one come along the oakbourne road at sunrise that morning must have have a pleasant sight in this tall broad-cheste man stride along with a carriage as upright and firm as any soldier s glance with keen glad eye at the dark-blue hill as they begin to show themselves on his way
seldom in adam s life have his face be so free from any cloud of anxiety as it be this morning and this freedom from care as be usual with constructive practical mind like his make he all the more observant of the object round he and all the more ready to gather suggestion from they towards his own favourite plan and ingenious contrivance
his happy love--the knowledge that his step be carry he near and nearer to hetty who be so soon to be his--be to his thought what the sweet morning air be to his sensation it give he a consciousness of well-being that make activity delightful
every now and then there be a rush of more intense feeling towards she which chase away other image than hetty and along with that would come a wonder thankfulness that all this happiness be give to he--that this life of our have such sweetness in it
for adam have a devout mind though he be perhaps rather impatient of devout word and his tenderness lie very close to his reverence so that the one could hardly be stir without the other
but after feeling have well up and pour itself out in this way busy thought would come back with the great vigour and this morning it be intent on scheme by which the road might be improve that be so imperfect all through the country and on picture all the benefit that might come from the exertion of a single country gentleman if he would set himself to get the road make good in his own district
it seem a very short walk the ten mile to oakbourne that pretty town within sight of the blue hill where he break-fast
after this the country grow barer and barer no more rolling wood no more wide-branch tree near frequent homestead no more bushy hedgerow but greystone wall intersect the meagre pasture and dismal wide-scatter greystone house on broken land where mine have be and be no long
a hungry land say adam to himself
iwould rather go south ard where they say itbe as flat as a table than come to live here though if dinah like to live in a country where she can be the most comfort to folk shebe i the right to live o this side for she must look as if shewould come straight from heaven like th angel in the desert to strengthen they as ha get nothing t eat and when at last he come in sight of snowfield he think it look like a town that be fellow to the country though the stream through the valley where the great mill stand give a pleasant greenness to the low field
the town lay grim stony and unsheltered up the side of a steep hill and adam do not go forward to it at present for seth have tell he where to find dinah
it be at a thatched cottage outside the town a little way from the mill--an old cottage stand sideways towards the road with a little bit of potato-ground before it
here dinah lodge with an elderly couple and if she and hetty happen to be out adam could learn where they be go or when they would be at home again
dinah might be out on some preach errand and perhaps she would have leave hetty at home
adam could not help hop this and as he recognize the cottage by the roadside before he there shine out in his face that involuntary smile which belong to the expectation of a near joy
he hurry his step along the narrow causeway and rap at the door

be dinah morris at home say adam

hitherto since sunday afternoon adam have be constantly among strange people and in strange place have no association with the detail of his daily life and now that by the light of this new morning he be come back to his home and surround by the familiar object that seem for ever rob of their charm the reality--the hard inevitable reality of his trouble press upon he with a new weight

iwill see you early to-morrow morning chapter xlii the morning of the trial at one o clock the next day adam be alone in his dull upper room his watch lie before he on the table as if he be count the long minute
he have no knowledge of what be likely to be say by the witness on the trial for he have shrink from all the particular connect with hetty s arrest and accusation
this brave active man who would have hasten towards any danger or toil to rescue hetty from an apprehend wrong or misfortune feel himself powerless to contemplate irremediable evil and suffering
the susceptibility which would have be an impelling force where there be any possibility of action become helpless anguish when he be oblige to be passive or else seek an active outlet in the thought of inflict justice on arthur
energetic nature strong for all strenuous deed will often rush away from a hopeless sufferer as if they be hard-hearted
it be the overmaster sense of pain that drive they
they shrink by an ungovernable instinct as they would shrink from laceration
adam have bring himself to think of see hetty if she would consent to see he because he think the meeting might possibly be a good to she--might help to melt away this terrible hardness they tell he of
if she see he bear she no ill will for what she have do to he she might open her heart to he
but this resolution have be an immense effort--he tremble at the thought of see her change face as a timid woman tremble at the thought of the surgeon s knife and he choose now to bear the long hour of suspense rather than encounter what seem to he the more intolerable agony of witness her trial
deep unspeakable suffering may well be call a baptism a regeneration the initiation into a new state
the yearn memory the bitter regret the agonized sympathy the struggle appeal to the invisible right--all the intense emotion which have fill the day and night of the past week and be compress themselves again like an eager crowd into the hour of this single morning make adam look back on all the previous year as if they have be a dim sleepy existence and he have only now awake to full consciousness
it seem to he as if he have always before think it a light thing that man should suffer as if all that he have himself endure and call sorrow before be only a moment s stroke that have never leave a bruise
doubtless a great anguish may do the work of year and we may come out from that baptism of fire with a soul full of new awe and new pity

chapter xliii the verdict the place fit up that day as a court of justice be a grand old hall now destroy by fire
the midday light that fall on the close pavement of human head be shed through a line of high point window variegate with the mellow tint of old paint glass
grim dusty armour hang in high relief in front of the dark oaken gallery at the far end and under the broad arch of the great mullioned window opposite be spread a curtain of old tapestry cover with dim melancholy figure like a doze indistinct dream of the past
it be a place that through the rest of the year be haunt with the shadowy memory of old king and queen unhappy discrowne imprison but to-day all those shadow have flee and not a soul in the vast hall feel the presence of any but a living sorrow which be quiver in warm heart
but that sorrow seem to have make it itself feebly feel hitherto now when adam bede s tall figure be suddenly see be usher to the side of the prisoner s dock
in the broad sunlight of the great hall among the sleek shaven face of other man the mark of suffer in his face be startling even to mr
irwine who have last see he in the dim light of his small room and the neighbour from hayslope who be present and who tell hetty sorrel s story by their fireside in their old age never forget to say how it move they when adam bede poor fellow tall by the head than most of the people round he come into court and take his place by her side

why do they say she be so change
in the corpse we love it be the likeness we see--it be the likeness which make itself feel the more keenly because something else be and be not
there they be--the sweet face and neck with the dark tendril of hair the long dark lash the rounded cheek and the pouting lip--pale and thin yes but like hetty and only hetty
other think she look as if some demon have cast a blight glance upon she wither up the woman s soul in she and leave only a hard despairing obstinacy
but the mother s yearning that complete type of the life in another life which be the essence of real human love feel the presence of the cherished child even in the debase degraded man and to adam this pale hard-look culprit be the hetty who have smile at he in the garden under the apple-tree bough--she be that hetty s corpse which he have tremble to look at the first time and then be unwilling to turn away his eye from
but presently he hear something that compel he to listen and make the sense of sight less absorbing
a woman be in the witness-box a middle-aged woman who speak in a firm distinct voice
she say my name be sarah stone
i be a widow and keep a small shop license to sell tobacco snuff and tea in church lane stoniton
the prisoner at the bar be the same young woman who come look ill and tired with a basket on her arm and ask for a lodging at my house on saturday evening the 27 th of february
she have take the house for a public because there be a figure against the door
and when i say i donot take in lodger the prisoner begin to cry and say she be too tired to go anywhere else and she only want a bed for one night
and her prettiness and her condition and something respectable about her clothe and look and the trouble she seem to be in make i as i couldnot find in my heart to send she away at once
i ask she to sit down and give she some tea and ask she where she be go and where her friend be
she say she be go home to her friend they be farm folk a good way off and shewould have a long journey that have cost she more money than she expect so as shewould hardly any money leave in her pocket and be afraid of go where it would cost she much
she have be oblige to sell most of the thing out of her basket but shewould thankfully give a shilling for a bed
i see no reason why i shouldnot take the young woman in for the night
i have only one room but there be two bed in it and i tell she she might stay with i
i think shehave be lead wrong and get into trouble but if she be go to her friend it would be a good work to keep she out of further harm the witness then state that in the night a child be bear and she identify the baby-clothe then show to she as those in which she have herself dress the child
those be the clothe
i make they myself and have keep they by i ever since my last child be bear
i take a deal of trouble both for the child and the mother
i couldnot help take to the little thing and be anxious about it
i donot send for a doctor for there seem no need
i tell the mother in the day-time she must tell i the name of her friend and where they live and let i write to they
she say by and by she would write herself but not to-day
she would have no nay but she would get up and be dress in spite of everything i could say
she say she feel quite strong enough and it be wonderful what spirit she show
but i benot quite easy what i should do about she and towards evening i make up my mind iwould go after meeting be over and speak to our minister about it
i leave the house about half-past eight o clock
i donot go out at the shop door but at the back door which open into a narrow alley
ihave only get the ground-floor of the house and the kitchen and bedroom both look into the alley
i leave the prisoner sit up by the fire in the kitchen with the baby on her lap
she havenot cry or seem low at all as she do the night before
i think she have a strange look with her eye and she get a bit flushed towards evening
i be afraid of the fever and i think iwould call and ask an acquaintance of mine an experienced woman to come back with i when i go out
it be a very dark night
i donot fasten the door behind i there be no lock it be a latch with a bolt inside and when there be nobody in the house i always go out at the shop door
but i think there be no danger in leave it unfasten that little while
i be long than i mean to be for i have to wait for the woman that come back with i
it be an hour and a half before we get back and when we go in the candle be stand burn just as i leave it but the prisoner and the baby be both go
shewould take her cloak and bonnet but shewould leave the basket and the thing in it i be dreadful frightened and angry with she for go
i donot go to give information because iwould no thought she mean to do any harm and i know she have money in her pocket to buy her food and lodging
i donot like to set the constable after she for shehave a right to go from i if she like the effect of this evidence on adam be electrical it give he new force
hetty could not be guilty of the crime--her heart must have clung to her baby--else why should she have take it with she
she might have leave it behind
the little creature have die naturally and then she have hide it
baby be so liable to death--and there might be the strong suspicion without any proof of guilt
his mind be so occupy with imaginary argument against such suspicion that he could not listen to the cross-examination by hetty s counsel who try without result to elicit evidence that the prisoner have show some movement of maternal affection towards the child
the whole time this witness be be examine hetty have stand as motionless as before no word seem to arrest her ear
but the sound of the next witness s voice touch a chord that be still sensitive she give a start and a frightened look towards he but immediately turn away her head and look down at her hand as before
this witness be a man a rough peasant
he say my name be john olding
i be a labourer and live at tedd s hole two mile out of stoniton
a week last monday towards one o clock in the afternoon i be go towards hetton coppice and about a quarter of a mile from the coppice i see the prisoner in a red cloak sit under a bit of a haystack not far off the stile
she get up when she see i and seem as if shewould be walk on the other way
it be a regular road through the field and nothing very uncommon to see a young woman there but i take notice of she because she look white and scared
i should have think she be a beggar-woman only for her good clothe
i think she look a bit crazy but it be no business of mine
i stand and look back after she but she go right on while she be in sight
i have to go to the other side of the coppice to look after some stake
therebe a road right through it and bit of opening here and there where the tree have be cut down and some of them not carry away
i donot go straight along the road but turn off towards the middle and take a short way towards the spot i want to get to
i havenot get far out of the road into one of the open place before i hear a strange cry
i think it donot come from any animal i know but i benot for stop to look about just then
but it go on and seem so strange to i in that place i couldnot help stop to look
i begin to think i might make some money of it if it be a new thing
but i have hard work to tell which way it come from and for a good while i keep look up at the bough
and then i think it come from the ground and there be a lot of timber-chopping lie about and loose piece of turf and a trunk or two
and i look about among they but could find nothing and at last the cry stop
so i be for give it up and i go on about my business
but when i come back the same way pretty nigh an hour after i couldnot help lay down my stake to have another look
and just as i be stoop and lay down the stake i see something odd and round and whitish lie on the ground under a nut-bush by the side of i
and i stoop down on hand and knee to pick it up
and i see it be a little baby s hand at these word a thrill run through the court
hetty be visibly trembling now for the first time she seem to be listen to what a witness say
there be a lot of timber-chopping put together just where the ground go hollow like under the bush and the hand come out from among they
but there be a hole leave in one place and i could see down it and see the child s head and i make haste and do away the turf and the chopping and take out the child
it have get comfortable clothe on but its body be cold and i think it must be dead
i make haste back with it out of the wood and take it home to my wife
she say it be dead and iwould well take it to the parish and tell the constable
and i say iwill lay my life itbe that young woman s child as i meet go to the coppice but she seem to be go clean out of sight
and i take the child on to hetton parish and tell the constable and we go on to justice hardy
and then we go look after the young woman till dark at night and we go and give information at stoniton as they might stop she
and the next morning another constable come to i to go with he to the spot where i find the child
and when we get there there be the prisoner a-sitting against the bush where i find the child and she cry out when she see we but she never offer to move
shewould get a big piece of bread on her lap adam have give a faint groan of despair while this witness be speak
he have hide his face on his arm which rest on the boarding in front of he
it be the supreme moment of his suffering hetty be guilty and he be silently call to god for help
he hear no more of the evidence and be unconscious when the case for the prosecution have close--unconscious that mr
irwine be in the witness-box tell of hetty s unblemished character in her own parish and of the virtuous habit in which she have be bring up
this testimony could have no influence on the verdict but it be give as part of that plea for mercy which her own counsel would have make if he have be allow to speak for she--a favour not grant to criminal in those stern time
at last adam lift up his head for there be a general movement round he
the judge have address the jury and they be retire
the decisive moment be not far off adam feel a shudder horror that would not let he look at hetty but she have long relapse into her blank hard indifference
all eye be strained to look at she but she stand like a statue of dull despair
there be a mingled rustling whispering and low buzzing throughout the court during this interval
the desire to listen be suspend and every one have some feeling or opinion to express in undertone
adam sit look blankly before he but he do not see the object that be right in front of his eye--the counsel and attorney talk with an air of cool business and mr
irwine in low earnest conversation with the judge--do not see mr
irwine sit down again in agitation and shake his head mournfully when somebody whisper to he
the inward action be too intense for adam to take in outward object until some strong sensation rouse he
it be not very long hardly more than a quarter of an hour before the knock which tell that the jury have come to their decision fall as a signal for silence on every ear
it be sublime--that sudden pause of a great multitude which tell that one soul move in they all
deeper and deeply the silence seem to become like the deepening night while the juryman s name be call over and the prisoner be make to hold up her hand and the jury be ask for their verdict
guilty it be the verdict every one expect but there be a sigh of disappointment from some heart that it be follow by no recommendation to mercy
still the sympathy of the court be not with the prisoner
the unnaturalness of her crime stand out the more harshly by the side of her hard immovability and obstinate silence
even the verdict to distant eye have not appear to move she but those who be near see her trembling
the stillness be less intense until the judge put on his black cap and the chaplain in his canonical be observe behind he
then it deepen again before the crier have have time to command silence
if any sound be hear it must have be the sound of beat heart
the judge speak hester sorrel the blood rush to hetty s face and then flee back again as she look up at the judge and keep her wide-open eye fix on he as if fascinate by fear
adam have not yet turn towards she there be a deep horror like a great gulf between they
but at the word and then to be hang by the neck till you be dead a pierce shriek rang through the hall
it be hetty s shriek
adam start to his foot and stretch out his arm towards she
but the arm could not reach she she have fall down in a fainting-fit and be carry out of court
chapter xliv arthur s return when arthur donnithorne land at liverpool and read the letter from his aunt lydia briefly announce his grand-father s death his first feeling be poor grandfather
i wish i could have get to he to be with he when he die
he might have feel or wish something at the last that i shall never know now
it be a lonely death it be impossible to say that his grief be deep than that
pity and soften memory take place of the old antagonism and in his busy thought about the future as the chaise carry he rapidly along towards the home where he be now to be master there be a continually recur effort to remember anything by which he could show a regard for his grandfather s wish without counteract his own cherished aim for the good of the tenant and the estate
but it be not in human nature--only in human pretence--for a young man like arthur with a fine constitution and fine spirit think well of himself believe that other think well of he and have a very ardent intention to give they more and more reason for that good opinion--it be not possible for such a young man just come into a splendid estate through the death of a very old man whom he be not fond of to feel anything very different from exultant joy
now his real life be begin now he would have room and opportunity for action and he would use they
he would show the loamshire people what a fine country gentleman be he would not exchange that career for any other under the sun
he feel himself rid over the hill in the breezy autumn day look after favourite plan of drainage and enclosure then admire on sombre morning as the good rider on the good horse in the hunt speak well of on market-day as a first-rate landlord by and by make speech at election dinner and show a wonderful knowledge of agriculture the patron of new plough and drill the severe upbraider of negligent landowner and withal a jolly fellow that everybody must like--happy face greet he everywhere on his own estate and the neighbouring family on the good term with he
the irwines should dine with he every week and have their own carriage to come in for in some very delicate way that arthur would devise the lay-impropriator of the hayslope tithe would insist on pay a couple of hundred more to the vicar and his aunt should be as comfortable as possible and go on live at the chase if she like in spite of her old-maidish way--at least until he be marry and that event lie in the indistinct background for arthur have not yet see the woman who would play the lady-wife to the first-rate country gentleman
these be arthur s chief thought so far as a man s thought through hour of travel can be compress into a few sentence which be only like the list of name tell you what be the scene in a long long panorama full of colour of detail and of life
the happy face arthur see greet he be not pale abstraction but real ruddy face long familiar to he martin poyser be there--the whole poyser family
what--hetty
yes for arthur be at ease about hetty--not quite at ease about the past for a certain burning of the ear would come whenever he think of the scene with adam last august but at ease about her present lot

irwine who have be a regular correspondent tell he all the news about the old place and people have send he word nearly three month ago that adam bede be not to marry mary burge as he have think but pretty hetty sorrel

he know arthur would like to hear that adam have this sort of happiness in prospect
yes indeed

but if adam be the same again as in the old day arthur wish to be the same too and to have adam mix up with his business and his future as he have always desire before the accursed meeting in august
nay he would do a great deal more for adam than he should otherwise have do when he come into the estate hetty s husband have a special claim on he--hetty herself should feel that any pain she have suffer through arthur in the past be compensate to her a hundredfold

you perceive clearly what sort of picture adam and hetty make in the panorama of arthur s thought on his journey homeward
it be march now they be soon to be marry perhaps they be already marry
and now it be actually in his power to do a great deal for they
sweet--sweet little hetty

thank heaven it have turn out so well
he should have plenty of affair and interest to fill his life now and not be in danger of play the fool again
pleasant the crack of the post-boy s whip
pleasant the sense of be hurry along in swift ease through english scene so like those round his own home only not quite so charming
here be a market-town--very much like treddleston--where the arm of the neighbour lord of the manor be bear on the sign of the principal inn then mere field and hedge their vicinity to a market-town carry an agreeable suggestion of high rent till the land begin to assume a trimmer look the wood be more frequent and at length a white or red mansion look down from a moderate eminence or allow he to be aware of its parapet and chimney among the dense-look mass of oak and elm--mass reddened now with early bud
and close at hand come the village the small church with its red-tile roof look humble even among the fade half-timbere house the old green gravestone with nettle round they nothing fresh and bright but the child open round eye at the swift post-chaise nothing noisy and busy but the gap cur of mysterious pedigree
what a much pretty village hayslope be
and it should not be neglect like this place vigorous repair should go on everywhere among farm-building and cottage and traveller in post-chaise come along the rosseter road should do nothing but admire as they go
and adam bede should superintend all the repair for he have a share in burge s business now and if he like arthur would put some money into the concern and buy the old man out in another year or two
that be an ugly fault in arthur s life that affair last summer but the future should make amend

no arthur have not an evil feeling in his mind towards any human being he be happy and would make every one else happy that come within his reach

poor grandfather
and he lie dead there
he be a young fellow once come into the estate and make his plan

chapter xlv in the prison near sunset that evening an elderly gentleman be stand with his back against the small entrance-door of stoniton jail say a few last word to the depart chaplain
the chaplain walk away but the elderly gentleman stand still look down on the pavement and stroke his chin with a ruminate air when he be rouse by a sweet clear woman s voice say can i get into the prison if you please he turn his head and look fixedly at the speaker for a few moment without answer
i have see you before he say at last
do you remember preach on the village green at hayslope in loamshire yes sir surely
be you the gentleman that stay to listen on horseback yes
why do you want to go into the prison i want to go to hetty sorrel the young woman who have be condemn to death--and to stay with she if i may be permit
have you power in the prison sir yes i be a magistrate and can get admittance for you
but do you know this criminal hetty sorrel yes we be kin
my own aunt marry her uncle martin poyser
but i be away at leed and donot know of this great trouble in time to get here before to-day

he be go back to his home now and the poor sinner be forsake of all
i beseech you to get leave for i to be with she what
have you courage to stay all night in the prison
she be very sullen and will scarcely make answer when she be speak to oh sir it may please god to open her heart still
donot let we delay come then say the elderly gentleman ring and gain admission i know you have a key to unlock heart dinah mechanically take off her bonnet and shawl as soon as they be within the prison court from the habit she have of throw they off when she preach or pray or visit the sick and when they enter the jailer s room she lay they down on a chair unthinkingly
there be no agitation visible in she but a deep concentrated calmness as if even when she be speak her soul be in prayer reposing on an unseen support
after speak to the jailer the magistrate turn to she and say the turnkey will take you to the prisoner s cell and leave you there for the night if you desire it but you cannot have a light during the night--it be contrary to rule

irwine

there be an old school-master with he

he somehow feel that the sound of his own rude voice would be grate just then

the two pale face be look at each other one with a wild hard despair in it the other full of sad yearn love

donot you know i hetty
donot you remember dinah

ibe come to be with you hetty--not to leave you--to stay with you--to be your sister to the last slowly while dinah be speak hetty rise take a step forward and be clasp in dinah s arm

the light get fainter as they stand and when at last they sit down on the straw pallet together their face have become indistinct

she do not know how long they sit in that way but it get dark and darker till there be only a pale patch of light on the opposite wall all the rest be darkness

and on monday when i cannot follow you--when my arm cannot reach you--when death have part we--he who be with we now and know all will be with you then
it make no difference--whether we live or die we be in the presence of god oh dinah willnot nobody do anything for i
will they hang i for certain i wouldnot mind if theywould let i live my poor hetty death be very dreadful to you
i know itbe dreadful
but if you have a friend to take care of you after death--in that other world--some one whose love be great than mine--who can do everything if god our father be your friend and be willing to save you from sin and suffering so as you should neither know wicked feeling nor pain again
if you could believe he love you and would help you as you believe i love you and will help you it wouldnot be so hard to die on monday would it but i cannot know anything about it hetty say with sullen sadness
because hetty you be shut up your soul against he by try to hide the truth
god s love and mercy can overcome all thing--our ignorance and weakness and all the burden of our past wickedness--all thing but our wilful sin sin that we cling to and will not give up
you believe in my love and pity for you hetty but if you have not let i come near you if you wouldnot have look at i or speak to i youwould have shut i out from help you
i couldnot have make you feel my love i couldnot have tell you what i feel for you
donot shut god s love out in that way by cling to sin he cannot bless you while you have one falsehood in your soul his pardon mercy cannot reach you until you open your heart to he and say i have do this great wickedness o god save i make i pure from sin while you cling to one sin and will not part with it it must drag you down to misery after death as it have drag you to misery here in this world my poor poor hetty
it be sin that bring dread and darkness and despair there be light and blessedness for we as soon as we cast it off
god enter our soul then and teach we and bring we strength and peace
cast it off now hetty--now confess the wickedness you have do--the sin you have be guilty of against your heavenly father
let we kneel down together for we be in the presence of god hetty obey dinah s movement and sink on her knee
they still hold each other s hand and there be long silence
then dinah say hetty we be before god
he be wait for you to tell the truth still there be silence
at last hetty speak in a tone of beseeching-- dinah help i i cannot feel anything like you my heart be hard dinah hold the cling hand and all her soul go forth in her voice jesus thou present saviour
thou hast know the depth of all sorrow thou hast enter that black darkness where god be not and hast utter the cry of the forsaken

chapter xlvii the last moment it be a sight that some people remember well even than their own sorrow--the sight in that grey clear morning when the fatal cart with the two young woman in it be descry by the waiting watch multitude cleave its way towards the hideous symbol of a deliberately inflict sudden death

book six chapter xlix at the hall farm the first autumnal afternoon sunshine of 1801 more than eighteen month after that parting of adam and arthur in the hermitage--be on the yard at the hall farm and the bull-dog be in one of his most excited moment for it be that hour of the day when the cow be be drive into the yard for their afternoon milking
no wonder the patient beast run confusedly into the wrong place for the alarming din of the bull-dog be mingle with more distant sound which the timid feminine creature with pardonable superstition imagine also to have some relation to their own movement--with the tremendous crack of the waggoner s whip the roar of his voice and the booming thunder of the waggon as it leave the rick-yard empty of its golden load

for adam though you see he quite master of himself work hard and delight in his work after his inborn inalienable nature have not outlive his sorrow--have not feel it slip from he as a temporary burden and leave he the same man again
do any of we
god forbid
it would be a poor result of all our anguish and our wrestling if we win nothing but our old self at the end of it--if we could return to the same blind love the same self-confident blame the same light thought of human suffering the same frivolous gossip over blighted human life the same feeble sense of that unknown towards which we have send forth irrepressible cry in our loneliness
let we rather be thankful that our sorrow live in we as an indestructible force only change its form as force do and pass from pain into sympathy--the one poor word which include all our good insight and our good love
not that this transformation of pain into sympathy have completely take place in adam yet
there be still a great remnant of pain and this he feel would subsist as long as her pain be not a memory but an exist thing which he must think of as renew with the light of every new morning
but we get accustomed to mental as well as bodily pain without for all that lose our sensibility to it
it become a habit of our life and we cease to imagine a condition of perfect ease as possible for we
desire be chasten into submission and we be content with our day when we have be able to bear our grief in silence and act as if we be not suffer
for it be at such period that the sense of our life have visible and invisible relation beyond any of which either our present or prospective self be the centre grow like a muscle that we be oblige to lean on and exert
that be adam s state of mind in this second autumn of his sorrow
his work as you know have always be part of his religion and from very early day he see clearly that good carpentry be god s will--be that form of god s will that most immediately concern he
but now there be no margin of dream for he beyond this daylight reality no holiday-time in the working-day world no moment in the distance when duty would take off her iron glove and breast-plate and clasp he gently into rest
he conceive no picture of the future but one make up of hard-work day such as he live through with grow contentment and intensity of interest every fresh week
love he think could never be anything to he but a live memory--a limb lop off but not go from consciousness
he do not know that the power of love be all the while gain new force within he that the new sensibility buy by a deep experience be so many new fibre by which it be possible nay necessary to he that his nature should intertwine with another
yet he be aware that common affection and friendship be more precious to he than they use to be--that he cling more to his mother and seth and have an unspeakable satisfaction in the sight or imagination of any small addition to their happiness
the poysers too--hardly three or four day pass but he feel the need of see they and interchange word and look of friendliness with they
he would have feel this probably even if dinah have not be with they but he have only say the simple truth in tell dinah that he put she above all other friend in the world
could anything be more natural
for in the dark moment of memory the thought of she always come as the first ray of return comfort

but it be almost certain that you too have be in love--perhaps even more than once though you may not choose to say so to all your feminine friend
if so you will no more think the slight word the timid look the tremulous touch by which two human soul approach each other gradually like two little quivering rain-stream before they mingle into one--you will no more think these thing trivial than you will think the first-detect sign of come spring trivial though they be but a faint indescribable something in the air and in the song of the bird and the tiny perceptible bud on the hedge-row branch
those slight word and look and touch be part of the soul s language and the fine language i believe be chiefly make up of unimposing word such as light sound star music word really not worth look at or hearing in themselves any more than chip or sawdust it be only that they happen to be the sign of something unspeakably great and beautiful
i be of opinion that love be a great and beautiful thing too and if you agree with i the small sign of it will not be chip and sawdust to you they will rather be like those little word light and music stir the long-wind fibre of your memory and enrich your present with your most precious past
