surely all other leisure be hurry compare with a sunny walk through the field from afternoon church as such walk use to be in those old leisurely time when the boat glide sleepily along the canal be the new locomotive wonder when sunday book have most of they old brown-leather cover and open with remarkable precision always in one place
leisure be go--go where the spinning-wheel be go and the pack-horse and the slow waggon and the pedlar who bring bargain to the door on sunny afternoon
ingenious philosopher tell you perhaps that the great work of the steam-engine be to create leisure for mankind
do not believe they it only create a vacuum for eager think to rush in
even idleness be eager now--eager for amusement prone to excursion-train art museum periodical literature and exciting novel prone even to scientific theorizing and cursory peep through microscope
old leisure be quite a different personage
he only read one newspaper innocent of leader and be free from that periodicity of sensation which we call post-time
he be a contemplative rather stout gentleman of excellent digestion of quiet perception undisease by hypothesis happy in his inability to know the cause of thing prefer the thing themselves
he live chiefly in the country among pleasant seat and homestead and be fond of saunter by the fruit-tree wall and scent the apricot when they be warm by the morning sunshine or of shelter himself under the orchard bough at noon when the summer pear be fall
he know nothing of weekday service and think none the bad of the sunday sermon if it allow he to sleep from the text to the blessing like the afternoon service well because the prayer be the short and not ashamed to say so for he have an easy jolly conscience broad-back like himself and able to carry a great deal of beer or port-wine not be make squeamish by doubt and qualm and lofty aspiration
life be not a task to he but a sinecure
he finger the guineas in his pocket and eat his dinner and sleep the sleep of the irresponsible for have he not keep up his character by go to church on the sunday afternoon
fine old leisure
do not be severe upon he and judge he by our modern standard
he never go to exeter hall or hear a popular preacher or read tracts for the times or sartor resartus
chapter liii the harvest supper as adam be go homeward on wednesday evening in the six o clock sunlight he see in the distance the last load of barley wind its way towards the yard-gate of the hall farm and hear the chant of harvest home rise and sink like a wave
fainter and fainter and more musical through the grow distance the fall die sound still reach he as he near the willow brook
the low westere sun shine right on the shoulder of the old binton hills turn the unconscious sheep into bright spot of light shine on the window of the cottage too and make they a-flame with a glory beyond that of amber or amethyst
it be enough to make adam feel that he be in a great temple and that the distant chant be a sacred song
itbe wonderful he think how that sound go to one s heart almost like a funeral bell for all it tell one o the joyfull time o the year and the time when man be mostly the thankfull
i suppose itbe a bit hard to we to think anythingbe over and go in our life and therebe a parting at the root of all our joy
itbe like what i feel about dinah
i should never ha come to know that her love ud be the great o blessing to i if what i count a blessing havenot be wrench and tear away from i and leave i with a great need so as i could crave and hunger for a great and a well comfort he expect to see dinah again this evening and get leave to accompany she as far as oakbourne and then he would ask she to fix some time when he might go to snowfield and learn whether the last good hope that have be bear to he must be resign like the rest
the work he have to do at home besides put on his good clothe make it seven before he be on his way again to the hall farm and it be questionable whether with his long and quick stride he should be there in time even for the roast beef which come after the plum pudding for mrs
poyser s supper would be punctual
great be the clatter of knife and pewter plate and tin can when adam enter the house but there be no hum of voice to this accompaniment the eating of excellent roast beef provide free of expense be too serious a business to those good farm-labourer to be perform with a divided attention even if they have have anything to say to each other--which they have not
and mr
poyser at the head of the table be too busy with his carving to listen to bartle massey s or mr
craig s ready talk
here adam say mrs
poyser who be stand and look on to see that molly and nancy do their duty as waiter herebe a place keep for you between mr
massey and the boy
itbe a poor tale you couldnot come to see the pudding when it be whole adam look anxiously round for a fourth woman s figure but dinah be not there
he be almost afraid of ask about she besides his attention be claim by greeting and there remain the hope that dinah be in the house though perhaps disinclined to festivity on the eve of her departure
it be a goodly sight--that table with martin poyser s round good-humour face and large person at the head of it help his servant to the fragrant roast beef and please when the empty plate come again
martin though usually bl with a good appetite really forget to finish his own beef to-night--it be so pleasant to he to look on in the interval of carve and see how the other enjoy their supper for be they not man who on all the day of the year except christmas day and sundays eat their cold dinner in a makeshift manner under the hedgerow and drink their beer out of wooden bottle--with relish certainly but with their mouth towards the zenith after a fashion more endurable to duck than to human bipeds
martin poyser have some faint conception of the flavour such man must find in hot roast beef and fresh-draw ale
he hold his head on one side and screw up his mouth as he nudge bartle massey and watch half-witted tom tholer otherwise know as tom saft receive his second plateful of beef
a grin of delight break over tom s face as the plate be set down before he between his knife and fork which he hold erect as if they have be sacred taper
but the delight be too strong to continue smoulder in a grin--it burst out the next instant in a long-draw haw haw follow by a sudden collapse into utter gravity as the knife and fork dart down on the prey
martin poyser s large person shake with his silent unctuous laugh
he turn towards mrs
poyser to see if she too have be observant of tom and the eye of husband and wife meet in a glance of good-natured amusement
tom saft be a great favourite on the farm where he play the part of the old jester and make up for his practical deficiency by his success in repartee
his hit i imagine be those of the flail which fall quite at random but nevertheless smash an insect now and then
they be much quote at sheep-shearing and haymake time but i refrain from record they here lest tom s wit should prove to be like that of many other bygone jester eminent in their day--rather of a temporary nature not deal with the deep and more lasting relation of thing
tom except martin poyser have some pride in his servant and labourer think with satisfaction that they be the good worth their pay of any set on the estate
there be kester bale for example beale probably if the truth be know but he be call bale and be not conscious of any claim to a fifth letter the old man with the close leather cap and the network of wrinkle on his sun-brown face
be there any man in loamshire who know well the natur of all farming work
he be one of those invaluable labourer who can not only turn their hand to everything but excel in everything they turn their hand to
it be true kester s knee be much bent outward by this time and he walk with a perpetual curtsy as if he be among the most reverent of man
and so he be but i be oblige to admit that the object of his reverence be his own skill towards which he perform some rather affect act of worship
he always thatch the rick--for if anything be his forte more than another it be thatch--and when the last touch have be put to the last beehive rick kester whose home lie at some distance from the farm would take a walk to the rick-yard in his good clothe on a sunday morning and stand in the lane at a due distance to contemplate his own thatching walk about to get each rick from the proper point of view
as he curtsy along with his eye upturned to the straw knob imitative of golden globe at the summit of the beehive rick which indeed be gold of the good sort you might have imagine he to be engage in some pagan act of adoration
kester be an old bachelor and repute to have stocking full of coin concern which his master crack a joke with he every pay-night not a new unseasoned joke but a good old one that have be try many time before and have wear well
th young measter s a merry mon kester frequently remark for have begin his career by frighten away the crow under the last martin poyser but one he could never cease to account the reign martin a young master
i be not ashamed of commemorate old kester
you and i be indebted to the hard hand of such man--hand that have long ago mingle with the soil they till so faithfully thriftily make the good they could of the earth s fruit and receive the small share as their own wage
then at the end of the table opposite his master there be alick the shepherd and head-man with the ruddy face and broad shoulder not on the good term with old kester indeed their intercourse be confine to an occasional snarl for though they probably differ little concern hedging and ditching and the treatment of ewe there be a profound difference of opinion between they as to their own respective merit
when tityrus and meliboeus happen to be on the same farm they be not sentimentally polite to each other
alick indeed be not by any mean a honeyed man
his speech have usually something of a snarl in it and his broad-shoulder aspect something of the bull-dog expression-- don t you meddle with i and i willnot meddle with you but he be honest even to the splitting of an oat-grain rather than he would take beyond his acknowledge share and as close-fisted with his master s property as if it have be his own--throw very small handfuls of damage barley to the chicken because a large handful affect his imagination painfully with a sense of profusion
good-temper tim the waggoner who love his horse have his grudge against alick in the matter of corn
they rarely speak to each other and never look at each other even over their dish of cold potato but then as this be their usual mode of behaviour towards all mankind it would be an unsafe conclusion that they have more than transient fit of unfriendliness
the bucolic character at hayslope you perceive be not of that entirely genial merry broad-grinning sort apparently observe in most district visit by artist
the mild radiance of a smile be a rare sight on a field-labourer s face and there be seldom any gradation between bovine gravity and a laugh
nor be every labourer so honest as our friend alick
at this very table among mr
poyser s man there be that big ben tholoway a very powerful thresher but detect more than once in carry away his master s corn in his pocket--an action which as ben be not a philosopher could hardly be ascribe to absence of mind
however his master have forgive he and continue to employ he for the tholoways have live on the common time out of mind and have always work for the poysers
and on the whole i daresay society be not much the bad because ben have not six month of it at the treadmill for his view of depredation be narrow and the house of correction might have enlarge they
as it be ben eat his roast beef to-night with a serene sense of have steal nothing more than a few pea and bean as seed for his garden since the last harvest supper and feel warrant in think that alick s suspicious eye for ever upon he be an injury to his innocence
but now the roast beef be finish and the cloth be draw leave a fair large deal table for the bright drinking-can and the foaming brown jug and the bright brass candlestick pleasant to behold
now the great ceremony of the evening be to begin--the harvest-song in which every man must join
he might be in tune if he like to be singular but he must not sit with closed lip
the movement be oblige to be in triple time the rest be ad libitum
as to the origin of this song--whether it come in its actual state from the brain of a single rhapsodist or be gradually perfect by a school or succession of rhapsodist i be ignorant
there be a stamp of unity of individual genius upon it which incline i to the former hypothesis though i be not blind to the consideration that this unity may rather have arise from that consensus of many mind which be a condition of primitive thought foreign to our modern consciousness
some will perhaps think that they detect in the first quatrain an indication of a lost line which later rhapsodist fail in imaginative vigour have supply by the feeble device of iteration
other however may rather maintain that this very iteration be an original felicity to which none but the most prosaic mind can be insensible
the ceremony connect with the song be a drinking ceremony
that be perhaps a painful fact but then you know we cannot reform our forefather during the first and second quatrain sing decidedly forte no can be fill
herebe a health unto our master the founder of the feast herebe a health unto our master and to our mistress
and may his doing prosper whate er he take in hand for we be all his servant and be at his command
but now immediately before the third quatrain or chorus sung fortissimo with emphatic rap of the table which give the effect of cymbal and drum together alick s can be fill and he be bind to empty it before the chorus cease
then drink boy drink
and see ye do not spill for if ye do ye shall drink two for tis our master s will
when alick have go successfully through this test of steady-handed manliness it be the turn of old kester at his right hand--and so on till every man have drink his initiatory pint under the stimulus of the chorus
tom saft--the rogue--take care to spill a little by accident but mrs
poyser too officiously tom think interfere to prevent the exaction of the penalty
to any listener outside the door it would have be the reverse of obvious why the drink boy drink should have such an immediate and often-repeat encore but once enter he would have see that all face be at present sober and most of they serious--it be the regular and respectable thing for those excellent farm-labourer to do as much as for elegant lady and gentleman to smirk and bow over their wine-glass
bartle massey whose ear be rather sensitive have go out to see what sort of evening it be at an early stage in the ceremony and have not finish his contemplation until a silence of five minute declare that drink boy drink be not likely to begin again for the next twelvemonth
much to the regret of the boy and totty on they the stillness fall rather flat after that glorious thumping of the table towards which totty seat on her father s knee contribute with her small might and small fist
when bartle re-enter however there appear to be a general desire for solo music after the choral
nancy declare that tim the waggoner know a song and be allay singing like a lark i the stable whereupon mr
poyser say encouragingly come tim lad letus hear it tim look sheepish tuck down his head and say he couldnot sing but this encourage invitation of the master s be echo all round the table
it be a conversational opportunity everybody could say come tim except alick who never relax into the frivolity of unnecessary speech
at last tim s next neighbour ben tholoway begin to give emphasis to his speech by nudge at which tim grow rather savage say let i alooan will ye
else iwill ma ye sing a toon ye wonna like a good-temper waggoner s patience have limit and tim be not to be urge far
well then david ye re the lad to sing say ben willing to show that he be not discomfit by this check
sing my loovebe a roo wi out a thorn the amatory david be a young man of an unconscious abstract expression which be due probably to a squint of superior intensity rather than to any mental characteristic for he be not indifferent to ben s invitation but blush and laugh and rub his sleeve over his mouth in a way that be regard as a symptom of yield
and for some time the company appear to be much in earnest about the desire to hear david s song
but in vain
the lyricism of the evening be in the cellar at present and be not to be draw from that retreat just yet
meanwhile the conversation at the head of the table have take a political turn
mr
craig be not above talk politic occasionally though he pique himself rather on a wise insight than on specific information
he see so far beyond the mere fact of a case that really it be superfluous to know they

say i be i a gardener as know his business or arn t i mill
answer i that to be sure y be craig say he--hebe not a bad fellow mills benot for a butler but weak i the head

craig pause a moment with an emphatic stare after this triumphant speciman of socratic argument and then add thump the table rather fiercely why itbe a sure thing--and therebe they ull bear witness to t--as i one regiment where there be one man a-miss they put the regimental on a big monkey and they fit he as the shell fit the walnut and you couldnot tell the monkey from the mounseer ah

tim think slightly of david s vocalization be impel to supersede that feeble buzz by a spirited commencement of three merry mower but david be not to be put down so easily and show himself capable of a copious crescendo which be render it doubtful whether the rose would not predominate over the mower when old kester with an entirely unmoved and immovable aspect suddenly set up a quavering treble--as if he have be an alarum and the time be come for he to go off
the company at alick s end of the table take this form of vocal entertainment very much as a matter of course be free from musical prejudice but bartle massey lay down his pipe and put his finger in his ear and adam who have be long to go ever since he have hear dinah be not in the house rise and say he must bid good-night

she be fearful lest the strength of her feeling towards he should hinder she from wait and listen faithfully for the ultimate guide voice from within
i wish ihave ask she to write to i though he think
and yet even that might disturb she a bit perhaps
she want to be quite quiet in her old way for a while
and ihave no right to be impatient and interrupt she with my wish
shebe tell i what her mind be and shebe not a woman to say one thing and mean another
iwill wait patiently that be adam s wise resolution and it throve excellently for the first two or three week on the nourishment it get from the remembrance of dinah s confession that sunday afternoon
there be a wonderful amount of sustenance in the first few word of love
but towards the middle of october the resolution begin to dwindle perceptibly and show dangerous symptom of exhaustion
the week be unusually long dinah must surely have have more than enough time to make up her mind
let a woman say what she will after she have once tell a man that she love he he be a little too flushed and exalt with that first draught she offer he to care much about the taste of the second
he tread the earth with a very elastic step as he walk away from she and make light of all difficulty
but that sort of glow die out memory get sadly dilute with time and be not strong enough to revive we
adam be no long so confident as he have be
he begin to fear that perhaps dinah s old life would have too strong a grasp upon she for any new feeling to triumph
if she have not feel this she would surely have write to he to give he some comfort but it appear that she hold it right to discourage he
as adam s confidence wan his patience wan with it and he think he must write himself
he must ask dinah not to leave he in painful doubt long than be needful
he sit up late one night to write she a letter but the next morning he burn it afraid of its effect
it would be bad to have a discouraging answer by letter than from her own lip for her presence reconcile he to she will
you perceive how it be adam be hunger for the sight of dinah and when that sort of hunger reach a certain stage a lover be likely to still it though he may have to put his future in pawn
but what harm could he do by go to snowfield
dinah could not be displeased with he for it
she have not forbid he to go
she must surely expect that he would go before long
by the second sunday in october this view of the case have become so clear to adam that he be already on his way to snowfield on horseback this time for his hour be precious now and he have borrow jonathan burge s good nag for the journey
what keen memory go along the road with he
he have often be to oakbourne and back since that first journey to snowfield but beyond oakbourne the greystone wall the broken country the meagre tree seem to be tell he afresh the story of that painful past which he know so well by heart
but no story be the same to we after a lapse of time--or rather we who read it be no long the same interpreter--and adam this morning bring with he new thought through that grey country thought which give an altered significance to its story of the past
that be a base and selfish even a blasphemous spirit which rejoice and be thankful over the past evil that have blight or crush another because it have be make a source of unforeseen good to ourselves
adam could never cease to mourn over that mystery of human sorrow which have be bring so close to he he could never thank god for another s misery
and if i be capable of that narrow-sighted joy in adam s behalf i should still know he be not the man to feel it for himself
he would have shake his head at such a sentiment and say evil s evil and sorrow s sorrow and you cannot alter itbe natur by wrap it up in other word
other folk be not create for my sake that i should think all square when thing turn out well for i but it be not ignoble to feel that the full life which a sad experience have bring we be worth our own personal share of pain
surely it be not possible to feel otherwise any more than it would be possible for a man with cataract to regret the painful process by which his dim blur sight of man as tree walk have be exchange for clear outline and effulgent day
the growth of high feeling within we be like the growth of faculty bring with it a sense of add strength
we can no more wish to return to a narrow sympathy than a painter or a musician can wish to return to his cruder manner or a philosopher to his less complete formula
something like this sense of enlarged be be in adam s mind this sunday morning as he ride along in vivid recollection of the past
his feeling towards dinah the hope of pass his life with she have be the distant unseen point towards which that hard journey from snowfield eighteen month ago have be lead he
tender and deep as his love for hetty have be--so deep that the root of it would never be tear away--his love for dinah be well and more precious to he for it be the outgrowth of that full life which have come to he from his acquaintance with deep sorrow
itbe like as if it be a new strength to i he say to himself to love she and know as she love i
i shall look t she to help i to see thing right
for shebe well than i be--therebe less o self in she and pride
and itbe a feeling as give you a sort o liberty as if you could walk more fearless when youhave more trust in another than y have in yourself
ihave always be think i know well than they as belong to i and thatbe a poor sort o life when you cannot look to they near to you t help you with a bit well thought than what youhave get inside you a ready it be more than two o clock in the afternoon when adam come in sight of the grey town on the hill-side and look searchingly towards the green valley below for the first glimpse of the old thatched roof near the ugly red mill
the scene look less harsh in the soft october sunshine than it have in the eager time of early spring and the one grand charm it possess in common with all wide-stretch woodless region--that it fill you with a new consciousness of the overarching sky--have a milder more soothing influence than usual on this almost cloudless day
adam s doubt and fear melt under this influence as the delicate weblike cloud have gradually melt away into the clear blue above he
he seem to see dinah s gentle face assure he with its look alone of all he long to know
he do not expect dinah to be at home at this hour but he get down from his horse and tie it at the little gate that he might ask where she be go to-day
he have set his mind on follow she and bring she home
she be go to sloman s end a hamlet about three mile off over the hill the old woman tell he--have set off directly after morning chapel to preach in a cottage there as her habit be
anybody at the town would tell he the way to sloman s end
so adam get on his horse again and ride to the town put up at the old inn and take a hasty dinner there in the company of the too chatty landlord from whose friendly question and reminiscence he be glad to escape as soon as possible and set out towards sloman s end
with all his haste it be nearly four o clock before he could set off and he think that as dinah have go so early she would perhaps already be near return
the little grey desolate-look hamlet unscreene by shelter tree lie in sight long before he reach it and as he come near he could hear the sound of voice sing a hymn
perhaps thatbe the last hymn before they come away adam think
iwill walk back a bit and turn again to meet she far off the village he walk back till he get nearly to the top of the hill again and seat himself on a loose stone against the low wall to watch till he should see the little black figure leave the hamlet and wind up the hill
he choose this spot almost at the top of the hill because it be away from all eye--no house no cattle not even a nibble sheep near--no presence but the still light and shadow and the great embrace sky
she be much long come than he expect
he wait an hour at least watch for she and thinking of she while the afternoon shadow lengthen and the light grow soft
at last he see the little black figure come from between the grey house and gradually approach the foot of the hill
slowly adam think but dinah be really walk at her usual pace with a light quiet step
now she be begin to wind along the path up the hill but adam would not move yet he would not meet she too soon he have set his heart on meet she in this assured loneliness
and now he begin to fear lest he should startle she too much
yet he think shebe not one to be overstartle shebe always so calm and quiet as if she be prepare for anything what be she think of as she wind up the hill
perhaps she have find complete repose without he and have cease to feel any need of his love
on the verge of a decision we all tremble hope pause with flutter wing
but now at last she be very near and adam rise from the stone wall
it happen that just as he walk forward dinah have pause and turn round to look back at the village--who do not pause and look back in mount a hill
adam be glad for with the fine instinct of a lover he feel that it would be good for she to hear his voice before she see he
he come within three pace of she and then say dinah she start without look round as if she connect the sound with no place
dinah adam say again
he know quite well what be in her mind
she be so accustomed to think of impression as purely spiritual monition that she look for no material visible accompaniment of the voice
but this second time she look round
what a look of yearn love it be that the mild grey eye turn on the strong dark-eyed man
she do not start again at the sight of he she say nothing but move towards he so that his arm could clasp her round
and they walk on so in silence while the warm tear fall
adam be content and say nothing
it be dinah who speak first
adam she say it be the divine will
my soul be so knit to yours that it be but a divide life i live without you
and this moment now you be with i and i feel that our heart be fill with the same love
i have a fulness of strength to bear and do our heavenly father s will that i have lose before adam pause and look into her sincere eye
then wewill never part any more dinah till death part we and they kiss each other with a deep joy
what great thing be there for two human soul than to feel that they be join for life--to strengthen each other in all labour to rest on each other in all sorrow to minister to each other in all pain to be one with each other in silent unspeakable memory at the moment of the last parting
chapter lv marriage bells in little more than a month after that meeting on the hill--on a rimy morning in depart november--adam and dinah be marry

so the lily face look out with sweet gravity from under a grey quaker bonnet neither smile nor blushing but with lip tremble a little under the weight of solemn feeling

irwine to his mother as they drive off
i shall write to he the first thing when we get home epilogue it be near the end of june in 1807
the workshop have be shut up half an hour or more in adam bede s timber-yard which use to be jonathan burge s and the mellow evening light be fall on the pleasant house with the buff wall and the soft grey thatch very much as it do when we see adam bring in the key on that june evening nine year ago
there be a figure we know well just come out of the house and shade her eye with her hand as she look for something in the distance for the ray that fall on her white borderless cap and her pale auburn hair be very dazzling
but now she turn away from the sunlight and look towards the door
we can see the sweet pale face quite well now it be scarcely at all alter--only a little full to correspond to her more matronly figure which still seem light and active enough in the plain black dress
i see he seth dinah say as she look into the house
let we go and meet he
come lisbeth come with mother the last call be answer immediately by a small fair creature with pale auburn hair and grey eye little more than four year old who run out silently and put her hand into her mother s
come uncle seth say dinah
aye aye webe come seth answer from within and presently appear stoop under the doorway be tall than usual by the black head of a sturdy two-year-old nephew who have cause some delay by demand to be carry on uncle s shoulder
well take he on thy arm seth say dinah look fondly at the stout black-eyed fellow
hebe troublesome to thee so nay nay addy like a ride on my shoulder
i can carry he so for a bit a kindness which young addy acknowledge by drum his heel with promising force against uncle seth s chest
but to walk by dinah s side and be tyrannize over by dinah s and adam s child be uncle seth s earthly happiness
where didst see he ask seth as they walk on into the adjoining field
i cannot catch sight of he anywhere between the hedge by the roadside say dinah
i see his hat and his shoulder
there he be again trust thee for catch sight of he if hebe anywhere to be see say seth smile
thee t like poor mother use to be
she be always on the look out for adam and could see he soon than other folk for all her eye get dim hebe be long than he expect say dinah take arthur s watch from a small side pocket and look at it itbe nigh upon seven now aye theywould have a deal to say to one another say seth and the meeting ud touch them both pretty closish
why itbe get on towards eight year since they part yes say dinah adam be greatly move this morning at the thought of the change he should see in the poor young man from the sickness he have undergo as well as the year which have change we all
and the death of the poor wanderer when she be come back to we have be sorrow upon sorrow see addy say seth lower the young one to his arm now and pointing therebe father come--at the far stile dinah hasten her step and little lisbeth run on at her utmost speed till she clasp her father s leg
adam pat her head and lift she up to kiss she but dinah could see the mark of agitation on his face as she approach he and he put her arm within his in silence
well youngster must i take you he say try to smile when addy stretch out his arm--ready with the usual baseness of infancy to give up his uncle seth at once now there be some rare patronage at hand
itbe cut i a good deal dinah adam say at last when they be walk on
didst find he greatly alter say dinah
why hebe alter and yet not alter
i should ha know he anywhere
but his colour s change and he look sadly
however the doctor say hewill soon be set right in his own country air
hebe all sound in th inside itbe only the fever shatter he so
but he speak just the same and smile at i just as he do when he be a lad
itbe wonderful how hebe always have just the same sort o look when he smile ihave never see he smile poor young man say dinah
but thee wilt see he smile to-morrow say adam
he ask after thee the first thing when he begin to come round and we could talk to one another
i hope she benot alter he say i remember her face so well i tell he no adam continue look fondly at the eye that be turn towards his only a bit plumper as thee dst a right to be after seven year
i may come and see she to-morrow maynot i he say i long to tell she how ihave think of she all these year didst tell he iwould always use the watch say dinah
aye and we talk a deal about thee for he say he never see a woman a bit like thee
i shall turn methodist some day he say when she preach out of door and go to hear she and i say nay sir you cannot do that for conference have forbid the woman preach and shebe give it up all but talk to the people a bit in their house ah say seth who could not repress a comment on this point and a sore pity it be o conference and if dinah have see as i do wewould ha leave the wesleyans and join a body that ud put no bond on christian liberty nay lad nay say adam she be right and thee wast wrong
therebe no rule so wise but what itbe a pity for somebody or other
most o the woman do more harm nor good with their preaching--they ve not get dinah s gift nor her sperrit--and shebe see that and she think it right to set th example o submitting for shebe not hold from other sort o teaching
and i agree with she and approve o what she do seth be silent
this be a stand subject of difference rarely allude to and dinah wish to quit it at once say didst remember adam to speak to colonel donnithorne the word my uncle and aunt entrust to thee yes and hebe go to the hall farm with mr
irwine the day after to-morrow
mr
irwine come in while we be talk about it and he would have it as the colonel must see nobody but thee to-morrow
he say--and hebe in the right of it--as itwill be bad for he t have his feeling stir with see many people one after another
we must get you strong and hearty he say thatbe the first thing to be do arthur and then you shall have your own way
but i shall keep you under your old tutor s thumb till then mr
irwine s fine and joyful at have he home again adam be silent a little while and then say it be very cutting when we first see one another
hehave never hear about poor hetty till mr
irwine meet he in london for the letter miss he on his journey
the first thing he say to i when wewould get hold o one another s hand be i could never do anything for she adam--she live long enough for all the suffering--and iwould think so of the time when i might do something for she
but you tell i the truth when you say to i once therebe a sort of wrong that can never be make up for why therebe mr
and mrs
poyser come in at the yard gate say seth
so there be say dinah
run lisbeth run to meet aunt poyser
come in adam and rest it have be a hard day for thee
